title: EMT Basic Exam Database
description: EMT NREMT exam questions with metadata

questions.text: The burn area confined to the patient's left anterior torso is red and wet. It is painful and has intact sensation. Blisters are beginning to form. These findings are consistent with which burn classification?
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The burn area confined to the patient's left forearm is white and leathery in appearance. There is no painful sensation from this area; however, the area that surrounds it is very painful. These findings are consistent with which burn classification?
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: On further examination, you determine that this patient has burns to her entire left arm, the left half of her anterior torso, and one half of her head and neck. Using the rule of nines, you calculate the TBSA burned to be approximately:
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The S in the "OPQRST" acronym stands for:
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: The rule of palms is recommended when:
options: The size of the patient's palm is at least 2 inches in width
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following should be considered first when responding to a chemical burn injury?
options: Prompt establishment of a patent airway
Immediate removal of the patient from the hazard area
The type of personal protective clothing necessary to enter the scene
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is correct treatment for chemical burns?
options: Irrigate the patient before removing his or her clothes or shoes
Irrigate with copious amounts of water before transport
Irrigate dry chemicals on the skin - do not brush them off
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What does the letter O represent in the muemanic memory device "OPQRST".
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: Normal respiratory rate in adult is:
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Capillary refill is considered most reliable in children less than ___ years of age.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: The first step in the focused history and physical exam performed on an unconscious medical patient is:
options: Measure baseline assessment of vital signs
Rapidly assess the patient from head to toe
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: The L in the "DCAP-BTLS" a cranium stands for:
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: During the assessment of the patient's pelvis, you should :-
options: Gently push the patient's pelvic wings posteriorly and then to the patient's midline
Gently push the patient's pelvic wings to the patient's midline and then posteriorly.
Gently push the patient's pelvic wings superiorly and then to the patient's midline.
There's no need to palpate the pelvic,just ask the patient if there's any pain and complete your assessment
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: During the assessment of the patient's mental status, the patient responded when you make gentle touch to his shoulders and spoke with him, so the patient is:-
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: If you make an error and discovered it after you completed writing the report, you should :-
options: Write the correct information in another form
Drawing a single horizontal line throw the error.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: A 63-year-old man is complaining of substernal chest pain radiating to his left arm and jaw. He rates his pain an 8 on a 1-10 scale. Baseline vital signs are: BP 136/70, P 126, R 16. The patient takes no medications regularly and has no allergies to medications. You should now:
options: Attach the external defibrillator pads to the patient's chest.
Contact medical control and request permission to administer nitroglycerin.
Ask your partner to assist the patient's respirations with a bag-valve-device.
Place the patient in a position of comfort and administer high-concentration oxygen by nonrebreather mask.
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: Which of the following conditions is characterized by increased reactivity of the lower airway to irritants with reversible narrowing of the bronchioles and increased mucus production?
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: The P in the "OPQRST" a croinym stands for:
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: If need to move patient complain of inadequate breathing, you choose :-
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Involves multiple breaks creating bone fragments .is the defining of :-
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Sprains is defined as:
options: The bone ends do not meet appropriately at a joint.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: (the patient lying flat on the back, on an incline, and with feet elevated 12"above the head) describe which position:
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Is the measurement of the force the blood exerts against the walls of blood vessels during the hearts contraction and relaxation:
questions.subject: Shock and Resuscitation

questions.text: maintaining the patient's dignity, right to privacy, and respecting their wishes is an example of which role of the EMT-Basic:
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The knees are ___ to the toes
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: What does the letter V represent in the muemanic memory device "AVPU":
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following is a commonly used acronym for evaluation of symptoms the patient is experiencing?
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: A rapid trauma assessment is performed to:
options: Identify the presence of any fractures.
Determine the presence of all injuries.
Determine the presence of life-threatening injuries
Identify the patient's name and past medical history.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: ___ is the presentation of the baby's head first in delivery :-
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: You are called to a private home. A 6-year-old girl has fallen, suffering an injury to her right lower leg. The girl's parents are on the scene and give their consent to treat, however, the child is refusing care. Which of the following statements regarding this situation is correct?
options: You may begin treatment of the child only if the parents and the child give consent.
You have a legal obligation to treat the child even if the child and parents do not wish treatment.
You have a legal right and duty to treat the child, even if the child does not wish to be treated.
You have no legal right and obligation to treat the child. Ask the child to sign a release form.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 31-year-old man has sustained a head injury. Assessment reveals the patient has an open wound on the side of his head that is bleeding. The patient's speech is slurred and he smells of alcohol. The patient is refusing treatment. Your best course of action will be to:
options: Contact the patient's significant other for permission to treat.
Inform dispatch that the patient has refused care and you are available for another call.
Realize the patient is of legal age and has the right to determine what will be done to his own body.
Realize the patient cannot make a rational decision at this time and treat the patient under the principle of implied consent
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The pupils should be evaluated for :
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The EMT-Basic obtains information regarding whether a scene is safe from :
options: Everything seen and heard at the scene.
Everything that is not seen or heard at the scene.
Both A and B are correct.
Neither A nor B is correct.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The extremities should be evaluated for DCAP-BTLS and :
options: Distal pulses, Motor function, and Sensation
Motor function, Instability, and Venous distention.
Venous distention, Distal pulses, and Paradoxical motion.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The "S" in DCAP-BTLS stands for:
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Comparing present patient information to previously recorded information is known as:
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following are trade names for albuterol ?
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: 63-year-old man is complaining of substernal chest pain radiating to his left arm and jaw. He rates his pain an 8 on a 1-10 scale. Baseline vital signs are: BP 136/70, P 126, R 16. The patient takes no medications regularly and has no allergies to medications. You should now :
options: Attach the external defibrillator pads to the patient's chest.
Contact medical control and request permission to administer nitroglycerin
Ask your partner to assist the patient's respirations with a bag-valve-device.
Place the patient in a position of comfort and administer high-concentration oxygen by nonrebreather mask.
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: The single most important factor in determining survival from cardiac arrest is:
questions.subject: Shock and Resuscitation

questions.text: All of the following are from the national highway traffic safety administrations standards EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Patient Safety

questions.text: ___ occurs when someone actually touches another person without consent.
questions.subject: Professionalism and Ethics

questions.text: Your patient have mental status changes and poor vital signs outside average limits you should reassess patient vital signs every :
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: (The measurement of the force the blood exerts against the walls of blood vessels during the hearts contraction and relaxation) called:
questions.subject: Shock and Resuscitation

questions.text: All of these are from the signs of adequate ventilation EXCEPT :
options: Chest rises and falls with ventilation
The heart rate return to normal
The breathing rate is too fast or too slow
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: The preferred method of ventilation a nonbreathing patient is :
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: If the patient is sitting up and you see that jugular veins are distended this indicate that patient have :
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Portable radio is a hand held transceiver that typically has a range of approximately:
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: A physician prescribed medications that patient already have and you may assist patients to administration with approval from medical direction are :
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The parts to check in the Cincinnati prehospital stroke scale are :
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: Epinephrine auto injector for infant or child contain the dose:
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Hyperventilation lowers the levels of.
questions.hint: Think about what gas is primarily exhaled during rapid breathing.
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation blows off excessive carbon dioxide, leading to hypocapnia. This can cause dizziness and respiratory alkalosis.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: You are evaluating a patient who was in a motor vehicle collision. You determine thet tha patient is responsive to pain only and has a partially amputated arm at the elbow, a possible pelvic fracture, and a flial chest. You classify this patient's condition is:
questions.hint: Multiple major injuries with altered mental status suggest immediate threat to life.
questions.explanation: Multiple major injuries (amputation, flail chest, pelvic fracture, decreased LOC) are immediately life-threatening, requiring rapid intervention and transport.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: After choosing the appropriate size LMA, what is the next step in preparing for insertion:
options: checks the integrity of the cuff
questions.hint: Always verify the device works before adding lubricant or inserting.
questions.explanation: Always inflate and test the cuff first to ensure no leaks before lubrication or insertion.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: The splinting device recommended for a painful and isolated fracture of the femer is:
questions.hint: The ideal splint for a femur fracture is not listed here.
questions.explanation: A traction splint is indicated for femoral shaft fractures. Since it is not an option, 'None of the above' is correct.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The splinting device recommended for a painful and isolated fracture of the tibia is:
questions.hint: Think about long bone fractures requiring stabilization along the axis.
questions.explanation: Although clinically controversial, exam logic often designates traction splints for long bone fractures like the tibia.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The splinting device recommended for a painful and isolated fracture of the humerus is:
questions.hint: Upper arm fractures are best immobilized close to the body.
questions.explanation: A sling and swathe immobilizes the upper arm effectively and reduces movement and pain.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following limb position is ideal for an immobilization of most extremity injuries?
questions.hint: Splint the limb as you find it unless circulation is compromised.
questions.explanation: Splint in the position found or natural alignment unless circulation is compromised.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: When assessing a trauma patient having peri orbital edema with basilar skull fracture. In this condition what sign could be called as:
questions.hint: Both 'Raccoon eyes' and periorbital ecchymosis describe the same finding.
questions.explanation: 'Raccoon eyes' = periorbital ecchymosis, classic for basilar skull fracture. Battle sign is mastoid ecchymosis.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Align serious angulated long-board fracture unless:
options: There is an absent distal pulse
Both sensation and pulses are intact
questions.hint: Stop if you feel significant resistance to movement.
questions.explanation: Stop if resistance or pain increases â€” risk of further damage to nerves and vessels.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: If after moving a limb to alignment you notice the distal is absent, you should:
options: Gently move the limb to restore the pulse
Return the limb to the original position
Elevate the limb and then splint it
questions.hint: Restoring perfusion is critical; revert to the previous position.
questions.explanation: If pulse is lost after alignment, immediately return to the original position to restore perfusion.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: An anterior hip dislocation normally presents with the:
questions.hint: Think about the direction of femoral head displacement.
questions.explanation: Anterior hip dislocation typically presents with external rotation of the limb and the knee turned outward.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: If a patient presents with an ankle deformed with the foot turned to the side, you would suspect which type of ankle dislocation.
questions.hint: Foot turned to the side often indicates posterior displacement.
questions.explanation: Posterior dislocations often present with deformity and abnormal foot positioning.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When patient's shoulder appears "squared-off" the patient complains of severe pain, and she cannot move her arm, you should suspect what type of shoulder dislocation.
questions.hint: Loss of the rounded deltoid contour is key.
questions.explanation: Squared-off shoulder is a classic sign of anterior shoulder dislocation due to humeral head displacement.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following injuries can be adequately splinted by using the short padded board splint, placing the hand in the position of function, and slinging and swathing the extremity?
questions.hint: Short board + sling works for distal forearm and wrist injuries.
questions.explanation: Short board splint with sling and swathe is appropriate for radius, ulna, and wrist fractures.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The "I" within acronym RICE used by athletic trainers stands for:
questions.explanation: RICE = Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation. Ice reduces swelling in the first 24â€“48 hours.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: What is the complete flow rate of nasal cannula?
questions.hint: Standard nasal cannula has a maximum flow rate.
questions.explanation: Typical range is 1â€“6 L/min, delivering ~24â€“44% oxygen. Complete/usual max is 6 L/min.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: In order to provide oxygen therapy at 100% what should the flow rate should be:
questions.hint: Non-rebreather needs high flow to prevent room air entrainment.
questions.explanation: A non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min delivers near 100% oxygen.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: What airway maneuver should be used on a man that has fallen off a ladder and is suffering of respiratory distress:
questions.hint: Assume cervical spine injury after a fall.
questions.explanation: Jaw thrust is used when cervical spine injury is suspected to avoid neck movement.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: You arrive at the scene of a patient who fell from a ladder. Which of the following information is not an important factor in care of the patient
options: what part of the body hits first
questions.hint: The actual fall distance matters, not the ladder's height.
questions.explanation: What matters is actual fall distance, not ladder height. Distance fallen, landing surface, and first contact point affect injury pattern.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: A laceration, spurting bright red blood most likely means what type of injury
questions.hint: Color and flow pattern identify the vessel type.
questions.explanation: Arterial bleeding is bright red, pulsatile/spurting, and life-threatening.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: When you are checking the eye with light, which part of eye will reacts:
questions.hint: The opening changes size in response to light.
questions.explanation: The pupil constricts in response to light (pupillary reflex).
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Oxygen saturation at sea level is:
questions.hint: Healthy individuals at sea level have near-perfect saturation.
questions.explanation: At sea level, normal SpOâ‚‚ is 96â€“100% in healthy individuals.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Carbon monoxide poisoning, O2 saturation at pulse oxymetry shows:
questions.hint: Pulse ox cannot distinguish CO from oxygen.
questions.explanation: Pulse oximeters cannot distinguish COHb from oxyhemoglobin, so readings appear normal or high â€“ dangerous false reassurance.
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: When assessing oral temperature. How many minutes you will place the thermometer inside the mouth.
questions.explanation: Standard oral thermometers require about 1â€“2 minutes for accurate reading.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: How often should you deliver ventilations for infant during respiratory arrest:
questions.explanation: Approximately 20 breaths/min, which matches pediatric resuscitation guidelines (1 breath every 3 seconds).
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: One factor which does not shown in ECG is:
questions.hint: ECG is a snapshot of electrical activity, not history.
questions.explanation: ECG shows rate, rhythm, and indirectly strength (amplitude), but not when the problem started.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: For assessing wheezing, which method of physical examination you will use:
questions.explanation: Wheezing is heard with a stethoscope (auscultation), not seen or felt.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: When you are assessing a patient, he have pulse, respiratory rate is <10 b/min. How will you ventilate the patient:
questions.hint: Slow breathing with a pulse requires assisted ventilation.
questions.explanation: Patient has inadequate breathing (bradypnea) â†’ assist ventilation with bag-valve-mask, not just oxygen.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: Nasal bleeding is known as:
questions.explanation: Epistaxis is the medical term for nasal bleeding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the compression-ventilation ration for 2 rescuer child CPR?
questions.hint: Child CPR ratio differs from adult.
questions.explanation: For children: 2 rescuers â†’ 15 compressions : 2 breaths.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: You are evaluating an ECG of a patient, PR interval is more than 0.20sec, QRS regular. What should you suspect?
questions.hint: Prolonged PR with regular rhythm is characteristic.
questions.explanation: Prolonged PR interval (>0.20 sec) with 1:1 conduction = 1st-degree AV block.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following assessment techniques should be performed first during the assessment
questions.explanation: Always start with inspection (visual examination) before palpation, auscultation, or percussion.
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: When you are using closed ended question, what type of question will you ask.
questions.hint: Closed-ended questions expect a short yes/no or factual answer.
questions.explanation: Closed-ended = yes/no or single factual answer. 'Does it radiate?' is yes/no.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: During the first stage of pulmonary edema, which sound you can here
questions.hint: Early pulmonary edema can mimic asthma.
questions.explanation: Early stage = bronchospasm-like wheezing due to interstitial fluid. Later stages produce crackles.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: When assessing BP of a patient, position of hands should:
questions.explanation: Proper BP measurement requires arm supported, slight elbow bend, relaxed muscles â€“ all of the above.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: What is the recommended depth of chest compression for an infant victim?
options: Atleast one fourth the depth of the chest, approximately 1 inches (2.5cm)
Atleast one third the depth of the chest, approximately 1.5 inches (4cm)
Atleast one half the depth of the chest, approximately 2 inches (5cm)
Atleast two third the depth of the chest, approximately 3 inches (8cm)
questions.hint: Infant depth is about 1.5 inches or 4 cm.
questions.explanation: Standard guideline: at least one third anterior-posterior chest depth (~1.5 inches / 4 cm) for infants.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Earning a general impression as a part of your initial assessment helps you:
options: Determine general clinical status and priority
questions.hint: General impression gives you an initial sense of the patient's level of consciousness.
questions.explanation: Initial impression quickly assesses appearance, work of breathing, circulation â€“ mental status is a key component of baseline status.
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: What is the compression-ventilation ratio for 2-rescuer infant CPR
questions.hint: Same ratio as child CPR for two rescuers.
questions.explanation: For infants and children, 2-rescuer CPR uses 15:2 compression-to-ventilation ratio.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: What is the preferred technique for providing chest compression during 2-rescuer CPR for an infant
questions.hint: Two rescuers allow better perfusion with encircling thumbs.
questions.explanation: The two-thumb encircling hands technique is preferred for two-rescuer infant CPR as it provides better perfusion and compression quality.
questions.subject: Shock and Resuscitation

questions.text: What is the best action to relive severe chocking in a responsive infant
options: Kneel behind the infant and perform abdominal thrusts
Give 2 breaths, reposioning the airway after each breath
Begin 5 cycles of back slaps, followed by 5 chest thrusts
questions.hint: Infant choking sequence is back blows then chest thrusts.
questions.explanation: For responsive infant with severe choking: 5 back blows followed by 5 chest thrusts. Abdominal thrusts are not used in infants.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the minimum amount of time for suctioning the airway
questions.hint: Limit suction duration to avoid hypoxia.
questions.explanation: Limit suction to â‰¤10 seconds (maximum time) to prevent hypoxia; minimum is not defined but 10 seconds is the safe maximum.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: Questions that allow the patient to explain a complaint in detail are known as questions.
questions.explanation: Open-ended questions encourage the patient to describe symptoms in detail, e.g., 'Tell me about your pain.'
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: What is the correct airway to use on a person with intact gag reflex
questions.explanation: OPA is contraindicated with intact gag reflex (may cause vomiting). NPA is tolerated.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: How often should you deliver ventilation during respiratory arrest
questions.hint: Adult ventilation rate is slower than infant.
questions.explanation: For adult respiratory arrest with a pulse: 1 breath every 5â€“6 seconds (10â€“12 breaths/min).
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: Identify the ecg
questions.explanation: Atrial flutter is characterized by sawtooth flutter waves, especially visible in leads II, III, aVF.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following statements about crush injury is true
options: Only closed injuries can classified as crush injury
A spongy sensation on palpation of the injured area indicates crush injury
Crush injuries are easily identifiable because they invariably result in deformity
The actual source of bleeding in crush injuries may be hard to identify
questions.hint: Bleeding may be internal and not obvious.
questions.explanation: The source of bleeding in crush injuries may be internal and difficult to identify, even without obvious external deformity.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A fall should be considered severe whenever patients have fallen
questions.explanation: Fall >3 times the patient's height is considered a severe mechanism in trauma triage guidelines.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: During scene-size up at a multiple vehicle crash, it is important to determining the number of patients because:
options: additional EMS resources may have to be called if there are more patients than the first crew on the scene can handle
run reports are required for each patient
ALS must be called if there are more than two patients
the media must be given accurate information
questions.hint: Resource allocation depends on patient count.
questions.explanation: Determining the number of patients helps decide if additional resources (ambulances, personnel) are needed for triage and transport.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following may be useful in determining the nature of illness/mechanism of injury
questions.hint: Different people may have different pieces of the puzzle.
questions.explanation: Patient, bystanders, and family all provide critical history clues about the nature of illness or mechanism of injury.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Injury is caused by a blow that does not penetrate the skin or other tissues, impact will travel through the body and may cause rupture of organs and vessels:
questions.hint: Think about injuries where the skin remains intact but internal damage occurs.
questions.explanation: Blunt-force trauma involves energy transfer through tissues without skin penetration, potentially causing internal organ damage.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: First part of the patient assessment. 1) check scene safety, take standard precautions, 2) note mechanism of injury or nature of patient illness, 3) determining the number of patients, 4) decide if any additional resources are needed 5) spine stabilization, this assessment is known as:
questions.hint: This step happens before you even touch the patient.
questions.explanation: Scene size-up covers safety, MOI/NOI, patient count, and resource needs before patient contact.
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: Caused by hand guns and short gun. Can cause damage any where in the body, damage from projectile and cavitation
questions.explanation: Medium velocity penetrating trauma: handguns. Low velocity = knives, high velocity = rifles.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: During insertion, the distal end of the esophareal-tracheal combitube ends in the esophagus about:
questions.hint: The combitube is designed to work regardless, but esophageal placement is far more common.
questions.explanation: The distal end enters the esophagus approximately 90% of the time, allowing ventilation through the proximal lumen.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: Steps for insertion of the esophageal-tracheal combitube include:
options: Checking the integrity of the balloons, lubricate the tube and blind insertion
Choosing the appropriate size tube and blind insertion
Choosing the appropriate size tube, checking the integrity of balloons, lubricate, and blind insertion
Choosing the appropriate size tube, lubricate, blind insertion
questions.hint: Don't skip the cuff check before lubrication.
questions.explanation: Correct order: 1) choose appropriate size, 2) check balloon integrity, 3) lubricate, 4) blind insertion.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: The technique of _____ involves carefull, noninvasive, informed observation is
questions.hint: This is simply using your eyes carefully.
questions.explanation: Observation (inspection) is careful, noninvasive, informed visual assessment.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which condition can cause an upper airway obstruction
questions.explanation: Epiglottitis causes upper airway obstruction. CHF, emphysema, asthma affect lower airways.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: After LMA is inserted what is your next step
questions.hint: You need a seal before ventilating.
questions.explanation: After insertion, immediately inflate the cuff to create a seal, then ventilate.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: After correct placement of the LMA, how should you secure the tube
options: Does not need to be secure
Holds the LMA tube with your hand
questions.explanation: Secure LMA with tape to prevent accidental displacement during transport.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: What is the LMA size generally used for adult females
questions.hint: Women typically need one size smaller than men.
questions.explanation: Typical LMA sizing: adult females = size 4, adult males = size 5.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: A sign and symptom that cause a patient or bystander to request to medical help is known as the:
questions.explanation: Chief complaint is the main symptom or reason the patient seeks medical help.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: When the EMT-B feels that a patient just doesn't look right, is called:
questions.explanation: Diagnostic insight is the intuitive sense that something is wrong, often guiding further assessment.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The Glasgow Coma Score evaluates which of the following: 1. Eye opening 2. Verbal response 3. Motor response 4. Loss of consciousness 5. Vital signs
questions.explanation: GCS evaluates eye opening (1), verbal response (2), and motor response (3).
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: If a patient is not alert and is breathing less than 8 breaths a minute, the EMT-B should:
options: Give high concentration oxygen via non-rebreather mask
Ventilate with a positive pressure device and 100% oxygen
Give high concentration oxygen with nasal cannula
questions.hint: Slow rate with altered mental status means inadequate breathing.
questions.explanation: RR <8 with altered LOC requires assisted ventilation with BVM and 100% oxygen.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The mental status of unresponsive infant is typically checked by flicking the feet and:
questions.explanation: For infants, check responsiveness by talking and flicking feet; avoid aggressive stimuli like sternal rub.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following is a potential complication of crush injury
options: Calcification in the vascular and nervous system
questions.explanation: Complications include renal failure (myoglobinuria), electrolyte disturbances, sudden death, and vascular/neural calcification.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A laceration that is perpendicular to the tension line of the body is more likely to:
questions.hint: Perpendicular cuts disrupt more tissue fibers.
questions.explanation: Lacerations perpendicular to tension lines gape more, bleed more, and scar worse.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following medications may help treat the patient with crush injury who has sustain significant blood loss. 1. Potassium chloride 2. Sodium bicarbonate 3. Furosemide 4. Mannitol 5. 5% dextrose in 0.4% sodium chloride solution
questions.hint: Avoid potassium â€“ crush injury causes hyperkalemia.
questions.explanation: Sodium bicarbonate (prevents acidosis), mannitol (diuresis), and D5NS (fluids) are used. Potassium chloride is dangerous due to existing hyperkalemia.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The bacteria most often associated with infection of open soft injury is:
questions.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is the most frequent cause of soft tissue wound infections.
questions.subject: Patient Safety

questions.text: Which of the following may result from am improperly applied tourniquet:
options: Neurovascular damage at the site of the tourniquet
Permanent tissue damage distal to the site of the tourniquet
questions.hint: Improper use can worsen the situation.
questions.explanation: Improper tourniquet application can cause increased bleeding, nerve/vessel damage, and distal ischemia.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The tough, fibrous sheaths that bundle skeletal muscle is called:
questions.explanation: Fascia is the tough fibrous sheath that surrounds and bundles skeletal muscle.
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: Tenderness would be discovering using which of the following assessment
questions.hint: Use your hands to feel for pain.
questions.explanation: Tenderness is elicited by palpation (touching the area).
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: After being awakened from her sleep because of coughing and shortness of breath, this 65-year old woman has called for an ambulance. You find the woman apprehensive and breathing with difficulty. She tells you she is being treated for hypertension and takes digoxin and a diuretic. Her pulse 122, BP 192/112, and respirations are 36/min. Her temp is normal. Diffuse rhonchi wheezes and rales are heard on auscultation of the chest. You note jugular venous distension when the bed is at a 45 degree angle. The most likely cause for this clinical picture is:
questions.hint: JVD + crackles + hypertension + orthopnea point to heart failure.
questions.explanation: CHF presents with JVD, pulmonary crackles, hypertension, tachycardia, and difficulty breathing when supine (orthopnea).
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: Which part of the hands and fingers is best suited to evaluate vibration
options: Back of the hands or fingers
questions.hint: Most sensitive area for fine tactile discrimination.
questions.explanation: Fingertips are most sensitive for detecting vibration and fine texture.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: One likely cause of the bradycardia is:
questions.explanation: Parasympathetic (vagal) stimulation slows heart rate. Fever, pain, and blood loss typically cause tachycardia.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following of not an aspect of pulse evaluation
questions.hint: Volume is related but not the standard term.
questions.explanation: Standard pulse assessment includes rate, rhythm, and quality (strength). Volume is often grouped under quality, but not a separate aspect.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Normal exhalation is:
options: An active process in involving the accessory muscles
Active in its early stage and passive in the late stage
An active process involving diaphragm and intercostals muscles
questions.hint: No muscle contraction needed to exhale normally.
questions.explanation: Normal exhalation is passive, driven by elastic recoil of lungs and chest wall.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: Which of the following are likely to influence a patient's blood pressure
questions.hint: Many factors can alter BP reading.
questions.explanation: Anxiety, body position, smoking, pain, and medications all affect blood pressure.
questions.subject: Shock and Resuscitation

questions.text: A light, popping, non-musical sound heard upon auscultation of the lungs is best described as:
questions.hint: Sounds like Velcro being pulled apart.
questions.explanation: Crackles (rales) are light, popping, non-musical sounds from fluid or opening of collapsed airways.
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: What should you observe when trying to observe if rescue breath for an infant victim are effective
options: Visible rise of the stomach with each rescue breaths
Visible rise of the chest with each rescue breaths
Complete compression of the ventilation bag
Air leaking around the ventilation bag
questions.hint: Look for the same sign as in adults.
questions.explanation: Effective rescue breaths cause visible chest rise. Gastric distention indicates too much volume.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Why do abbreviations and acronyms pose potential interpretation problems and possible confusion.
options: Nurses are not allowed to use them
questions.hint: One abbreviation can stand for several things.
questions.explanation: Abbreviations can have multiple meanings (e.g., 'MS' = morphine sulfate or multiple sclerosis), leading to errors.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: You are attending a 54-year old male who is complaining of dyspnea. He is two pack her day smoker who acknowledges he has a smoker's cough. Over the last few days of the cough has become more bothersome and the sputum is now yellow-green and streaked with blood. He appears flush, somewhat cyanotic and in obvious respiratory distress. His vital signs are: pulse- 112, BP-150/90, and respiration-38, temperature-102. On auscultation of the chest rhonchi and rales are heard on the right side. You should suspect he has:
questions.hint: Fever + productive purulent sputum + localized sounds = infection.
questions.explanation: Bacterial pneumonia presents with fever, purulent sputum, increased WBC, and focal rales/rhonchi.
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: A 26 yr old male is found unconscious behind the local mini market. His arms are covered with needle tracks and his pupils are fully constricted. His respiratory rate is 6 per/min. Which body system is malfunctioning?
questions.hint: Opioids depress the brain's respiratory center.
questions.explanation: Opioid overdose causes CNS depression â†’ pinpoint pupils, unconsciousness, respiratory drive failure.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: An open wound characterized by smooth edges that is frequently caused by knives, razors or glass is called a(n):
questions.explanation: An incision is a clean, smooth-edged wound from a sharp object like a knife, razor, or glass.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Scenario: You are treating a patient who fell of a second-story roof. When assessing the chest during the rapid trauma assessment, you discover absent lung sounds on the right side. You should next:
options: Move to the abdomen and continue the rapid trauma assessment
Search for other signs of tension pneumothorax
questions.hint: Don't decompress without confirming tension physiology.
questions.explanation: First, search for other signs of tension pneumothorax (hypotension, JVD, tracheal deviation) before decompressing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following should paramedics use with every patient:
questions.explanation: Gloves are the minimum PPE for every patient contact. Mask and eyewear are for splash risk.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: If a victim of foreign-body airway obstruction becomes unresponsive. After you send someone to activate emergency response system, what is the next recommended action
questions.explanation: Once unresponsive, begin CPR starting with chest compressions. Blind finger sweeps are not recommended.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: Pale skin color can be a result of all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Decreased blood flow to the skin.
questions.explanation: Carotenemia (excess beta-carotene) causes yellow-orange skin, not pallor. Pallor is from vasoconstriction, anemia, or shock.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: When you are assessing cardiac patient PVC. Which condition you will intervene immediately
questions.hint: Treat the patient, not the monitor.
questions.explanation: Intervene for PVCs if they are symptomatic (chest pain, hypotension) or frequent/paired. Asymptomatic PVCs may not need immediate treatment.
questions.subject: Shock and Resuscitation

questions.text: _____ respiration is characterized by rappid shallow breathing followed by apnea
questions.explanation: Cheyne-Stokes respiration: cyclic crescendo-decrescendo breathing alternating with apnea.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: 1st stage of wound healing is:
questions.explanation: Hemostasis (clotting) is the first stage of wound healing, occurring within seconds to minutes.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: While assessing a patient with complaints of pain. He is explaining it by sign, sight. What is your underlying finding
questions.explanation: Present illness (HPI) is the detailed description of the patient's pain or complaint, including signs, location, quality, etc.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: The traction splint is designed to splint which musculoskeletal injury.
questions.explanation: Traction splints are specifically for femoral shaft fractures to reduce pain, bleeding, and muscle spasms.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Hyperventilation leads to which condition, except:
questions.hint: Hyperventilation blows off COâ‚‚, making blood alkaline.
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation causes respiratory alkalosis (low COâ‚‚), not acidosis. It also causes hypocapnia and can cause carpopedal spasm.
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: A patients upper airway obstructed with sputum. You insist the pt for coughing. While breathing which sound can hear:
questions.explanation: Stridor is a high-pitched inspiratory sound from upper airway obstruction (e.g., sputum, foreign body, croup).
questions.subject: Airway

questions.text: One talking patient suddenly become mute. We need to observe the patient. What are the things you can do at this situation
options: Keep calm and quiet for time and observe for non verbal clues
questions.hint: Sudden mutism may have many causes; don't panic.
questions.explanation: Stay calm, observe for nonverbal clues, and reassess. Shouting or leaving is inappropriate; informing dispatch is not the immediate action.
questions.subject: Fundamentals and Operations

questions.text: What should you do when you are administering breath by using bag-mask for a child who is not breathing, but does have a pulse
options: Give breath at a rate of 1 breath every 3 to 5 seconds
Position the child on his or her stomach
Squeeze the bag as often as possible
questions.hint: Child respiratory arrest with pulse needs slow, controlled breaths.
questions.explanation: For a child with a pulse but not breathing, give 1 breath every 3â€“5 seconds (12â€“20 breaths/min).

questions.text: Once the baby's head has delivered, you should:
options: clear the airway by suctioning the baby's mouth and nose
massage the mother's uterus to stimulate contractions to deliver the baby's body
gently pull on both sides of the head to assist with delivery of the body
time and assess the mother's contractions to estimate the length of the delivery
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: If a pregnant patient experiences a seizure, you should:
options: massage the uterus to relax any contractions
look for vaginal bleeding due to placenta previa
perform a rapid assessment, particularly of the airway, and transport the patient on her left side
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Care for a premature infant includes:
options: cooling the newborn to stimulate respirations
covering both eyes with moist, sterile dressings
withholding oxygen until arrival at the hospital
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: The hollow organ in the female reproductive system where the fetus grows is called the:
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Which of the following statements about injuries to the female genitalia is true?
options: Foreign objects should be removed from the vagina before transport
Vaginal bleeding should be controlled by the insertion of sterile dressings
Bleeding involving the external female genitalia should be controlled with a dry, sterile dressing and direct pressure
Bleeding of the ureter can be easily controlled with direct local pressure
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: When assisting a woman during the second stage of labor, you should:
options: check for the umbilical cord around the infant's neck once the head is delivered
keep constant pressure against the infant's head from the time it presents in the vagina until it is delivered
allow the infant's head to hang dependent from the vagina without support to help the drainage of fluid from the airway
suction the nose and mouth once the infant's body is born
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Once an infant has been born, you should:
options: place it on the mother's stomach
place two clamps on the umbilical cord and cut the cord as close to the infant's abdomen as possible
keep the infant at the level of the mother's vagina
elevate the infant's head while you suction the mouth and both nostrils
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: If the amniotic sac does not rupture before an infant is born, you should:
options: immediately transport the mother to the emergency department
immediately break the membrane using a sterile clamp or your sterile, gloved fingers
break the membrane once the entire infant is born
massage the uterus to rupture the sac while protecting the infant from a gush of fluid
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: You have delivered a limp infant who is not breathing. You should first:
options: stimulate the infant by flicking the soles of the feet
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A woman loses approximately 500 mL of blood after delivering a healthy infant. You recognize that:
options: this is a normal amount of blood loss after delivery
the vagina must be packed with sterile gauze
the mother should be placed in the shock position
the placenta must be removed immediately by manual traction
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: En route to the hospital with a woman who is 9 months' pregnant and in active labor, you notice that the umbilical cord is prolapsed. You should first:
options: push the cord back into the vagina
place a dry, sterile towel around the cord
position the mother with her head down and/or hips raised
insert your sterile, gloved hand into the vagina and push the infant's head up
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Care of a patient who is experiencing heavy vaginal bleeding should include:
options: placing the patient in a semi-Fowler's position
holding the patient's legs together to slow the bleeding
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: A woman in labor tells you that she is expecting twins. The first infant is born en route to the hospital. You should:
options: clamp, but not cut, the umbilical cord of the first infant
leave the umbilical cord hanging free until the second infant is delivered
clamp and cut the umbilical cord of the first infant
apply gentle traction to the umbilical cord to assist with delivery of the second infant
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Proper care for a premature infant should include:
options: warming the patient compartment of the ambulance to 90 to 95F
placing hot water bottles on the infant to keep it warm
giving oxygen with an infant-sized face mask
allowing the mother, father, and any other family members to handle the infant
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Why is it important to keep a newborn warm while providing treatment?
options: A newborn has difficulty regulating body temperature
It is easier for you to provide treatment in a warm environment
Oxygen will flow from an oxygen source with little difficulty in a warm environment
The warm environment will make the newborn sleepy, making transport easier
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: When you compare a primagravida vs. a multigravida woman, a general rule of thumb is that a primagravida's length of the first stage of labor is:
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Para refers to the number of:
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: A 35-year-old woman, 32 weeks' gestation, reports increased swelling in her hands and feet the last week and now complains of a sudden, intense headache. Her vital signs are as follows: pulse of 92/min; respirations of 24/min; and blood pressure of 144/92 mm Hg. You are concerned she has:
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: A 26-year-old woman complains of vaginal bleeding, heavier than usual and a sharp pain in her RLQ. She reports her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago and has a history of pelvic inflammatory disease. You are suspicious she has:
options: an exacerbation of her pelvic inflammatory disease
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Which of the following would NOT be an indication of imminent birth?
options: The mother feels the urge to move her bowels
The mother states she needs to push
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Which of the following is an indication of imminent birth?
options: The mother states the baby is coming
Contractions are 10 minutes apart and lasting 30 seconds
The mother instinctively begins to clean the house and pack for the hospital
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: While preparing a pregnant woman to deliver in the ambulance, she should be positioned:
options: flat on her back with her legs bent
in a semi-Fowler's position with padding under her hips
on her left side with the right leg elevated
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Greenish amniotic fluid is an indication of:
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: Thick meconium staining is most likely to result in:
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Calculate an APGAR score for the following infant. Body is pink with acrocyanosis. Pulse is 88/min and respirations are absent. Infant does not cry or move when stimulated, and muscle tone is weak.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: You have just delivered a baby boy. His appearance is pink and his pulse is 148/min with slow respirations. He has a weak cry when stimulated and resists attempts to straighten his legs. His APGAR score would be?
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Prolapsed cords are more common with which presentation?
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: A 17-year-old woman has delivered a small infant breech. The head remains in the birth canal. The EMT-B should immediately:
options: press firmly on the lower abdomen to push the head out
apply gentile traction to the infant's body
make a V with your fingers and create an airway for the infant
questions.subject: OB/GYN

questions.text: What is all-hazards preparedness?
options: Process of identifying at-risk populations for a disaster or public health emergency
Determination if needs are greater than resources
Uniform approach to prepare and respond to hazard regardless of cause
Unique approach to respond to various types of natural disasters
questions.explanation: Uniform approach to prepare and respond to hazard regardless of cause
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate authority to contact if an incident of bioterrorism is suspected?
options: Local or state public health department
questions.explanation: Local or state public health department
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: What is a blast injury?
options: Injury resulting from an explosive device
questions.explanation: Injury resulting from an explosive device
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is one essential component of a regional disaster plan?
options: Consider all types of natural disasters
Rely on FEMA or other federal authorities
questions.explanation: Be created in advance
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: In a scene with hazardous materials, in what zone would decontamination occur?
options: None of these answers are correct
questions.explanation: Warm
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: How should a responder prepare for deployment to a disaster zone?
options: All of these answers are strategies to prepare for deployment
questions.explanation: All of these answers are strategies to prepare for deployment
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: What is one tool to help diagnose nerve agents?
questions.explanation: DUMBELS
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: What is the most common disaster worldwide?
questions.explanation: Flooding
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: Which statement is true about disaster triage?
options: Transport all casualties to nearest hospital quickly
Provide the greatest good for each individual patient
questions.explanation: Triage is dynamic and continuous
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: What is the leading environmental cause of death in the United States?
questions.explanation: Heat emergencies
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: What is the first step of SALT mass casualty triage?
questions.explanation: Sort
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: Which of the following is a function of the public health system during a disaster?
options: Protect population against injury and disease
Transport casualties to local health care centers
None of these answers are functions of the public health system
questions.explanation: Protect population against injury and disease
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: Which of the following best defines the function of public health?
options: To recognize health threats that may affect communities
To provide emergency care and transport sick and injured to medical facility
To ensure scene safety and security
To prevent, delay, and treat disease, injury, and disability in individual patients
questions.explanation: To recognize health threats that may affect communities
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: What is one challenge of Incident Management?
questions.explanation: Communication
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: What is the initial goal of mass casualty triage?
options: Ensure that all patients are treated equally
Remove the dead from the scene
Treat all patients, regardless of injury, as quickly as possible
Sort and assess casualties, identifying life-threatening injuries
questions.explanation: Sort and assess casualties, identifying life-threatening injuries
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: What type of injury is least common after a nuclear or radiological disaster?
questions.explanation: Communicable disease
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a mental health impact of disaster?
questions.explanation: Fear
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: What is a nuclear event?
options: Release of radioactive materials to human-populated area
Nuclear detonation and accompanying massive explosion
questions.explanation: Nuclear detonation and accompanying massive explosion
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: What is the first priority of all citizens or responders?
options: Be able to help in a crisis
Be ready to immediately deploy to disaster area
questions.explanation: Protect self from danger
questions.subject: Patient Safety

questions.text: Which of the following is a clue that a chemical disaster is probable?
options: Known hazard, substance leaking or spilling
All of these answers are clues of a chemical disaster
Multiple 911 calls from the same area
questions.explanation: All of these answers are clues of a chemical disaster
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: Which activity should take place as part of the recovery from a disaster or public health emergency?
questions.explanation: After action review
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: What does resilience in the PRE-DISASTER Paradigm refer to?
options: The ability of the federal government to return things to normal
The ability of a community to prevent disasters
The ability of a community or a person to return to normal after an event
questions.explanation: The ability of a community or a person to return to normal after an event
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: What is a component of a risk analysis?
options: Do the greatest good for the greatest number
Ability to overcome adversity due to a disaster
questions.explanation: Assess potential vulnerabilities
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: When does treatment at the disaster scene end?
options: After all casualties transported from scene
questions.explanation: After all casualties transported from scene
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: What sort of treatment may be performed prior to decontamination?
options: Treatment not requiring personal protective equipment
questions.explanation: Life-saving interventions
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: What triage category would you assign to a casualty that requires care that can be safely delayed without affecting probability of survival?
questions.explanation: Delayed (Yellow)
questions.subject: Patient Assessment

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a vulnerable population in a disaster or other public health emergency?
options: All of these answers are examples of a vulnerable population
questions.explanation: All of these answers are examples of a vulnerable population
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: Which of the following is not a way that a chemical may enter the body?
questions.explanation: Transmigration
questions.subject: Environmental and Toxicology

questions.text: What is a potential illness or injury that would occur weeks after a natural disaster?
questions.explanation: Communicable disease
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: Which of the following account for most terror events worldwide?
questions.explanation: Explosive devices
questions.subject: Disaster

questions.text: All of the following are the components of SEA-3 assessment tool except?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: When an EMT begins to assist someone and then leaves the person without providing alternative form of care is referred to as?
questions.subject: Professionalism and Ethics

questions.text: While assessing a patient, you note that he is unable to smile or stick his tongue out when asked. This change in neuromotor response is known as what type of apraxia?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: You are dispatched to a home for an 85 year old female who is not feeling well. Upon arrival the family member tells you that the patient has been confused for the past one hour. She is disoriented to time place or person, otherwise she is a normally an active person who is alert to time, place, person and event. As part of your differential diagnosis u suspect all of the following except
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: The mental disorder that is present since birth that is diagnosed during childhood and is based upon the measurement of IQ is referred to as?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: The inability to experience pleasure is referred to as?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: You are dispatched to evaluate a 67 year old male who is standing in the middle of the local fast food restaurant. He appears disheveled and unkempt. The manager tells you that he is a local homeless man who thinks that something is wrong with him because he calls himself 'God'. When you enter the restaurant u see him standing on the table and telling the customers he is god and has the power to strike them dead. The belief of his power is an example of?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: You are dispatched to a call for a 36 year old male construction site worker who is complaining of anxiety like symptoms at work. Upon your arrival the worker tells you that he feels palpitations, chest discomfort, vomiting, nausea, sweating and shaking. 12 lead ECG reveals no changes in relation to myocardial infarction. Based on the presentation of symptoms what is the condition of the patient
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: Time-limited periods in which one exhibits signs and symptoms of a disorder are called?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: Which of the following statements about delirium tremens is not true?
options: They usually develop 2 to 3 days after withdrawal of alcohol
They occur in individuals who have experienced recent binge drinking
DT's are marked by decreased level of consciousness with hallucinations
Seizures and delirium tremens are ominous sign
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: The best approach for gaining information from behavioral emergency patient is to?
options: Physically restrain the patient before questioning
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: Hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thought, speech and behaviors are commonly associated with which type of psychiatric condition?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: Due to which of the following conditions are cardiac abnormalities or dysrhythmias common in patients with eating disorders
options: Bleeding Of Gl tract or esophagus
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: You are dispatched to a local jail for a 20 year old man who has been arrested for destruction of property. Upon arrival the officers inform you that he a long criminal record for fighting and cruelty to animals since adolescence. His crimes have become so aggressive and he appears to have no regard to his or anyone else's safety. Based on this case presentation which personality disorder do u suspect?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: Many patients who have lapses in memory will make up events or fill in the gaps with stories about events that never occurred. This process is known as?
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: In a rear-impact motor vehicle collision, which area of the spine is most susceptible to injury?
questions.explanation: The sudden increase in acceleration produces posterior displacement of the occupants and possible hyperextension of the cervical spine if the headrest is not properly adjusted. The potential for cervical spine injuries is great.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 36-year-old male sustains blunt force thoracic trauma and fits the criteria for a load-and-go patient. Which of the following should be performed on the scene?
options: Obtain a finger-stick serum lactate level
Assess for other potentially life-threatening conditions
questions.explanation: The purpose of the initial assessment is to prioritize the patient and to identify all immediately life-threatening conditions. The information gathered is used to make decisions about critical interventions and time of transport.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The use of external laryngeal manipulation:
options: Decreases the risk of airway trauma
questions.explanation: Manipulating the thyroid cartilage can help bring the vocal cords into view during endotracheal intubation. This is called external laryngeal manipulation (ELM). The movement is usually pressing the thyroid cartilage backward against the esophagus and then upward and slightly to the patient’s right side.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A pericardiocentesis is performed to:
options: To remove fluid from the lining around the lungs
To remove fluid from the lining around the heart
To inject medications directly into the heart
questions.explanation: The pericardial sac is an inelastic membrane that surrounds the heart. If blood collects rapidly between the heart and pericardium from a cardiac injury, the ventricles of the heart will be compressed, making the heart less able to refill, and cardiac output falls.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 56-year-old male sustains a gunshot wound to the abdomen. Vital signs are BP 74/32, P 136 present only at the carotid, and R 24 and shallow. The target of fluid resuscitation is:
questions.explanation: This is an example of internal, uncontrolled hemorrhage. Administer sufficient normal saline to maintain peripheral perfusion, following local or EMS agency medical direction policies. Maintaining peripheral perfusion is generally defined as giving enough fluid—usually in boluses—to return a peripheral pulse, such as a radial pulse.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unconscious 15-year-old male was struck on the head with a baseball bat. His arms and legs are extended and his right pupil is dilated and fixed. You should:
options: Insert an oropharyngeal airway, administer oxygen, and restrict fluids
Apply a non-rebreather mask, restrict fluids, and administer Mannitol
Perform nasotracheal intubation, restrict fluids, and provide adequate ventilation
Perform bag-mask ventilation at 20 per minute
questions.explanation: The classic findings of this life-threatening situation are a decreasing level of consciousness (LOC) that rapidly progresses to coma, dilation of the pupil and an outward—downward deviation of the eye on the side of the injury, paralysis of the arm and leg on the side opposite the injury, or decerebrate posturing (arms and legs extended). The danger of immediate herniation outweighs the risk of cerebral ischemia that can follow hyperventilation. The cerebral herniation syndrome is the only situation in which hyperventilation is still indicated. (You must ventilate every three seconds [20/minute] for adults.)
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following mechanisms of injury does not commonly cause damage to the spinal cord?
questions.explanation: Certain mechanisms of trauma can overcome the protective properties, injuring the spinal column and cord. The most common mechanisms are hyperextension, hyperflexion, compression, and rotation. Less commonly, lateral stress or distraction will injure the cord.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 4-year-old female is unconscious after falling off a bicycle. Which of the following is the best method to open the airway?
questions.explanation: Look for signs of airway obstruction in the child, including apnea, stridor, and “gurgling” respirations. If identified, perform a jaw-thrust maneuver without moving the neck.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When performing the ITLS Primary Survey, the team leader may minimize errors by:
options: Permitting crew to continue the assessment
questions.explanation: This team approach makes the most efficient use of time and allows you to rapidly perform the initial assessment without becoming distracted by performing the necessary interventions yourself, which can interrupt your thought process and cause errors.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You have placed an NPA in your patient and now observe mild hemorrhage from the nares. You should:
options: Immediately remove the NPA and pack the nose with gauze
Immediately remove the NPA and reinsert on the opposite nare
Leave the NPA in place so as not to disturb the clot or reactivate bleeding
Contact medical control as an NPA will not cause hemorrhage
questions.explanation: Mild hemorrhage from the nose after insertion of the airway is not an indication to remove it. In fact, it is probably better to keep an NPA in place so as not to disturb the clot or reactivate the bleeding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 27-year-old male with blunt chest trauma from a motor vehicle collision was successfully intubated at the scene. While ventilating the patient, you note resistance with absence of right chest wall movement. You should suspect a:
questions.explanation: The development of decreased lung compliance (difficulty in squeezing the bag-mask device) in the intubated patient should always alert you to the possibility of a tension pneumothorax.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 34-year-old man has a gunshot wound to the right groin area. Arterial bleeding, which cannot be controlled with direct pressure or tourniquet, is coming from the wound. The patient appears confused, diaphoretic, and has weak peripheral pulses. What is the appropriate fluid resuscitation regimen for this patient?
options: Intravenous fluid at a "keep open" rate
Intravenous fluid; give enough fluid to maintain peripheral pulses
Intravenous fluid at a "wide open" rate
No intravenous access should be established in this situation
questions.explanation: Give only enough normal saline to maintain a blood pressure high enough for adequate peripheral perfusion. Maintaining peripheral perfusion may be defined as producing a peripheral pulse (such as a radial pulse).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The gold standard to confirm that the endotracheal tube is placed in the trachea is:
options: Presence of condensation in the tube
questions.explanation: Although the most reliable method of ensuring proper placement is actually visualizing the tube passing through the glottic opening, even this is not 100% certain. In fact, it is only reliable for the moment you see it. The gold standard for confirming and monitoring ETT placement is waveform capnography.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the causes of pediatric injury listed below is most likely to result in severe injury or death?
questions.explanation: Injuries sustained by children riding bicycles, motorcycles, and dirt bikes, especially if they are not wearing a helmet, are often severe. Motor-vehicle collisions — especially if car seats, booster seats, and lap-belt restraints are improperly used — may result in seat-belt syndrome, which can include life-threatening injuries to the liver, spleen, intestines and/or lumbar spine.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following injuries would change a trauma patient's transport classification from 'stable' to 'load and go'?
questions.explanation: Conditions that can rapidly lead to shock include penetrating wounds to the torso, abnormal chest exam, tender distended abdomen, pelvic instability and bilateral femur fractures.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following findings would not make a patient difficult to ventilate with a bag-valve mask?
questions.explanation: Predictors of difficult mask ventilation can be remembered using the “BOOTS” mnemonic: B- Beards, O - Obesity, O - Older patients, T- Toothlessness, S- Snores or stridor
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the most frequent cause for an intubated trauma patient to develop poor lung compliance while being ventilated?
questions.explanation: The development of decreased lung compliance (difficulty in squeezing the bag-mask device) in the intubated patient should always alert you to the possibility of a tension pneumothorax.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Hemostatic agents applied directly to the source of bleeding must be used in conjunction with:
options: Pressure points to arteries proximal to the wound
Elevation of the wound above the level of the heart
questions.explanation: Pack the hemostatic agent in the wound and hold firm pressure. The hemostatic agent is an “adjunct” to assist in controlling hemorrhage, not a hemorrhage control by itself.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Routine use of hyperventilation in the traumatic brain injury (TBI) patient will:
options: Cause vasoconstriction and increased cerebral ischemia
Cause vasodilation and decreased intracranial pressure (ICP)
Cause an increase of end-tidal CO2
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation actually has only a slight effect on brain swelling, but causes a significant decrease in cerebral perfusion from that same vasoconstriction, resulting in cerebral hypoxia. Thus, both hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which finding requires interruption of the ITLS Primary Survey?
questions.explanation: You may interrupt the assessment sequence only if (1) the scene becomes unsafe, (2) you must treat exsanguinating hemorrhage, (3) you must treat an airway obstruction, or (4) you must treat cardiac arrest. (Respiratory arrest, dyspnea, or bleeding management should be delegated to other team members while you continue assessment of the patient.)
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Bag-valve-mask ventilation:
options: Rarely causes gastric distention due to low airway pressures
Is more effective in patients whose dentures have been removed
Is easily accomplished by one rescuer at the head
May exceed 60 cm H20 pressure in the airway
questions.explanation: Bag-valve-mask ventilation has challenges because pressures generated by squeezing the bag may equal or exceed 60 cm H20.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding pulmonary contusion?
options: It is uncommon in chest trauma
It is caused by hemorrhage into the lung parenchyma
It is only caused by blunt force trauma
questions.explanation: Pulmonary contusion is a very common chest injury. It is caused by hemorrhage into lung parenchyma secondary to blunt force trauma or penetrating injury such as a missile. It occurs commonly with flail segment or multiple rib fractures. A pulmonary contusion takes hours to develop and rarely develops during prehospital care.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 25-year-old female was stabbed in the left arm. She presents with an altered mental status and signs of shock. Bleeding is controlled by direct pressure and use of a tourniquet. Which intravenous fluid therapy is appropriate?
options: Estimate blood loss and administer fluid at a 3:1 ratio
Fluid administration to maintain systolic pressure of 60 mmHg
Intravenous fluid administration bolus of 500 mL, then reassess
questions.explanation: When bleeding is controlled, give normal saline as a bolus (500 mL in adults; 20 mL/kg in pediatric patients) rapidly and then repeat the ITLS Reassessment Exam.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In the absence of herniation syndrome, adult patients with suspected traumatic brain injury should be ventilated as a rate of:
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintain normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45 mmHg.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the most likely cause of unequal pupils in a patient whose mental status is altered due to a head injury?
questions.explanation: Suspect head injury unless patient is alert, then suspect eye injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 57-year-old male has a respiratory rate of 36, ETCO2 of 30 mmHg, and an oxygen saturation of 80%. You should:
options: Coach the patient to slow his breathing as his ETCO2 level indicates hyperventilation
Ventilate with a BVM at a higher rate to increase the patient's oxygen levels
Ventilate with a BVM at a rate of 6 to increase the patient's ETCO2 level
questions.explanation: Supplemental oxygen is recommended for all trauma patients, especially if hypoxic.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 31-year-old male presents with difficulty breathing, rapid and weak pulse, flat neck veins, and midline trachea. Your assessment of the left side of the chest reveals decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Clinical signs of shock may be apparent. The neck veins are usually flat secondary to profound hypovolemia, but may very rarely be distended due to mediastinal compression. Other signs of hemothorax include decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion on the affected side.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 45-year-old female is found unconscious at the scene of a motor vehicle collision. Her vital signs are BP 80/40, P 130, and R 30. Which of the following is the most likely cause for her vital signs?
options: Bleeding into the chest or abdomen
Spinal cord injury with neurogenic shock
questions.explanation: Hypovolemic shock victims usually have tachycardia, are pale, and have flat neck veins. So, if you find a trauma victim with a fast heart rate, who is pale, with weak radial pulses and flat neck veins, this patient is probably bleeding from some injury, either internally or externally (or possibly both).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The cause of a secondary brain injury is:
questions.explanation: Secondary brain injury is the result of hypoxia and/or decreased perfusion of brain tissue.
questions.subject: Trauma
