questions.text: The first step in artificial ventilation with a bag-valve-mask unit in patients with no suspected trauma is to
options: position the mask correctly on the face using both hands.
place the patient’s head in a hyperextended, sniffing position.
insert an airway adjunct and select the correct mask size.
have an assistant squeeze the bag until the patient’s chest rises.
questions.explanation: Correct order of steps would be b, c, a, and d.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You take a report from a first responder who describes a patient as postictal; based on this report, you would expect to find the patient
questions.explanation: The seizure event is referred to as ictal, hence postictal refers to the period after the seizure wherein a patent is often confused for a period of time.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: When suctioning a patient, how far should you insert a soft suction catheter?
options: as far as you can see
as far as the base of the tongue
questions.explanation: Choice b is the correct procedure. Choice a is correct for a rigid catheter, choice c is the correct method for inserting an oropharyngeal airway, and choice d is applicable only to endotracheal intubation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the correct procedure for a patient who has secretions or emesis that suctioning cannot easily remove?
options: Insert an oropharyngeal or nasopharyngeal airway immediately.
Suction for 15 seconds, ventilate for two -minutes, and then repeat.
Logroll the patient and clear the oropharynx and nasopharynx.
Hyperventilate the patient with a bag-valvemask unit. –the emt-basic Practice exam 131
questions.explanation: Choice c is the correct procedure for clearing the airway when simple suctioning is not working. Choice b is appropriate for a patient with copious frothy secretions like the kind produced with pulmonary edema. Choices a and d are inappropriate without first clearing the airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the purpose of the head-tilt/chin-lift technique?
options: to position the patient for insertion of an -airway adjunct
to remove foreign bodies from the upper -airway
to help the rescuer better visualize the larynx and vocal cords
to lift the tongue and epiglottis out of their obstructing position
questions.explanation: The purpose of the head-tilt/chin-lift is to move the tongue and epiglottis out of the way of the airway; it is not useful for intubation (use the sniffing position), to remove foreign bodies, or to visualize the larynx.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: After opening the airway, the next step in patient management is to
questions.explanation: After opening the airway, the EMT should assess the rate and depth of ventilations; choices a, c, and/or d would occur only after respiratory -adequacy has been assessed and they are appropriate to use.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A patient should receive high-flow oxygen if he or she exhibits
questions.explanation: Cyanosis, or a bluish coloration of the mucus membranes and skin, is caused by inadequate oxygen supply to the body tissues; the other choices are unrelated to oxygen supply.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The first step in the delivery of high-flow oxygen via a nonrebreather mask is to
options: inflate the reservoir bag with oxygen.
questions.explanation: Masks come in adult and pediatric sizes. In order to deliver the highest possible concentration of oxygen to the patient, you must have a properly fitting mask. Steps d, b, and c must then be completed (in that order) before placing the mask on the patient’s face.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: When providing mouth-to-mask ventilation with supplementary oxygen, what is the first step after sealing the mask to the patient’s face?
options: Follow body substance precautions before touching the patient.
Connect the one-way valve and filter (if available) to the mask.
Exhale slowly over the ventilation port for 1.5 to 2 seconds.
Attach oxygen tubing to the mask and set the flow rate at 15–30 L/min.
questions.explanation: After sealing the mask to the patient’s face, you should begin mouth-to-mask ventilation with a long, slow breath; steps a, b, and d should be done before applying the mask.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The correct rate of artificial ventilations for an adult patient is
questions.explanation: The correct rate is 12 ventilations per minute for an adult (one breath every five seconds), and the correct rate for a child is 20 ventilations per minute.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: When using the two-person bag-valve-mask procedure, one EMT ventilates the patient while the other
options: suctions the patient and administers CPR.
inserts the oral or nasopharyngeal airway.
maintains the mask seal and monitors chest rise.
questions.explanation: During a two-person bag-valve-mask procedure, one EMT ventilates the patient while the other maintains the airway and monitors the patient’s chest rise.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Where is the cricoid cartilage located?
questions.explanation: The cricoid cartilage forms a ring of firm cartilage and is located inferior to (below) the larynx.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is awake, confused, and disoriented. How would you grade her using the AVPU scale?
questions.explanation: A means alert. The other letters in the acronym stand for verbal, pain, and unresponsive.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A bulb syringe is used to suction infants up to the age of
questions.explanation: A bulb syringe is used to suction an infant’s nasal passage or mouth and is generally used with infants up to age 3–4 months. If you are using mechanical or hand-powered suction equipment in infants of this age, be very careful to use the lowest possible pressures needed to accomplish the job.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The right ventricle pumps blood into the
questions.explanation: The right ventricle receives oxygen-poor blood from the right atrium. It then pumps the blood to the lungs via the pulmonary artery, where it receives oxygen and releases carbon dioxide.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A 56-year-old female patient complains of mild chest discomfort. You should
options: decide what type of heart problem it might be.
decide whether the patient has a heart -problem.
maintain a high index of suspicion for cardiac compromise.
questions.explanation: The role of the EMT-Basic is not to diagnose the exact cause of the patient’s chief complaint. Maintaining a high suspicion for a cardiac emergency will guide your next step in managing this patient appropriately.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 62-year-old male with a history of heart disease. He is experiencing chest pain. Your first action should be to
options: place the pads for the AED on his chest.
begin CPR while preparing the AED.
ask him if he has taken his nitroglycerin, and if not, offer to assist him.
place him in a comfortable position and administer high-flow oxygen.
questions.explanation: Your first action would be to administer oxygen and place the patient in a comfortable position; next, if not contraindicated, you would request permission to administer nitroglycerin. You should first obtain a set of vital signs to ensure he is not in cardiogenic shock. Never put defibrillator pads onto a conscious patient with a pulse; this procedure is contraindicated. AED pads are not serving the same function as the electrodes used by ALS providers to monitor the heart rhythm.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The EMT-Basic should request prehospital ACLS for the care of the cardiac arrest patient because
options: ACLS intervention provides higher survival rates.
EMT-Basics must have prehospital ACLS present to perform defibrillation.
only paramedics can transport cardiac arrest patients.
the EMT-Basic is not adequately trained to manage cardiac arrest.
questions.explanation: Prehospital ACLS provides additional medications and other therapies that may help either terminate the cardiac arrest state or help prevent the patient who has regained a pulse from going back into cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 29-year-old male who has fallen off a ladder. He is bleeding profusely from a wound on his right forearm and has severe pain in his left thigh. Which of the following is an appropriate initial treatment for this patient?
options: Perform a quick initial assessment to assess his ABCs.
Stop the bleeding by applying a tourniquet near the elbow.
Maintain an open airway and ventilate the patient with a gas-valve mask.
Elevate the patient’s legs 20–30 cm to treat him for shock.
questions.explanation: Although you note several injuries, you still need to perform a rapid initial assessment to determine if any problems with the ABCs that you have not noted may be more life threatening than what you can obviously see. Stop the bleeding (but do not use a tourniquet) and treat the patient for shock, but do not elevate the patient’s legs if there is an injury to the legs, pelvis, head, neck, chest, abdomen, or spine. There is no indication that this patient needs ventilation at this time.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Touching the patient when the semiautomatic external defibrillator (SAED) is analyzing the rhythm
options: is acceptable with today’s modern defibrillators.
is indicated to maintain cardiac compressions.
is indicated to maintain artificial ventilation.
can cause the SAED to misinterpret a rhythm. –the emt-basic Practice exam 133
questions.explanation: When the SAED is attempting to analyze the patient’s electrical rhythm, any movement of the patient or the unit could cause the machine to misinterpret the signal.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What should you do for the cardiac arrest patient found in the rain?
options: Perform one rapid defibrillation, then move the patient inside.
Defibrillate three times, then move the patient inside.
Move the patient inside, away from the rain.
Perform one rapid defibrillation, then start CPR if pulseless.
questions.explanation: Safety is critical when performing defibrillation. Any defibrillation attempt in the rain may possibly harm anyone near the patient during the discharge of the unit.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is bleeding profusely from a wound on her right forearm. Where is the pressure point for this injury?
questions.explanation: The pressure point for wounds of the forearm is the brachial artery.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Kinetic energy is described as the
options: energy an object has while in motion.
tendency for an object to stay in motion.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy an object has while in motion. Choice b describes mass, choice c describes energy, and choice d describes inertia.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: A bystander is performing CPR when you arrive. You evaluate the scene, practice body substance isolation, and begin your initial assessment by having the bystander
options: provide a history of cardiac arrest.
questions.explanation: Having the bystander stop CPR will allow you to reassess the patient’s ventilatory and circulatory status. This will tell you whether you need to continue CPR or whether the patient has regained a pulse or is breathing.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is bleeding from a wound to the forearm. The blood flows in a steady, dark-red stream. What type of bleeding should you -suspect?
questions.explanation: Venous bleeding flows in a steady, dark-red stream. Arterial bleeding is bright red and spurts from the wound, while capillary bleeding oozes. Internal bleeding may or may not present externally recognizable signs or symptoms.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is restless, anxious, and complaining of thirst. She exhibits increased heart rate and pale, clammy skin. You should do all the following EXCEPT
options: maintain an open airway and provide oxygen.
elevate her legs if not contraindicated.
cover the patient with a blanket to keep her warm.
give the patient small amounts of liquid to drink.
questions.explanation: This patient has the classic signs and symptoms of shock. Do not offer anything to eat or drink to a patient you suspect of being in shock, since surgery may be necessary later.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: If a person was hit by an object described in the following, which would have the potential to cause the most damage?
questions.explanation: Speed is squared in the calculation of kinetic energy. A one-pound object traveling at 30 mph is nine times more injurious than a one-pound object traveling at 10 mph.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Which heart rhythm often converts to ventricular fibrillation?
questions.explanation: Ventricular tachycardia often converts to ventricular fibrillation, a life-threatening heart rhythm that the AED is designed to correct.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the reason for stopping CPR before applying the AED?
options: to give the patient a chance to breathe on his or her own
to allow the ACLS system to take over patient care
to allow the EMT to verify pulselessness and apnea
to allow other rescuers to get out of the way –the emt-basic Practice exam 134
questions.explanation: Stop CPR briefly to verify pulselessness and apnea. Once you have the leads ready for placement on the chest wall, you should stop CPR, clear away from the patient, attach the AED leads, and perform the initial analysis. Resume CPR after the first round of shocks if they were unsuccessful and the patient persists in -pulselessness. Stop CPR again every time you deliver shocks.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: After receiving three consecutive “no shock indicated” messages on the AED, you should do which of the following?
options: Check for a pulse and begin CPR.
questions.explanation: After three consecutive “no shock indicated” messages, you should make sure to begin transporting the patient while continuing CPR. Some jurisdictions may adopt a protocol or standing order that requires you to consider transporting following the first or second set of AED rounds, regardless of whether you shock. At the minimum, you should begin your transport by the third time you perform an analysis.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are transporting a patient who has been resuscitated but is still unresponsive. You should check the patient’s pulse every
questions.explanation: If the patient has been resuscitated but is still unresponsive, check the pulse every 30 seconds during transport and keep the AED leads attached to the patient.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The medical direction physician orders you to deliver additional shocks to a patient in cardiac arrest while en route to the hospital. What is the correct procedure to follow?
options: Wait for the arrival of the ACLS team.
Deliver the shocks without stopping CPR.
Stop the vehicle before reanalyzing the rhythm.
Refuse to defibrillate the patient while en route.
questions.explanation: If it becomes necessary to deliver shocks while en route with the patient, the proper procedure is to stop the vehicle before reanalyzing the rhythm because the AED has a motion detector sensor in place that will not allow the unit to operate in the presence of motion.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the primary action of nitroglycerin?
questions.explanation: The primary action of nitroglycerin is to dilate the myocardial (coronary) arteries, therefore easing the heart’s workload by increasing the blood flow. Lowering of blood pressure is a secondary effect seen with vessel dilation. Reflex tachycardia, not bradycardia, may occur as a result of nitroglycerine administration. It does not have any direct effects on the muscles of the heart.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Patients commonly describe heart attack pain as which of the following -characteristics?
questions.explanation: Myocardial pain is often difficult to determine because it can take on many different characters; however, patients most commonly (over 40% of the time) describe the pain of a myocardial infarction as a crushing, squeezing pressure that radiates outward to the arms and upper back.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: In pedestrian versus automobile impacts, which of the following statements is true?
options: Children often turn toward the impact and are often thrown down and under the vehicle.
Adults tend to turn toward the vehicle before impact.
Children often turn toward the impact and are often scooped and thrown over the vehicle.
There tends to be no difference in the way adults and children respond in these situations.
questions.explanation: Children often turn toward the impact and are often thrown down and under the vehicle.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient has profuse bleeding from a wound on her lower leg but no signs of skeletal injury. The steps you should take to stop the bleeding, in the correct order, are
options: direct pressure, elevation, pressure dressing, and pressure point.
pressure point, tourniquet, and concentrated or diffuse direct pressure.
pneumatic anti-shock garments (PASG), lower extremity elevation, and diffuse direct pressure.
elevation, pressure point, pressure dressing, and PASG.
questions.explanation: The combination of direct pressure, elevation, pressure dressing, and pressure point pressure is almost always successful in stopping bleeding in the extremities. Tourniquets are seldom needed. PASG use is performed cautiously under direct medical control, and it is never the first line of -treatment.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Where should you place your hands when using the head-tilt/chin-lift maneuver to open an unconscious patient’s airway?
options: on the nose, with the fingertips pinching it closed, and under the neck
on the nose, with the fingertips pinching it closed, and on the forehead
on the forehead, with the other hand under the neck
on the forehead, with the fingertips of the other hand under the lower jaw –the emt-basic Practice exam 135
questions.explanation: Lifting the jaw is necessary to dislodge the tongue from the back of the throat and provide a patent airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is found lying on the ground after falling off a roof. He is unconscious and apneic. Which method should you use to open the patient’s airway?
questions.explanation: The head-tilt/chin-lift may jeopardize the patient’s cervical spine. The other two procedures will not adequately open the airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Following an explosion, a patient is trapped in a collapsed structure and suffers crush injuries to both lower extremities. How would the injuries be classified based on the blast-injury phase?
questions.explanation: Tertiary blast injury includes those injuries resulting from structural collapse.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When splinting an injured limb, you should assess pulse, motor function, and sensation distal to the injury
options: before and after applying the splint.
questions.explanation: Assess pulse, motor function, and sensation distal to a splint both before and after applying the splint to ensure that the splint is not adversely affecting circulation to the limb.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When performing the modified jaw-thrust maneuver to open your patient’s airway, which of the following steps is NOT correct?
options: Stabilize the patient’s cervical spine with your forearms.
Rest your elbows on the same surface as the patient.
Tilt the head by applying gentle pressure to the forehead.
Use your index fingers to push the angles of the lower jaw forward.
questions.explanation: Tilting the head may compromise the stabilization of the cervical spine when using the modified jaw thrust.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The golden hour in emergency medicine refers to the first 60 minutes after the
questions.explanation: The first 60 minutes after the occurrence of multi-system trauma is the golden hour.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your unconscious patient has blood in his airway. You should
options: use a suction unit to immediately clear the -airway.
apply oxygen using a nonrebreather mask at 15 L/min.
use a bag-valve mask to clear the airway.
perform a finger sweep to remove the -blockage.
questions.explanation: Blood is too fluid to be cleared adequately by a finger sweep. The other answers are not appropriate unless the airway was cleared first by suction.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is behaving abnormally but refuses treatment after falling down a flight of stairs. Before transporting the patient without consent, you should
options: document the presence of any injury.
ask bystanders to serve as witnesses.
have bystanders help talk him into care.
questions.explanation: Before transporting a patient without consent, it is best to always seek medical direction.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You should not suction a patient’s airway for more than 15 seconds because
options: the patient’s tongue may be injured.
the suction unit’s battery may drain too quickly.
the patient will become hypoxic during this time.
you may cause the patient to vomit. –the emt-basic Practice exam 136
questions.explanation: While the other answers may be true, they may occur regardless of the time interval.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding using a pocket mask to ventilate a nonbreathing patient?
options: There is direct contact between the rescuer and the patient’s mouth.
Oxygen cannot be connected to the mask.
A one-way valve prevents exhaled air from contacting the rescuer.
Oxygen levels of 100% may be achieved.
questions.explanation: The one-way valve minimizes potential cross-exposure of the patient’s secretions and exhaled breath to the rescuer.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: To which patient should you administer oral -glucose?
options: 60-year-old female behaving as if she is intoxicated, and whose daughter informs you that she takes insulin by injection
45-year-old male with a history of diabetes behaving erratically after falling and hitting his head in the bathtub
70-year-old male with a long history of diabetes who is unconscious and cannot swallow
52-year-old female who tells you that she is feeling dizzy and has low blood sugar
questions.explanation: Administer oral glucose on medical direction (through protocol or standing order) only to patients with altered mental status and history of diabetes. Patient b should be first treated as a trauma patient, and because research shows poor outcomes following brain injuries and glucose administration, it is best to withhold its use until blood sugar can be checked (which is an ALS-provider skill in many areas). Patient c is not appropriate because of an inability to swallow properly; ALS should be called to provide IV dextrose to this patient. Glucose should be withheld until you can better determine if patient d is actually diabetic or not. (A blood glucose reading would be helpful with this patient as well.)
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The focused history for patients with altered mental status should include questions about a history of trauma, diabetes, seizures, and which of the following?
questions.explanation: Common causes of altered mental status include trauma, diabetes, seizures, and infectious disease. Asking about fever helps determine if there is a history of recent infection.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: All of the following may be signs of allergic reaction EXCEPT
questions.explanation: Signs of allergic reactions include increased heart rate, as the heart attempts to compensate for hypoperfusion. The two primary life--threatening events that occur during an allergic -reaction are profound vasoconstriction (resulting in shock) and compromised airway due to swelling, constriction, or mucous production.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Under medical direction, the EMT-Basic may administer epinephrine to a patient with respiratory distress or hypoperfusion resulting from an allergic reaction if the
options: patient has no history of heart -disease.
patient is in severe respiratory distress or arrest.
medication has been prescribed for this patient.
medication has been stored in the -refrigerator.
questions.explanation: EMTs can administer epinephrine under medical direction only if the medication was previously prescribed for this patient. The patient will be prescribed the correct dosage by the physician, and you are only allowed to administer the patient’s own medication.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Ethics is best described as
options: the principles of conduct; concerns for what is right or wrong, good or bad.
a code of conduct put forward by a society or some other group such as a religion.
the principle of doing good for the patient.
the obligation to treat all patients fairly.
questions.explanation: This is the principles of conduct; concerns for what is right or wrong, good or bad. Choice b describes morals, choice c is a better description of bioethics, and choice d describes justice for the patient.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Your patient is a 25-year-old female who is severely hypothermic after having plunged into an icy river. Although she was rescued after only a few minutes in the water, she is showing a diminished level of responsiveness. Your care should include
options: encouraging the patient to walk in order to improve her circulation.
covering the patient in blankets and turning up the heat in the ambulance.
giving her hot coffee or tea to drink and massaging her extremities.
beginning active rewarming measures under direct medical direction.
questions.explanation: Care for a hypothermic patient with a diminished level of responsiveness should include passive rewarming (blankets and heated room) only; active rewarming should take place only in the hospital environment, and hypothermic patients should not be allowed to exercise or to take stimulants, such as coffee or tea. However, your proto-cols may allow you to give warmed liquids that are not alcoholic or caffeinated to slightly hypothermic patients with a normal mental -status.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A sign of generalized cold emergency, or hypothermia, is cool skin on the
questions.explanation: Cool skin on the abdomen is a reliable sign of hypothermia in a patient because the abdomen is in the central core of the body and is generally covered under layers of clothing.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Two important principles in the emergency treatment of local cold injuries are to remove the patient from the cold environment and to
options: warm the whole body as soon as possible.
questions.explanation: The goal of care in cases of localized cold damage is to prevent further damage by removing the patient from the cold environment and protecting the damaged tissues from further injury. Rewarming is best accomplished in the hospital setting, where pain medication can be administered and the danger of reinjury due to -recooling is diminished.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following indicates that a patient with hyperthermia is in serious danger?
questions.explanation: A hyperthermic patient with hot skin must be treated aggressively before permanent organ damage sets in. When the skin is hot and dry, the normal sweating mechanisms have stopped functioning and the patient is in danger of brain damage due to excessive high body -temperature.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient has been stung by a bee, and the stinger is present in the wound. You should attempt to remove it by
options: cutting around it with a knife.
scraping it away with a rigid object.
questions.explanation: Grabbing a stinger with tweezers or your fingers can squeeze more venom into the wound (also, there is personal risk of accidental exposure to the venom if you use your hands). Instead, scrape the stinger out of the skin with a piece of cardboard or rigid plastic (a credit card is ideal). Cutting around the stinger causes more tissue damage.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In legal terms, a tort is a(n)
options: civil wrong committed by one individual against another.
action by an employee for a workers’ compensation claim.
questions.explanation: A tort is a civil wrong committed by one individual against another. Improper or negligent patient care would be considered a tort.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following are the signs of early respiratory distress in children and infants?
options: breathing rate of less than ten per minute, limp muscle tone, slow or absent heart rate, weak or absent distal pulses
increased rate of breathing, nasal flaring, intercostal or supraclavicular retractions, mottled skin color, abdominal muscle use
altered mental status, respiratory rate of over 60 or under 20 breaths per minute, severe retractions, severe use of accessory muscles
inability to cough, crying with tears but no sounds, cyanosis, abdominal or chest-wall movements with absent breath sounds
questions.explanation: Choice b describes early respiratory distress. Choice a describes the signs of impending respiratory arrest from insufficiency, choice c describes respiratory failure, and choice d describes airway obstruction.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is an 8-year-old female who had a single, brief seizure at school. Her mother arrives at the same time you do and reports that her daughter has seizures often and is under medical treatment. What should you do?
options: Request advanced life support (ALS) and law enforcement backup so you can transport the child.
Administer a dose of the child’s prescribed seizure-control medication.
Maintain ABCs, monitor vital signs, and transport the patient immediately.
Ensure a patent airway and request medical direction regarding transport.
questions.explanation: For patients who have routine seizures and whose condition returns to normal quickly after a seizure, you should request medical direction about whether to transport.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: All of the following are signs of possible child abuse EXCEPT
options: the presence of multiple bruises in various stages of healing.
a single, severe traumatic event that occurred for no reason.
injuries inconsistent with the mechanism described.
conflicting histories of the injury from the guardians/parents. –the emt-basic Practice exam 138
questions.explanation: Multiple injuries, conflicting stories of the cause, and repeated calls to the same address are characteristic of child abuse.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The head of a newborn infant has just delivered. You should
options: suction the baby’s mouth and nostrils with a bulb syringe.
push down on the baby’s upper shoulder to facilitate the rest of the delivery.
push up on the baby’s lower shoulder to facilitate the rest of the delivery.
ventilate the baby with a pediatric bag-valve mask and high-flow oxygen.
questions.explanation: Suctioning the baby’s mouth and nose will help to open the airway while the baby has not yet begun breathing. You should not force any part of the delivery process.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Emergency care for a responsive 7-year-old child with a foreign-body airway obstruction includes
options: holding the child on your knee and performing back blows.
standing behind the child and performing sub-diaphragmatic thrusts.
placing the child supine on the floor and attempting to see the obstruction.
placing the child supine on the floor and performing abdominal thrusts.
questions.explanation: Care for a responsive child consists of standing behind the child and attempting to relieve the obstruction with a series of subdiaphragmatic thrusts.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: A 2-year-old male is in respiratory failure when he has
options: altered mental status and breathing rate of 68 per minute.
limp muscle tone and weak or absent distal pulses.
nasal flaring and mottled skin color.
breathing rate of 6 per minute and heart rate of 50 per minute.
questions.explanation: Signs of respiratory failure include altered mental status and a slow or fast breathing rate with fatigue. There is not enough information to determine if patients b and c are in respiratory insufficiency, respiratory failure, or impending respiratory arrest. Patient d is in impending respiratory arrest.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A sign or symptom of a predelivery emergency is
options: the presence of a bloody show.
questions.explanation: Profuse vaginal bleeding may indicate a true obstetrical emergency such as uterine rupture or torn placenta.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Select the correct-size oral airway for a small child by measuring from the corner of the patient’s mouth to what structure?
questions.explanation: Select the correct-size oral airway for an infant or child by measuring from the corner of the patient’s mouth to the angle of the jaw.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Vitreous humor is found
options: behind the lens of the eye.
in the bone marrow of the upper arm.
in front of the lens of the eye.
in the joint lubrication of the upper arm.
questions.explanation: Vitreous humor is found behind the lens of the eye. It is the clear gel that fills the space between the lens and the retina. Aqueous humor is a thick, watery substance that fills the space between the lens and the cornea.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You are assisting with childbirth in the field. As the infant’s head is delivered, you discover that the umbilical cord is wrapped tightly around the neck. You should immediately
options: place the mother on her side and transport rapidly.
deliver the infant with the cord wrapped around its neck.
clamp the cord in two places and cut it between clamps.
suction the infant’s mouth and nose to clear secretions.
questions.explanation: If the cord is wrapped around the infant’s neck and you cannot easily loosen and remove it, you should clamp it in two places and cut the cord.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You have just assisted in delivering an infant with a pink body, a pulse rate of 106 per minute, and good muscle tone. The infant is crying lustily. How should you care for this newborn?
options: Wrap the newborn in clean towels and give her to the mother to hold during transport.
Provide positive pressure ventilations at the rate of 60 per minute with a bag-valve mask.
Monitor the infant for one minute and reassess vital signs to see if the heart rate increases.
Administer free-flow oxygen by holding an oxygen mask or tubing over the -newborn’s face. –the emt-basic Practice exam 139
questions.explanation: This newborn has a high Apgar score; there is no need for respiratory support unless the condition changes. You should always follow the steps in the inverted pyramid for neonatal resuscitation by drying and warming, positioning the head down for drainage, suctioning the airway and nose as needed, and providing tactile stimulation to stimulate breathing.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The presence of a bloody show during the first stage of labor is a sign that
options: the delivery of the infant is imminent.
the newborn is in danger of respiratory -distress.
the second stage of labor has begun.
questions.explanation: The presence of a bloody show (which is the expulsion of the mucous plug from the mouth of the cervix) occurs during the first stage of labor. It is normal and indicates that the cervix is beginning to open or dilate and may occur several hours prior to delivery. The second stage of labor continues until the baby is born.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient has experienced a spontaneous abortion or miscarriage. You should
options: remove any tissues from the vagina.
place a sanitary napkin in the vagina.
questions.explanation: There can be large blood loss suffered by the mother during a miscarriage. The EMTBasic should treat the patient for possible shock as well as provide emotional care.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the first treatment when a mother bleeds excessively from her vagina after delivery?
questions.explanation: More than 500 mL of blood loss after delivery is excessive; massage the mother’s abdomen by rubbing firmly in one direction from the symphasis pubis bone toward the umbilicus. This will help the uterus contract and stop the bleeding. Oxygen administration, shock treatment, and rapid transport will follow uterine massage.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Emergency care for an infant when meconium is present in the amniotic fluid includes
options: stimulating the infant to cough to expel the meconium.
performing bag-valve-mask ventilation to improve lung compliance.
performing back blows and chest thrusts to remove the meconium.
suctioning and notifying the hospital that meconium was present.
questions.explanation: If meconium is present in the amniotic fluid, suction the infant before stimulating it to cry, then notify the hospital of the presence of meconium. An ALS crew may be able to perform tracheal suctioning and intubate the infant. If the lungs are stiff and noncompliant, it may indicate that aspiration of the meconium has occurred. Aspiration pneumonia due to meconium is often fatal.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In addition to caring for injuries, emergency care for a rape victim should focus on which of the following?
options: performing a pelvic or rectal exam on the patient
collecting evidence of the rape and bagging it in plastic
allowing the patient to shower and change clothes
preserving evidence in a paper bag and -reassuring the victim
questions.explanation: In addition to providing routine emergency care, care for a rape victim should focus on preserving evidence and providing comfort and reassurance. Although you want to provide comfort to the patient, you should not allow him or her to shower or change clothes, as this will destroy evidence. Any clothing or personal effects that are removed from the patient should be placed in paper bags to prevent the growth of bacteria that might occur if stored in plastic bags. You should not need to examine the genital or rectal area unless you note significant bleeding.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The reason to position a pregnant woman on her left side is to
options: reduce the pressure of the fetus on maternal circulation.
make labor proceed more slowly by slowing down contractions.
help turn a breech fetus in the birth canal to the vertex position.
ensure that there is sufficient blood flow to the placenta.
questions.explanation: This condition, called supine hypotension syndrome, is the result of compression that the enlarged uterus causes on the vena cava of the maternal circulatory system. This corrective position, called the left lateral recumbent position, is accomplished by placing the mother on her left side with her legs bent slightly or kept straight. Maternal positional changes have no effect on the speed of labor or the position of the infant in the birth canal. By assisting in venous blood return in the mother, you will get the secondary effect of increasing blood flow to the uterus, but this is a secondary effect seen in correcting supine hypotension -syndrome.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The patient is a 29-year-old female pregnant with her second child. She is 39-weeks pregnant and saw a bloody show approximately 4 hours ago. Her contractions are 2 minutes apart and lasting 60 seconds. Transport time is approximately 45 minutes. You should
options: protect the airway and monitor vital signs while transporting.
prepare for an imminent on-scene delivery.
position the mother on her left side and begin transport.
notify dispatch of the need for ALS -assistance. –the emt-basic Practice exam 140
questions.explanation: The patient is likely to deliver imminently, so prepare for a normal delivery.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: When arriving at the scene of a possible hazardous materials incident, you would identify hazards by
options: scanning with binoculars from a safe distance.
assisting law enforcement officers in the search.
questions.explanation: Never enter a scene where hazardous materials are present until you have verified that the scene is safe. Use binoculars to survey the scene from a distance in order to identify hazardous materials placards. Consider victims and bystanders contaminated and take appropriate precautions.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: For which of these procedures should you wear gloves, gown, mask, and protective -eyewear?
questions.explanation: Bleeding control with spurting blood carries maximum danger of contamination, and maximum protection is therefore required. Airway suctioning or intubation does not generally require the use of a cover gown, and cleaning contaminated instruments does not generally require eye/facial protection unless there is danger of splashing.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following situations represents your abandonment of a patient?
options: You begin assessing a patient, but turn -responsibility for that patient over to a first responder.
You begin CPR on a cardiac arrest patient, but stop when the ALS team takes over care.
With the approval of medical direction, you do not transport a patient who feels fine after having a seizure.
You refuse to help a patient administer nitroglycerin that has been prescribed for someone else.
questions.explanation: Abandonment occurs when you relinquish care without a patient’s consent or without insuring that care is continued by someone of the same or higher level, such as a paramedic, ALS unit, or physician.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You are called to a store where a holdup has been committed. Police are already on the scene searching for the gunman. Through the store window, you see the store manager, who has been shot. You should
options: enter the store immediately to care for the manager.
leave immediately and seek cover a distance away.
wait until the police tell you it is safe to enter the scene.
request medical direction to determine if you can enter.
questions.explanation: EMTs should not enter a crime scene until it has been secured by police. As you travel to the scene, you should determine where it is most appropriate to park your vehicle. Generally, you want cover (protection from attack) and concealment (out of direct visual range) in your staging area.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the first thing you should do after receiving orders from the medical direction physician?
options: Repeat the orders exactly as you heard them.
Question anything you did not understand.
Document the orders in your report.
questions.explanation: To avoid misunderstanding, always repeat medical orders exactly as you heard them. Once you have done that, you can question any order you do not understand or about which you are unclear. When you complete your written patient-care report, you should include the order in your report.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your pregnant patient is experiencing -contractions. She feels like she needs to move her -bowels. This may indicate that
options: birth is still some time away.
the baby is still very high in the birth canal. –the emt-basic Practice exam 141
questions.explanation: The sensation of needing to move one’s bowels during labor is the result of the head pressing down on the anal sphincter as the baby passes through the birth canal. The head is very close to the opening of the birth canal, and delivery is imminent.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Which statement about a patient’s right to refuse care is correct?
options: A child who is old enough to understand danger is old enough to refuse care and transport.
An adult patient who is of sound mind and understands the consequences can refuse treatment.
No one can authorize treatment or transport for any other individual, regardless of his or her age.
EMTs should leave immediately whenever a patient says that he or she will refuse care.
questions.explanation: An adult of sound mind can refuse treatment, but the EMT should first make an effort to clearly explain the consequences; refusal of treatment should be documented in writing.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which situation requires that an emergency patient be moved?
options: Your patient has undergone cardiac arrest while seated in a chair.
Your patient is found on the ground, unresponsive, and alone.
Your patient is found in his bed, displaying early symptoms of shock.
Your patient is showing signs of inadequate breathing and shock.
questions.explanation: An emergency move is required in a situation where a patient is in immediate life-threatening danger. Emergency moves require only cervical spine stabilization (if it is a trauma situation) and should be performed quickly. Once the patient is in a safer location, you should begin with your initial assessment as you do in all patient situations.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Bacterial meningitis has an incubation period of
options: weeks to months, depending on the type.
questions.explanation: Bacterial meningitis has an incubation period of 2–10 days. Bacterial infections of the meninges are extremely serious illnesses, and may result in death or brain damage even if treated.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You can assess a pregnant woman’s uterine contractions by placing your gloved hand on
options: the right side of her abdomen.
the left side of her abdomen.
questions.explanation: The uterus is most easily felt just above the navel.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The purpose of incident management systems is to provide a(n)
options: clear chain of command in case of legal -liability.
means of evaluating the EMS system’s response to an event.
orderly method for communications and decision making.
questions.explanation: An incident management system is a coordinated system of procedures that allows for smooth operations at the scene of an -emergency.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: A 23-year-old pregnant female is bleeding -profusely from her vagina. All of the following actions are appropriate EXCEPT
options: placing a sanitary napkin in the vagina.
replacing pads as they become soaked.
questions.explanation: Placing napkins in the birth canal will not stop the source of bleeding. Placing bulky dressings or sanitary napkins at the vaginal opening will help prevent the blood from spreading.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In a multiple-casualty situation, which patient should be assigned the highest priority?
options: adequate breathing, responsive, suspected spine injury
inadequate breathing, responsive, suspected broken tibia
inadequate breathing, unresponsive, suspected internal bleeding
questions.explanation: Patients with breathing difficulties and serious bleeding receive the highest priority in a -multiple-casualty situation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is an 84-year-old female having difficulty breathing. Her daughter, age 45, is with her. When communicating with this patient, you should assume that she is
options: hard of hearing; speak extremely slowly and loudly.
competent and able to understand; speak respectfully.
confused; explain your treatment clearly to the daughter. –the emt-basic Practice exam 142
questions.explanation: Do not assume that an elderly patient is incompetent, deaf, or confused. Address all patients respectfully.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which statement about patient confidentiality is correct?
options: Patients who are cared for in a public place lose their right to confidentiality.
The right to confidentiality does not apply to minors or to wards of the state.
The patient who signs a statement releasing confidential information relinquishes all rights to privacy.
A patient must sign a written release before any confidential information can be disclosed.
questions.explanation: Patient information can be released only if the patient has signed a specific consent form.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Your patient, a 69-year-old male, is in cardiac arrest. His wife informs you that their physician has written a do not resuscitate order for the patient, but she does not have the written order. You should
options: provide all necessary care to save the patient’s life.
obey the do not resuscitate order and leave immediately.
leave right after documenting the wife’s -statement.
call the patient’s doctor and try to confirm the order.
questions.explanation: If you are informed of a do not resuscitate order but do not actually see it, you must still provide all necessary care.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 6-year-old female who fell off her bicycle. She has a suspected broken ankle, no respiratory compromise, and no suspected internal injuries. After providing necessary care at the scene, you are transporting the child and her father to the hospital. The father loudly insists that you use your siren and lights en route. You should
options: request medical direction in dealing with the father.
request permission from dispatch to use lights and siren.
refuse, because it may cause an unnecessary hazard.
comply, it will relax the father and comfort the patient.
questions.explanation: You are responsible for helping make your patient feel at ease, but you are also responsible for operating your ambulance in the safest possible way.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which patient’s vital signs are NOT within -normal limits?
questions.explanation: Normal values for a newborn are: pulse, 120–160; respirations, 40–60; BP, 80/40.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 45-year-old male is breathing at a rate of 32 times per minute with shallow respirations. He is altered, and his skin signs are cool, -cyanotic, and diaphoretic. You should
options: provide oxygen at 6 L/min using a nasal cannula.
provide oxygen at 12 L/min using a nonrebreather mask.
provide artificial ventilation with a gas-valve mask and high-flow oxygen.
place the patient into the left lateral “recovery” position.
questions.explanation: Based on the information provided, the patient is breathing too quickly to provide his own -adequate ventilations. This patient cannot be managed with a nonrebreather alone; artificial ventilations are needed to oxygenate the patient adequately.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You should assess skin color by examining an adult patient’s
questions.explanation: Assess skin color in an adult patient by examining the nailbeds, oral mucosa, and conjunctiva.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 45-year-old female is breathing at a rate of 22 times per minute with adequate tidal volume. She is alert, but her skin signs are cool, pale, and diaphoretic. You should
options: provide oxygen at 6 L/min using a nasal cannula.
provide oxygen at 12 L/min using a nonrebreather mask.
provide artificial ventilation with a gas-mask valve and high-flow oxygen.
place the patient into the left lateral “recovery” position.
questions.explanation: Based on the information provided, the patient is ventilating adequately but has poor skin signs, indicating inadequate perfusion. High-flow oxygen via a nonrebreather mask should ensure that the patient is fully oxygenated.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You would locate a patient’s carotid pulse by first finding the Adam’s apple and then
options: pressing hard on only one side of the patient’s neck.
placing one hand gently on each side of the neck.
pressing with your thumb on one side of the neck.
sliding two fingers toward one side of the neck.
questions.explanation: When assessing the carotid pulse, first locate the Adam’s apple and then slide two fingers toward one side of the neck; never exert strong pressure or assess the carotid pulse on both sides of the neck at the same time.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient’s pupils react unequally to light. You should suspect the presence of
questions.explanation: Head injury, eye injury, or drug use may cause the pupils to be nonreactive or unequally reactive. Shock, airway obstruction, and cardiac arrest will cause both pupils to dilate equally.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A 70-year-old female is complaining of shortness of breath. She has a history of emphysema. You should
options: withhold oxygen, since these patients do not respond to oxygen.
withhold oxygen, because you could eliminate the hypoxic drive.
administer oxygen, because in most cases, the hypoxic drive will not be a problem.
withhold oxygen, because these patients become apneic if they receive high-flow -oxygen. –the emt-basic Practice exam 258
questions.explanation: While it may be true that providing high levels of oxygen over prolonged time periods may cause the hypoxic drive to fail, it is a rare occurrence in the prehospital field. With the complaint of shortness of breath, it is better to ensure that the patient is fully oxygenated rather than taking a chance that she is hypoxic. The EMT-Basic is prepared to ventilate the patient if she goes into respiratory arrest due to the high levels of oxygen.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Ecchymosis refers to
options: a bad taste in the mouth.
questions.explanation: Blood under the skin, appearing on the surface as a bruise, is also known as ecchymosis.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: In which situation should you determine the patient’s blood pressure through palpation?
options: Your patient is under 1 year old.
The setting is unusually quiet, such as a private home.
Your patient’s pulse is very weak and difficult to hear.
Your patient cannot tolerate pressure to the cartoid artery.
questions.explanation: Use palpation only when it is difficult to hear the pulse, either because the setting is extremely noisy or because the patient’s pulse is very weak.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The correct way to select the proper size oropharyngeal airway (OPA) is to measure the distance from the
options: corner of the mouth to the tip of the earlobe.
nose to the tip of the earlobe.
corner of the mouth to the nose.
nose to the tip of the chin.
questions.explanation: The other methods will provide an either too large or too small measurement of an OPA.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which patient should receive only a focused physical exam and SAMPLE history?
options: 46-year-old male, unresponsive after falling from a 10-meter scaffold
80-year-old male, responsive to painful stimuli after being hit by a car
5-year-old female, responsive and in pain after falling from a standing position
16-year-old female, responsive to verbal stimuli after a gunshot wound
questions.explanation: You can omit the detailed physical exam if your patient shows no alterations in consciousness and if the mechanism of injury does not suggest high risk of trauma.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 19-year-old male who has been in a motorcycle accident and shows deformity and bleeding to the left leg. You would NOT perform a detailed physical exam if the patient
options: said that he was feeling fine except for his leg.
began to spurt blood from an open wound.
was in a great deal of pain and discomfort.
showed a weak pulse or other signs of shock.
questions.explanation: Do not perform a detailed physical exam if, as in the case of suspected arterial bleeding, your time should be devoted to caring for the airway, breathing, or circulation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Continuous monitoring of a patient’s mental status is best accomplished by
options: repeatedly asking the patient’s name and address.
continuously monitoring the patient’s vital signs.
questions.explanation: The best way to monitor the patient’s mental status is to interact with the patient so that you are immediately aware of any changes.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You should assess the brachial pulse in patients who
options: have a history of cardiac problems.
have a pulse rate less than 60/min.
questions.explanation: Assess the brachial pulse instead of the radial or carotid in patients less than 1 year old.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which patient is showing early signs of shock (decreased tissue perfusion)?
options: 23-year-old female: pulse, 104; respiration, 25/min; BP, 118/78; cool, clammy skin
45-year-old female: pulse, 68; respiration, 20/min; BP, 110/72; warm, moist skin
5-year-old male: pulse, 110; respiration, 22/min; BP, 88/52; cool, dry skin
60-year-old male: pulse, 76; respiration, 10/min; BP, 96/60; hot, dry skin –the emt-basic Practice exam 259
questions.explanation: Patient a, with elevated pulse and respiratory rate and cool, clammy skin is showing early signs of shock. Patients b and c have normal vital signs. Patient d has a normal pulse, low blood pressure, and slow respiratory rate but hot and dry skin, so shock is not clearly evident in this patient.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is the most important sign of a diabetic emergency?
questions.explanation: The most important sign of a diabetic emergency is altered mental status; patients may appear intoxicated or act anxious or combative. Patients who present an altered level of consciousness should have an evaluation for -diabetic emergency.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Of the following diseases, which poses the most risk to EMTs?
questions.explanation: While many EMS workers are understandably concerned about HIV/AIDS, it presents far less risk than hepatitis or TB because the virus does not survive well outside the human body.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Your patient is a 45-year-old homeless male who is suspected of having hypothermia. What is the most important question to ask him?
options: Do you know who you are and where you are?
Are you having any trouble breathing?
Are you currently taking any prescription medications?
What makes the pain feel better or worse?
questions.explanation: Assessing the patient’s level of consciousness will help you find out the stage of hypothermia and what treatment the patient needs.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The zygomatic bones are found in
questions.explanation: The zygomatic bones are found in the face. They are also known as cheekbones.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is the most common cause of airway obstruction in an unconscious patient?
questions.explanation: Although all the choices can cause airway obstruction, the tongue is the most common cause, especially in unconscious patients.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: All of the following are reasons that infants and children are prone to respiratory difficulties EXCEPT that they
options: use their diaphragm rather than their intercostal muscles.
questions.explanation: Infants and children are prone to breathing difficulties because they have small air passages that are easily occluded; they also rely heavily on their diaphragms and suffer frequent respiratory infections.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a newborn. You should consider the possibility of breathing difficulty if the -respiratory rate is
questions.explanation: The normal respiratory rate for an infant is 40–60/min.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: When assessing your patient’s airway, you hear snoring sounds. You should suspect that
options: there is fluid in the airway.
the tongue is blocking the airway.
questions.explanation: Snoring indicates that the tongue has relaxed into the upper airway, partially obstructing it.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: How many vertebrae are there in the human spinal column?
questions.explanation: There are 33 vertebrae in the human spinal column.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You hear gurgling in your patient’s airway. You should immediately
options: insert an oropharyngeal airway. –the emt-basic Practice exam 260
questions.explanation: Gurgling is a sign that fluid is present in the airway; the correct procedure is to immediately open and suction the airway. An airway adjunct may be used to assist in maintaining airway patency once it is cleared.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: According to the American Heart Association, at least ______________ of air should be delivered to the patient when using an adult bag-valve mask.
questions.explanation: According to the American Heart Association, at least 800 mL of air should be delivered to the patient when using an adult bag-valve mask.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The correct way to select the size of a nasopharyngeal airway is to measure
options: from the corner of the mouth to the angle of the jaw.
from the earlobe to the corner of the jaw.
the diameter of the patient’s little finger.
from the chin to the Adam’s apple.
questions.explanation: Measure the diameter of the patient’s little finger. An alternative method is to measure from the tip of the nose to the tip of the ear. The other choices will produce too small or too large of a measurement.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In what position should you place a child’s head for ventilation?
questions.explanation: A child’s head should be slightly hyperextended for ventilation in a position called the sniffing position. For infants, the correct position is the neutral position with padding placed below the shoulders and upper back. You should never flex the airway forward as this will close off the airway. The recovery position is used for spontaneously breathing patients to protect their -airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient has a foreign-body airway obstruction. Three attempts to clear the airway have been unsuccessful. You should
options: transport and attempt to clear the airway en route.
call for an ALS unit as backup.
carry out three additional cycles before requesting help.
questions.explanation: If you are unsuccessful at clearing the airway after three attempts, transport immediately, continuing your attempts en route.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: After obtaining medical direction, you are helping your patient use a prescribed inhaler. You should tell the patient to
options: inhale deeply and hold her breath.
exhale as slowly as she can.
questions.explanation: The patient should inhale deeply and hold her breath to absorb the medicine.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient, a 78-year-old male, has no pulse and agonal respirations. You should
options: administer high-flow oxygen via bagvalve mask.
transport immediately to the closest medical facility.
request the patient’s permission to administer nitro-glycerin.
questions.explanation: Agonal respirations are a sign that the patient is nearing death. Because he is also pulseless, begin CPR immediately.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You have assisted a patient in administering a prescribed inhaler. After one dose of the medication, the patient’s pulse rate increases, and he reports feeling nauseated. You should
options: administer another dose of the medication.
assess respiratory rate, rhythm, and quality.
document and report the signs and -symptoms.
questions.explanation: Document all side effects of medication administration and report them to the receiving facility; besides increased pulse rate and nausea, other common side effects are tremors and nervousness.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You should remove your patient’s dentures in order to provide ventilation when
options: it is necessary to insert an oral airway.
questions.explanation: Because dentures can make it easier to obtain a good mask seal, you should remove them only if they become dislodged.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: During the management of a cardiac arrest, the AED gives a “no shock indicated” message. Which of the following statements will most likely prompt this condition?
options: The patient is in ventricular tachycardia.
The patient is in ventricular fibrillation. –the emt-basic Practice exam 261
questions.explanation: While b may seem correct, an AED cannot detect a pulse. It can only detect an organized rhythm that may produce a pulse.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which patient is breathing adequately?
options: 3-month-old male: respiratory rate, 62/min, using diaphragm and muscles in chest and neck
7-year-old female: respiratory rate, 12/min, irregular rhythm, using diaphragm primarily
18-year-old male: respiratory rate, 28/min, shallow chest motions
43-year-old female: respiratory rate, 15/min, regular chest motions
questions.explanation: The normal respiratory rate for an adult is 12–20 breaths per minute, and chest-wall motion should be regular and neither shallow nor very deep. Patient a is breathing too quickly for a 3-month-old (it should be around 40 at this age), and the use of accessory muscles in the neck shows some level of distress is present. Patient b has a slower than normal rate (it should be around 20), and the irregularity and diaphragmatic nature suggest a spinal cord injury may be present. Patient c is breathing much too fast (rate 12–20 for adults), and the shallow chest motions suggests insufficiency or distress.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: All of the following are signs of difficult breathing EXCEPT
questions.explanation: Abdominal breathing, not abdominal pain, may be a sign of a breathing difficulty.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You have delivered three shocks with the SAED. Your cardiac arrest patient has shallow, agonal respirations with a pulse. What should you do next?
options: Deliver second shock to assure patient does not arrest again.
Provide artificial ventilation with high-concentration oxygen.
Give high-concentration oxygen by nonrebreather mask.
Check pulse and deliver two more shocks.
questions.explanation: A nonrebreather mask will not be adequate to provide ventilations. Additional electrical shocks may actually send the patient back into a pulseless rhythm.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A danger of using a rigid suction catheter with infants and young children is that stimulating the back of the throat can
options: cause changes in the heart rhythm.
cause the tongue to fall into the airway.
questions.explanation: When using a rigid catheter to suction infants and small children, take care not to touch the back of the throat, since stimulation here can cause bradycardia due to stimulation of the vagus nerve. Touching the back of the throat or around the base of the tongue in any patient can trigger a gag, which could lead to vomiting, but the chance of this is not any greater in pediatric patients. The tongue cannot “fall into the airway”; however, in the absence of proper positioning, a flaccid tongue can relax into a position that could lead to obstruction of the airway.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: It is permissible to use the head-tilt/chin-lift on a trauma patient only if
options: there is no other way to open the airway.
a third EMT is available to stabilize the spine.
you have already performed CPR for 15 -minutes.
questions.explanation: Use the head-tilt/chin-lift on a suspected trauma patient only if you cannot open the airway by any other means.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The function of the white blood cells is to
questions.explanation: The white blood cells, which make up a part of the body’s immune system, fight infections.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Angina differs from a heart attack because in an attack of angina, the
options: pain radiates outward from the heart.
administration of nitroglycerin provides no relief.
heart muscle is not permanently damaged.
questions.explanation: In angina, unlike a heart attack, the reduced blood flow to the heart does not result in permanent damage.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The following patients all have signs and symptoms of cardiac chest pain and have their own prescriptions for nitroglycerin. Which patient should you NOT assist with taking nitroglycerin?
options: 51-year-old female: pulse, 72; -respirations, 14/min; BP, 130/80 –the emt-basic Practice exam 262
questions.explanation: Do not administer nitroglycerin if the patient’s systolic blood pressure is below 100 mm Hg.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: When deciding whether to assist a patient in administering nitroglycerin, you should check the medicine for the patient’s name, the route of administration, the dose, and the
questions.explanation: Before assisting a patient to administer nitroglycerin, check for the right patient, the right route of administration, the right dose, and the expiration date.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient, a 67-year-old male with a history of cardiac disease, is unresponsive. After checking ABCs and finding no pulse, you begin CPR. The next thing you should do is
questions.explanation: After verifying that there is no pulse and opening the airway, your first priority is to attach the AED and determine if defibrillation is required.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is showing signs and symptoms of shock and has a tender abdomen. She reports vomiting material that “looked like coffee grounds.” You should suspect
questions.explanation: “Coffee-grounds” vomit is digested blood and indicates the presence of internal bleeding, as do abdominal tenderness and signs and symptoms of shock. A ruptured appendix and fractured pelvis will not cause bleeding into the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. Ingested poisoning may or may not result in GI bleeding, but inhaled poisoning will result in respiratory problems.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which patient is showing signs and symptoms of cardiac compromise?
options: 85-year-old male: difficulty breathing, high fever, rapid pulse
72-year-old female: wheezing, labored breathing, tightness in throat
53-year-old female: dull chest pain, sudden sweating, difficulty breathing
51-year-old male: headache, dizziness, gagging, chest pain
questions.explanation: This woman shows classic signs of cardiac compromise: dull chest pain, sudden onset of sweating, and difficulty breathing.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which blood vessel carries oxygen-poor blood to the heart?
questions.explanation: The vena cava carries oxygen-poor blood from the body to the right atrium, so it can be transported to the right ventricle and from there to the lungs.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Central pulses may be palpated at the
questions.explanation: Central pulses may be palpated at the carotid artery in the neck and at the femoral artery in the groin. The brachial and radial pulses are peripheral pulses.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The diastolic blood pressure represents the pressure in the brachial artery when the
questions.explanation: The diastolic blood pressure represents the pressure in the brachial artery when the ventricles are at rest (diastole).
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Stridor is a sign of
questions.explanation: Stridor, a harsh sound usually heard during inspiration, is a sign of upper-airway -obstruction.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is complaining of chest pain. Which question would you ask to assess the O part of the OPQRST algorithm?
options: What were you doing when the pain started?
What does the pain feel like?
How long ago did the pain begin?
How bad is the pain now? –the emt-basic Practice exam 263
questions.explanation: Assess onset by asking when the pain began and how long it took to reach its greatest severity.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: All of the following are contraindictions for the administration of nitroglycerin EXCEPT when the patient
options: has a systolic blood pressure of less than 100 mm Hg.
has taken a previous dose of nitroglycerin two minutes ago.
has a heart rate less than 60 beats per minute.
questions.explanation: A patient may take up to three doses of nitroglycerin, each dose three to five minutes apart. The blood pressure should be greater than 100, and the pulse rate should be greater than 60. Nitroglycerine use is contraindicated in children.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient is in greater danger of severe internal bleeding from fracturing which bone?
questions.explanation: Pelvic fractures carry danger of severe internal bleeding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Why is an infant more likely to suffer an airway obstruction than an adult?
options: An infant’s ribs are less flexible than an adult’s.
The shape of the infant’s head will cause the neck to flex when the child is supine.
The adult has a relatively larger tongue -compared to an infant.
The adult has a relatively smaller airway -compared to the infant.
questions.explanation: The back of an infant’s head (occiput) is relatively larger compared with an adult’s. Placing the infant on his or her back, without shoulder padding, may cause the head to tilt forward excessively, closing the airway.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Which patient would be most likely to have a barrel chest?
options: 10-month-old male: premature birth and -history of respiratory problems
6-year-old female: history of asthma and -frequent respiratory infections
58-year-old male: history of emphysema and years of smoking
70-year-old female: recent history of -pneumonia and bronchitis
questions.explanation: A barrel chest is associated with a long history of respiratory disease, such as bronchitis, emphysema, or COPD. Because the alveoli are not functioning properly, air trapping occurs in the lungs. The increased effort it takes to move air in and out of the lungs results in an overdevelopment of the chest muscles and in time, leads to the barrelshaped appearance.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You have received medical control permission to apply the PASG to your patient who has severe bleeding caused by soft-tissue damage, below the knee on both legs. Which compartments of the PASG should you inflate?
questions.explanation: To stop bleeding caused by massive softtissue injury, inflate only the compartments that cover the damaged areas.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The primary reason you auscultate both sides of the chest is to determine whether breath sounds are
questions.explanation: You auscultate (listen with a stethoscope) through the chest wall to determine if breath sounds are present and equal on both sides of the chest. As you are listening, you may also note the relative rate and quality of breathing, but those are secondary reasons for listening to the chest wall. If you are having trouble determining the respiratory rate when you are performing vital signs, you can listen to one side of the chest wall to determine the respiratory rate.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You should elevate a patient’s leg to control bleeding only if
options: medical direction has refused permission to apply PASGs.
no other method successfully stops the -bleeding.
there are no signs or symptoms of skeletal injury.
questions.explanation: Elevate the extremity to control bleeding only after checking for signs and symptoms of skeletal injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 24-year-old female with a history of asthma. She is wheezing and gasping for air and has a pulse rate of 88/min. You may assist her in using an inhaler if
options: she has not yet taken more than three doses of medication.
she has her own inhaler and you obtain medical direction.
her respiratory rate is greater than 24/min.
her blood pressure is greater than 100/70. –the emt-basic Practice exam 264
questions.explanation: An EMT-Basic may help a patient in respiratory difficulty to administer an inhaler if the inhaler was prescribed for that patient and medical direction is obtained. In some jurisdictions, you may have standing orders to assist the patient, which means medical direction is provided ahead of time in the standing order. It is important for you to determine how many doses she has already taken (choice a), but you must first determine if the medication she took was prescribed to her before you can assist her further. The standard dosing regimen for an EMT-Basic assisting with a prescribed inhaler is to provide a total of two doses, one every three minutes.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: After assisting a patient to administer nitroglycerin, you should
options: place the patient in Trendelenburg position.
give a second dose two minutes later.
reassess vital signs and chest pain.
questions.explanation: After helping a patient take prescribed nitroglycerin, reassess vital signs and chest pains; a second dose may be given three to five minutes later.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The AED is used to detect
options: the patient’s pulse rate and rhythm.
the contraction force of the heart.
questions.explanation: The AED can detect only the electrical activity within the patient’s heart. It does not assess rate, mechanical activity (pumping action), or the degree of cardiac compromise.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient, the victim of a car accident, has an obvious injury to her right leg. You should splint the injury before moving her unless
options: transport time is less than 15 minutes.
the patient is in severe pain.
bones are protruding through the skin.
