questions.explanation: If life-threatening conditions are present, you should focus on those injuries or begin to package the patient for rapid transport if you cannot manage her life-threatening problems.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your 18-month-old patient is experiencing respiratory distress. Which of the following conditions is NOT a likely cause of the difficulty in breathing?
questions.explanation: COPD is unlikely to occur in pediatric patients. The other three choices may cause some level of respiratory distress in the pediatric patient.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A seesaw (chest and abdomen move in opposite directions) pattern of breathing is a sign of
questions.explanation: A seesaw pattern is a sign of breathing difficulty in infants.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which mechanism of injury suggests the likelihood of a spinal injury?
options: restrained patient in a motor vehicle crash
stabbing penetration to the upper torso
gunshot wound to the upper thigh
questions.explanation: A patient who sustains injuries in a motor vehicle crash should be suspected of spinal injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A sign of early respiratory distress in the pediatric patient is
questions.explanation: As the body tries to compensate for a decrease in oxygen levels in the blood stream, the heart will try to beat more quickly to circulate blood to the cells more quickly. The skin will also turn pale and cool as the body shifts blood flow back to the critical organs. The patient will also breathe more quickly to draw in more oxygen and expel more carbon dioxide.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 33-year-old female with a suspected spinal cord injury. After you have immobilized her to a long board, she vomits. What should you do?
options: Ask her what she last ate.
Remove the board and suction the airway.
Tilt the board to clear the airway.
questions.explanation: If a patient is immobilized to a long board, you can tilt the entire board to clear the airway.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 28-year-old male who appears intoxicated. Bystanders report that the man seemed fine but suddenly began “acting strange.” You should first suspect
questions.explanation: Sudden onset of altered mental status strongly suggests diabetic emergency. Alcohol intoxication will have a slower onset. Poisoning may also cause altered mental status, but you should first rule out the possibility of a diabetic emergency as it is a more common occurrence. An allergic reaction will not cause the sudden onset of altered mental status.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which patient’s vital signs are within normal range?
options: 28-year-old female: pulse, 52; respirations, 9/min; BP, 80/40 –the emt-basic Practice exam 265
questions.explanation: The values for the 5-year-old are within normal ranges; those for the newborn and the adult are all abnormally low, while those for the 15-year-old are too high.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is a 23-year-old female who calmly tells you that her thoughts are controlling the weather. Her body language and speech are nonthreatening and gentle. You should
options: talk quietly to this patient and keep her calm.
request permission to restrain the patient.
questions.explanation: Talk quietly with the patient to help her remain calm and persuade her to seek medical help. Be on guard for a violent outburst. Restraints and police backup are not necessary to manage this patient. Asking too many personal questions may agitate her.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A 17-year-old male patient is experiencing difficulty breathing and abdominal pain after being struck with a bat in his left lower quadrant. He is alert, cool, and diaphoretic, with a tachycardic heart rate. You should provide oxygen using a
questions.explanation: This patient may be experiencing internal bleeding and possible signs of shock. Highflow oxygen is warranted.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The last vital sign to change in a patient going into shock is
questions.explanation: In shock, the body attempts to preserve perfusion by shunting blood away from the skin, increasing heart rate and increasing respiratory rate.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The central nervous system consists of the brain and the
questions.explanation: The central nervous system consists of the brain and the spinal cord.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Your male patient has climbed out of his car unassisted after a car crash, but he is now complaining of back pain. You should
options: transport him in whatever position is the most comfortable.
immobilize him to a long spine board with a standing take-down.
immobilize him to a short spine board in the sitting position.
immobilize him with a Kendrick Extrication Device.
questions.explanation: Even if the patient has extricated himself from the car, immobilize him to a long spine board if the mechanisms of injury lead you to suspect spine damage. A standing takedown will allow you to immobilize the patient from the standing position.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 37-year-old female who has been in a minor accident. The patient is alert and oriented, and baseline vital signs are pulse, 76 and regular; respirations, 16/min and unlabored; and BP, 118/78. When you check her vital signs 15 minutes later, you find that her pulse rate is now 92 and her respirations are 24/min and -shallow. You should
options: continue to reassess her every 15 minutes.
call for medical direction to treat her -dyspnea.
treat for shock and reassess every five -minutes.
questions.explanation: The patient’s condition has become unstable; you should treat her for shock and reassess every five minutes. She should already be on oxygen. Alert medical control of your findings, but permission is not needed to treat this patient for shock. Because she is unstable, 15 minutes is too long a time period to go between vital signs assessments.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following signs or symptoms might you expect to see in a patient suffering from hypothermia?
options: burning or itching in the underarms –the emt-basic Practice exam 266
questions.explanation: The other choices do not make sense as potential signs or symptoms of hypothermia. As blood flow to the brain diminishes, altered mental status may occur, causing confusion and eventually unconsciousness.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: With medical direction, you may administer epinephrine from a patient’s own autoinjector if the patient displays signs and symptoms of respiratory distress or
questions.explanation: Indications for use of epinephrine are signs and symptoms of respiratory distress or hypoperfusion (shock). Glucose is indicated for diabetic patients. There are no medications (other than oxygen) indicated for use by EMTs in the treatment of cardiac arrest patients.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which set of vital signs suggests early -hypothermia?
options: pulse, 56 and faint; respirations, 9/min, shallow; BP, 96/60; cyanotic; sluggish pupils
pulse, 74; respirations, 16/min, strong; BP, 124/80; cool, dry skin; reactive pupils
pulse, 92; respirations, 26/min; BP, 118/76, flushed (red) skin; reactive pupils
pulse, 68; respirations, 13/min, irregular; BP, 110/70; hot, moist skin; reactive pupils
questions.explanation: Patients with early hypothermia have a rapid pulse and respiratory rate and red skin; patient a has the signs of late hypothermia. Patient d has hyperthermia. Patient b has normal vital signs.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 2-year-old female is in severe respiratory distress. Her skin is mottled, and she does not respond to verbal or physical stimulus. You should
options: administer oxygen by the blow-by method.
assist her ventilations with a bag-valve mask and supplemental oxygen.
administer blind finger sweeps to attempt removal of an obstruction.
provide oxygen by pediatric nonrebreather mask.
questions.explanation: This patient is in late stages of respiratory distress. Simply providing oxygen without ventilation will be inadequate for this child’s needs.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the goal of emergency care for a hypothermic patient with a reduced level of consciousness?
questions.explanation: The most important principle of care for patients with severe hypothermia is to prevent further heat loss by removing the patient from the cold environment; active rewarming should be done in the hospital.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient, a 26-year-old male, has a suspected broken ankle sustained while playing tennis. His vital signs are pulse, 58; respirations, 12; BP, 108/70; pupils normal and reactive; skin warm and dry. After recording these vital signs, you should
options: ask the patient what his normal pulse and blood pressure are.
treat for signs and symptoms of shock.
administer oxygen and prepare to transport immediately.
recheck pulse, respiration, and blood pressure every five minutes.
questions.explanation: You should always ask a conscious patient his normal blood pressure and pulse rate because even though this patient’s pulse rate and blood pressure are low, they may be normal for him; also, no other signs or symptoms of shock are present.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What does the presence of abdominal breathing signify in infants and small children?
questions.explanation: In infants and children, who rely heavily on the diaphragm for breathing, abdominal breathing is normal in the absence of other signs of labored breathing, such as supraclavicular and intercostal retractions.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: While providing artificial ventilation to a 14-year-old near-drowning patient, you feel resistance in the airway. The possible cause of the resistance is that
options: there is water in the stomach.
the patient has chronic obstructive -pulmonary disease.
the epiglottis is swollen, causing an -obstruction.
questions.explanation: Water or air in the stomach caused by involuntary swallowing during a drowning episode may cause the stomach to expand and press against the diaphragm, making it difficult to ventilate the lungs.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The correct rate for providing artificial ventilations to infants and children is
options: 24 breaths per minute. –the emt-basic Practice exam 267
questions.explanation: The correct rate of providing artificial ventilations to infants and children is 20 breaths per minute, or one breath every three seconds.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Care for an unresponsive infant with a complete airway obstruction includes
options: performing back blows and ventilation attempts.
performing continuous chest thrusts until clear.
giving back blows, chest thrusts, and -ventilation.
questions.explanation: Care for an unresponsive infant with a -foreign-body airway obstruction includes a series of back blows, followed by chest thrusts, alternating with ventilation attempts.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: It is important to recognize signs of early respiratory distress in a child. A sign of early respiratory distress includes
questions.explanation: Decreased heart rate and altered mental status are considered late signs of respiratory distress and most likely are really signs of respiratory failure. A breathing rate of 22 can be normal for a child.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A common side effect of high fever in infants and small children is
questions.explanation: Seizures, seen as body stiffness and/or shaking, are a common side effect of high fevers in infants and small children.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is an 8-month-old infant with a recent history of vomiting and diarrhea. Which signs should alert you to the possibility of shock?
options: dry diaper and the absence of tears while crying
capillary refill time of two seconds or less
strong peripheral pulses; heart rate of 100
skin that is flushed and hot to the touch
questions.explanation: A dry diaper and absence of tears when the infant cries are signs of dehydration, a cause of hypovolemic shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: An 11-month-old crying female has swallowed a piece of a hot dog. She is coughing, and you can hear high-pitched sounds coming from the throat. You should
options: administer back blows and chest thrusts.
perform a blind finger sweep to attempt removal of the object.
provide high-concentration oxygen by blow-by mask.
provide ventilations by bag-valve mask and oxygen.
questions.explanation: The patient is not experiencing a foreignbody airway obstruction, and she appears to be ventilating adequately. Therefore, oxygen provided with a blow-by mask will help ensure adequate oxygenation of the patient.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 26-year-old male who has been in a motor vehicle accident. The patient’s radial pulse is weak, while the carotid pulse is strong. You should
options: treat for signs and symptoms of shock.
recheck by taking the brachial pulse.
wait 15 minutes, then recheck vital signs.
check for low blood pressure on the other arm.
questions.explanation: Suspect shock (decreased tissue perfusion) whenever the distal pulse is weaker than the central pulse. Low blood pressure is a late sign of shock; do not wait to treat the patient for shock until the blood pressure drops.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The sniffing position refers to the
options: way children position themselves when feeling respiratory distress.
recovery position used for children in -respiratory distress.
position used to insert the oropharyngeal or nasopharyngeal airway.
placement of a child’s head for the head-lift/chin-tilt maneuver.
questions.explanation: The sniffing position refers to the placement of a child’s head when you perform the head-tilt/chin-lift, with the face lying parallel to the surface he or she is lying on. It improves breathing by opening the airway further than hyperextending does.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Diastolic pressure is a measure of the
options: force exerted against the walls of the blood vessels when the heart contracts.
force exerted against the walls of the blood vessels when the heart relaxes.
rhythm and strength of the heart’s contractions during arterial circulation.
time it takes the capillary beds in the extremities to refill after being blanched. –the emt-basic Practice exam 268
questions.explanation: Diastolic pressure is a measure of the force exerted against the walls of the blood vessels when the heart muscle relaxes; it is thus the lower of the two numbers that make up a blood pressure reading. Choice d describes the capillary refill test. The force of arterial circulation is not assessed in the prehospital setting as it requires very invasive procedures.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 5-year-old male is experiencing severe respiratory distress. He is altered with poor skin signs. You should
options: assist ventilations with a pediatric bag-valve mask and supplemental oxygen.
perform blind finger sweeps to attempt to remove an obstruction.
provide oxygen by pediatric nonrebreather mask.
provide oxygen by the blow-by method.
questions.explanation: With the patient being altered in his mental status, it appears that he is not ventilating adequately. A nonrebreather mask will not be able to ventilate the child appropriately. There is no information to indicate a foreign-body obstruction.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Fontanels are the
options: strong contractions that signal the end of labor.
soft spots located on the infant’s head.
blood vessels in the umbilical cord.
special forceps doctors use to assist the -delivery.
questions.explanation: The fontanels are the soft spots on the baby’s head where the bony parts of the skull have not yet grown together, allowing the head to contract somewhat during delivery.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Where does blood travel when it leaves the right ventricle of the heart?
options: to the pulmonary veins, then to the lungs
to the aorta, then out to the body
to the vena cava, then to the left atrium
to the pulmonary arteries, then to the lungs
questions.explanation: Oxygen-poor blood arrives in the right atrium and is pumped out through the right ventricle via the pulmonary arteries to the lungs.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The third stage of labor consists of the
questions.explanation: The third stage of labor consists of the delivery of the placenta after the baby is delivered.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You have just assisted in the delivery of a newborn who has good color, a strong pulse, and is not yet breathing. You should
questions.explanation: To stimulate a healthy newborn to breathe, rub his or her back, or flick the soles of his or her feet.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Amniotic fluid with a yellow or brownish color means a high likelihood of
questions.explanation: Meconium, or fetal stool, appears as yellow, brown, or green material in the amniotic fluid. When present, it is associated with an increased risk of fetal distress.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is a 68-year-old male who is complaining of chest pain. Your focused assessment findings include a pulse rate of 92, a BP of 140/90, and some difficulty breathing. After administering oxygen, you should focus your questioning to determine if the patient has a history of
questions.explanation: For a patient such as this one who is displaying signs and symptoms of cardiac compromise, use the SAMPLE survey to focus your questioning to determine if there is any past history of cardiac disease and if the patient has a prescription for nitroglycerin.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 24-year-old female who tells you that she is three months pregnant. She is experiencing profuse vaginal bleeding and severe cramping. After administering oxygen, you should
options: be alert for signs and symptoms of shock, and transport quickly.
prepare for an emergency on-scene delivery of a premature infant.
attempt to stop the bleeding before transporting the patient.
place the patient on her left side and call for an ALS unit.
questions.explanation: The patient is likely to be experiencing a miscarriage; shock is possible. If she delivers, the child will be too small to survive. There is no method to stop the bleeding in the prehospital setting. There is no need to place her in the left lateral recumbent position.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is a 28-year-old pregnant female who has just been in a minor car accident. Which set of vital signs would be normal for this patient?
options: pulse, 82; respirations, 22; BP, 140/96 –the emt-basic Practice exam 269
questions.explanation: It is normal for a pregnant woman to have a slightly increased pulse and decreased blood pressure. The respiratory rate of patient b is too fast. Patient c has a too slow respiratory rate, and patient d is hypertensive and has a respiratory rate that is too high.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following signs and symptoms may indicate shock in children?
questions.explanation: Blood is shunted away from the skin early in children with shock, causing it to pale, become cool, and sweat.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: As an EMT-Basic, you are acting as a patient advocate when you
options: treat patients with dignity and respect.
consult with medical direction in the field.
questions.explanation: Acting as a patient advocate means treating all patients as you would like to be treated yourself.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What are the earliest signs of shock?
options: weak pulse and low blood pressure
questions.explanation: The earliest signs of shock are subtle changes in mental status, such as anxiety and restlessness, and tachycardia. Fatigue and depression are not common signs of shock. Cyanosis is not one of the earliest signs, and shallow respirations can occur any time during the shock process. Low blood pressure is a late sign of shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The following are all effective techniques for stress reduction EXCEPT
questions.explanation: Effective stress-reduction techniques include balancing work and recreation, getting enough rest, eating a healthy diet, getting regular exercise, and seeking help if necessary.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: It is necessary to wear a mask and eye -protection when
questions.explanation: Wear a mask and eye protection when there is a high probability of splattering, such as when suctioning a patient.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which statement about disposable gloves is -correct?
options: You should remove gloves by grasping the ends of the fingers and pulling them off right side out.
It is not necessary to wear gloves when -suctioning or ventilating a patient with a bag-valve-mask device.
Gloves protect both you and the patient from the transmission of infectious diseases.
One pair of gloves is sufficient for any call, no matter how many patients there are.
questions.explanation: Because gloves protect both you and your patients, most protocols now call for EMTs to wear gloves for any patient contact. Remove gloves by pulling them off inside out, so you do not touch the soiled outer surface; change gloves for each new patient contact.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which of the following situations illustrates implied consent?
options: You splint the broken arm and leg of a 6-yearold female with her mother’s permission.
You care for a cardiac patient who asks you to help him take a dose of nitroglycerin.
You arrive at the scene of a car crash, and the injured driver says, “Please help my child first.”
You provide life support to a man who was found unconscious by bystanders who called EMS.
questions.explanation: Implied consent means that, because your adult patient cannot give consent to treatment, you act without it.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Placing a patient in the recovery position allows
options: the patient to breathe more deeply.
the EMT to position the airway.
the EMT to provide basic life support.
questions.explanation: The recovery position, with the patient on the left side with the left arm under the head, allows secretions to drain more easily from the airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: During the scene size-up, you should determine whether the scene is safe to enter and whether
options: the patient is sick or a trauma victim. –the emt-basic Practice exam 270
questions.explanation: During scene size-up, determine whether the scene is safe for you to enter, the number of patients, if you have the appropriate BSI equipment and precautions, and whether you will need backup from additional EMS units or other services.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT part of the -standard medical report you give to the -receiving facility?
questions.explanation: The EMT’s report should not include a diagnosis, but rather a complete description of the patient’s condition.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You are splinting the injured leg of a 5-year-old male. What should you say?
options: “It is necessary to immobilize the extremity to prevent further injury.”
“After I strap your leg to this board, it won’t hurt so much.”
“Stop crying! I can’t work when you’re making loud noises.”
“Mom, if your child stops crying, I’ll explain what I’m doing.”
questions.explanation: Always speak directly to the patient, and explain what you are doing in words he or she can understand. If the parent is present, explain your treatment to him or her as well.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Why is it important to maintain body heat in a patient with shock?
options: Shivering wastes vital oxygen and energy.
Patients in shock often have fever as well.
Blood congeals more easily when the body is cold.
Hypothermia may increase the severity of shock.
questions.explanation: Blood carries heat, and a loss of blood will result in a drop in body temperature. Shivering, the body’s response to cold, increases metabolism by wasting oxygen and energy that would be better spent in fueling hypoxic cells.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a subjective statement that could be included in a prehospital-care report?
options: Bystander stated, “He was drunk as a skunk.”
Patient vomited two times during transport.
Bystanders assisted EMTs in moving patient.
Patient is unsure of the reason for the call.
questions.explanation: This is a subjective statement. Statement a is inappropriate and unnecessary. Statements b and c are objective statements.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient has a wound on her anterior left lower leg that is spurting large amounts of blood. Direct pressure alone is ineffective. Where should you apply indirect pressure?
options: on the left side of the groin
both above and below the wound
questions.explanation: Apply indirect pressure (pressure point pressure) on the femoral artery by pressing on the left side of the groin. By pressing on the femoral artery, you will slow the flow of blood into the leg.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is an 84-year-old female who is complaining of severe abdominal pain. The abdomen is rigid and tender. What should you suspect?
questions.explanation: Internal bleeding caused by the rupture of an abdominal artery is an occasional emergency among elderly patients; symptoms are abdominal pain, tenderness, and rigidity. This patient is too old to be pregnant. A thoracic aneurysm will not cause abdominal distension.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of care that would be provided by the EMT performing triage at a mass-casualty incident?
options: covering the patient to prevent shock
questions.explanation: Only the most critical care, such as opening the airway to assess respirations, is provided during triage. Unfortunately, CPR cannot be started by the individuals performing triage.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which of the following describes an emergency patient move?
options: The patient cannot be cared for adequately in the present location.
Because of danger, there is no time to -immobilize the spine.
The patient is being moved to a more -comfortable location.
The patient is being moved against his or her will. –the emt-basic Practice exam 271
questions.explanation: An emergency move is one required to remove the patient from imminent danger, such as a fire, or when you must move a patient to gain access to other critically wounded patients. You should stabilize the head and neck with your hands, and if possible, apply a cervical collar before moving the patient.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 6-year-old male who is having a severe nosebleed. No trauma is suspected. How should you treat this child?
options: Pinch his nostrils together and transport him sitting upright, leaning forward.
Pack the nostrils with gauze and place the patient in the recovery position.
Apply indirect pressure to the upper lip and have the patient lie supine.
Apply an occlusive dressing and place the patient in the recovery position.
questions.explanation: For a nosebleed, when no trauma is suspected, have the patient sit up and lean forward to prevent blood from entering the airway and gently pinch the nostrils together.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: How does cardiac pain differ from respiratory pain?
options: Cardiac pain increases when the patient breathes.
Cardiac pain is localized around the heart and arms.
Cardiac pain does not change with movement or palpation.
Cardiac pain generally occurs on the left side of the chest.
questions.explanation: Cardiac pain, unlike respiratory pain, usually is not increased by movement or palpation; it is more diffuse than respiratory pain and may be felt in the shoulder, neck, and jaw, as well as in the chest.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which set of vital signs is within normal limits?
questions.explanation: This patient’s vital signs are all within the normal range for his age.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Use of accessory muscles, grunting, and stridor are all signs of
questions.explanation: Signs of labored respirations, or increased effort of breathing, include use of accessory muscles to breathe, grunting, and stridor. Stridor is a loud, high-pitched sound heard on respirations that usually indicates an upper-airway obstruction. Agonal respirations are gasping slow respirations that happen for some patients as they are dying.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You assess the color, temperature, and condition of a patient’s skin to gather information about his or her
questions.explanation: The color, temperature, and condition of the skin allows you to form an indirect assessment of the patient’s perfusion, or circulatory status.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which statement about assessing blood pressure is correct?
options: If you obtain one normal reading, it is not necessary to reassess blood pressure.
A single reading is not as useful as multiple readings used to look for a trend.
Variations of more than 5 mm Hg from normal is considered very significant.
Assess blood pressure by palpation only when the patient is in a quiet place.
questions.explanation: A single blood pressure reading, unless it is extremely high or extremely low, is not in itself significant. Blood pressure readings are most useful in establishing a trend that can signal changes in the patient status. Blood pressure varies constantly during the day by 20 mm Hg or more -during sleep, work, and relaxation activities. Assessment of BP by palpation is useful when the patient is in a very noisy environment, making auscultation difficult.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: While evaluating your 56-year-old male patient with chest discomfort, he suddenly collapses and becomes unconscious. He is apneic and pulseless. Which of the following will most likely reverse this condition?
options: Provide high-flow oxygen with a nonrebreather mask.
Begin chest compressions at a rate of 80 beats per minute.
Begin the process of defibrillating a patient with an AED.
Ventilate the patient with a pocket mask and supplemental oxygen.
questions.explanation: Chances are, this patient is experiencing sudden ventricular fibrillation that may be most likely reversed with defibrillation attempt.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 67-year-old female whom you found in cardiac arrest. You have resuscitated her by using an AED and are now transporting her. If she again becomes pulseless, you should
options: stop the ambulance and use the AED.
begin CPR and continue en route.
questions.explanation: For a pulseless patient, defibrillation takes precedence over either administering oxygen or performing CPR, but always stop the ambulance before using the AED. Do not analyze a heart rhythm in a moving vehicle.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which trauma patient is at greatest risk for -serious injury?
options: 43-year-old male, who fell seven feet from a stepladder
24-year-old female, involved in a moderatespeed vehicle collision
17-year-old male, who fell off a bicycle onto -concrete
5-year-old female, involved in a moderatespeed vehicle collision –emt-basic practice exam 386
questions.explanation: Children are likely to suffer serious injury in lower-speed collisions than adults; none of the other choices represents a serious mechanism of injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A bystander is doing CPR on a patient in cardiac arrest. You size up the scene, practice body substance isolation, and begin your initial assessment by having the bystander
options: provide a history of cardiac arrest.
questions.explanation: In order to accurately assess the patient’s airway, breathing, and circulation status, all external compressions or ventilations must be stopped.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient, a trauma victim, has flat jugular veins while she is lying down. What is this a sign of?
questions.explanation: The jugular veins are normally somewhat distended while a patient is lying down; flat veins signify blood loss.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In addition to evaluating for DCAP-BTLS, you would evaluate a patient’s lower extremities for which of the following?
questions.explanation: In addition to evaluating for specific injuries (DCAP-BTLS), check the extremities for sensation, motor function, and the presence of distal pulses.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: For which patient can you safely perform only a focused assessment, after taking vital signs and a SAMPLE history?
options: 6-year-old male, who fell off his bicycle in the road
11-year-old male, who had a diving accident
25-year-old female, with cuts and bruises after a car crash
43-year-old female, who cut her finger in the kitchen
questions.explanation: This patient has no significant mechanism of injury. If vital signs and a SAMPLE history disclose no abnormal findings, you need only perform a focused assessment. If you are ever unsure, however, you should perform a very thorough assessment, like a rapid trauma assessment, to look for hidden injuries.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are treating a 39-year-old female who has been in a motor vehicle accident. She has a suspected broken arm and leg, moderate bleeding from the leg wound, and signs and symptoms of early shock. Vital signs are pulse, 96 and thready; respirations, 28 and shallow; BP, 110/78. Your treatment should focus on
options: stabilizing the patient and transporting her to the hospital.
carefully splinting and bandaging all the patient’s injuries.
performing a detailed trauma assessment in the field.
opening the airway and monitoring vital signs every 15 minutes.
questions.explanation: Although you should quickly splint the patient’s wounds and stop major bleeding, treatment of a patient in shock should focus on rapidly transporting her to the hospital. You can continue to stabilize and treat her injuries while you are transporting her. Perform any other examinations while en route to the hospital. Monitor and assess her vital signs every five minutes.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are en route to the receiving facility with a 71-year-old male patient with a history of heart disease. The patient’s distress has been relieved with nitroglycerin, and his vital signs are stable. How often should you reassess the patient’s vital signs?
options: There is no need to reassess a stable patient.
questions.explanation: Reassess a stable patient every 15 minutes while en route to the receiving facility.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The major purpose of the EMT’s interactions with a patient during transport is to continuously evaluate the patient’s
options: skin color and pupils. –emt-basic practice exam 387
questions.explanation: Although interacting with the patient continuously will keep you informed about his or her level of anxiety and allow you to observe skin color and pupils, the main purpose is to assess changes in mental status and patency of airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your trauma patient has an amputated foot. How should you care for an amputated foot?
options: Wrap it in a sterile dressing, then in plastic, and keep it cool.
Immerse it in a tub of ice water and transport it with the patient.
Put it inside the PASG and inflate the other leg of the garment.
Put the foot back on the leg and then splint it in place.
questions.explanation: Always transport an amputated part along with the patient, wrapped in sterile dressings and then in plastic. Keep the amputated part cool, but do not put the foot directly in contact with ice or ice water because the ice can cause further tissue damage, which may result in the inability to reimplant the amputated part.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: If a patient’s respiratory rate is irregular, you should count the respirations for how long?
questions.explanation: If a patient’s respiratory rate is irregular, count for one full minute; otherwise, count for 30 seconds and multiply by two to obtain the respiratory rate.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which patient’s vital signs are abnormal?
questions.explanation: This patient’s pulse and respiratory rate are both elevated.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which statement about the assessment of a patient with cardiac compromise is correct?
options: You cannot determine the degree of cardiac damage in the field.
You should not ask patients about nitroglycerin or other drug use.
The purpose of the focused history is to determine whether to use the AED.
Patients usually describe cardiac pain as localized and moderately severe.
questions.explanation: It is impossible to determine the actual degree of tissue damage in the field, so the purpose of the focused assessment is to gather information for the receiving facility. You should always ask about medications; it is the M of SAMPLE. The focused history has nothing to do with AED use. Cardiac pain has all sorts of presentations, making it difficult to differentiate from other conditions.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is a priority patient who should be transported immediately?
options: 27-year-old female: 40 weeks pregnant, crowning, no complications
67-year-old male: history of heart disease, pain leaves after taking nitroglycerin
32-year-old female: allergic reaction to bee sting, rapid pulse, difficulty breathing
15-year-old male: fell off bicycle, possible fractured wrist, cuts, and bruises
questions.explanation: Patients who are having difficulty breathing are priority patients, as are patients who are unstable or who give a poor general impression, are having complications of childbirth, or who have signs and symptoms of shock.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The main purpose of the detailed physical examination is to
options: reveal hidden or less obvious injuries.
detect changes in the patient’s condition.
check vital signs and breath sounds.
questions.explanation: The purpose of the detailed physical examination, which is performed while en route to the hospital on all trauma patients as well as unresponsive medical patients, is to reveal hidden injuries.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: While performing a rapid trauma assessment of a patient who was injured in a motor vehicle accident, you note paradoxical motion. This indicates the presence of
questions.explanation: Paradoxical motion, the movement of one portion of the chest wall in the opposite direction from the rest of the chest during respirations, indicates serious injury to the chest wall.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The glottis is the
options: soft tissue at the base of the tongue.
opening between the pharynx and the -trachea. –emt-basic practice exam 388
questions.explanation: The glottis is the opening between the pharynx and the trachea.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 19-year-old male who has been in a motorcycle crash. Vital signs are pulse, 92 and weak; respirations, 24 and shallow; BP 116/80. He has a suspected spinal injury, as well as a painful, deformed, swollen right foot. Blood loss is not significant. What should your treatment plan focus on?
options: immobilizing the injured foot in position of function
immobilizing the patient and transporting him rapidly
opening the airway and ventilating the patient
applying the PASG and inflating all -compartments
questions.explanation: For a patient who is showing signs of shock as well as possible spinal injury, care focuses on immobilization and rapid transport, rather than on caring for isolated extremity injuries. This patient already has an open airway, and ventilation is not necessary, although oxygen should be administered by a nonrebreather mask and his respiratory status should be closely monitored as his respiratory rate is a little fast and his effort is shallow.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the general rule for administration of high-flow oxygen in the field?
options: Administer high-flow oxygen only under specific online medical direction.
Do not administer high-flow oxygen to children, elderly, or pregnant patients.
Do not administer high-flow oxygen to patients with obvious signs of shock.
Administer high-flow oxygen to all patients who are in respiratory distress.
questions.explanation: EMTs should administer high-flow oxygen to all patients who show signs and symptoms of respiratory distress.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The use of reasonable force when dealing with a patient with a behavioral disorder refers to
options: the force necessary to incapacitate the patient.
calling in police backup in a reasonable way.
using soft restraints rather than handcuffs.
the force necessary to restrain the patient.
questions.explanation: Reasonable force refers to the amount of force necessary to prevent injury to the patient or anyone else, including the rescuers. It is sometimes necessary to use force to restrain a patient with a behavioral disorder. Reasonable force does not define the type of restraints needed.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Rapid onset of altered mental status in a diabetic patient often results when the patient
questions.explanation: Rapid onset of altered mental status in diabetic patients is associated with hypoglycemia, or low blood sugar. This most commonly occurs when patients take their normal dose of insulin but skip a meal. Excessive exercise can also bring on a hypoglycemic state.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: While suctioning, if you cannot clear a patient’s mouth in 15 seconds, you should
options: suction the nose along with the mouth.
suction for 15 more seconds and reassess.
logroll the patient to clear the mouth.
questions.explanation: If you cannot clear the patient’s mouth of secretions, logroll the patient so the secretions can drain out. Never suction for longer than 15 seconds at a time, and oxygenate the patient between suction attempts.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A 65-year-old female is complaining of chest pressure, difficulty breathing, and is pale. She presents supine in bed. What should you do before sitting her up?
options: Check her blood pressure to make sure it is adequate.
Do nothing; sit her up right away.
Help the patient administer her own nitroglycerin tablets.
Check her pupils to make sure they are reactive.
questions.explanation: The position of comfort for most people having trouble breathing is sitting; however, this patient may have low blood pressure, possibly due to a cardiac emergency. In this case, it would be important to ensure that her blood pressure is adequate to support a sudden change in body position.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 20-year-old male who has been in a motorcycle accident. Because of clues from the mechanism of injury on the scene, spinal and head injuries are suspected. After your initial assessment, how often should you reassess vital signs?
questions.explanation: Because patients with spinal injuries are at risk of paralysis and breathing difficulties, reassess vital signs after every intervention, such as cervical immobilization or wholebody immobilizations. Once the patient is a little more stable, you would continue to reassess vital signs every five minutes because he would still be a critical patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 70-year-old male is complaining of chest pain and shortness of breath. He is alert with pale, cool, sweaty skin. His pulse is 100; BP, 136/64; and respirations, 24. Upon auscultation, you can hear crackles in the lung fields. Which of the following actions would be appropriate?
options: Have the patient lie flat because he could be in shock.
Provide oxygen at 2 L/min using a nasal cannula.
Administer nitroglycerin that is prescribed to the patient’s wife.
Have the patient sit up to assist with his breathing effort.
questions.explanation: This patient’s blood pressure is adequate to support a sitting patient. This patient also requires high-flow oxygen via nonrebreather as part of the treatment plan.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 23-year-old male who has suffered possible spinal trauma while playing football. He is still wearing his sports helmet as you begin your initial assessment. When should you remove his helmet?
options: when it is time to apply a cervical collar
if it prevents you from assessing the airway
before you position him on the long board
when you assess his level of consciousness
questions.explanation: Leave a helmet in place if it fits well, does not interfere with assessment or administration of oxygen, and the patient is not in cardiac arrest. Leave the shoulder pads in place along with the helmet. You do not need to apply a cervical collar to this patient, but you should immobilize his body and head to a long spine board. You can assess his level of consciousness without moving the helmet.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You would assess a patient’s breath sounds by placing your stethoscope over the midaxillary line and the
questions.explanation: Assess breath sounds bilaterally over the midaxillary and midclavicular lines.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient, a 45-year-old female, is still seated in her car after a crash. She does not appear to be in a life-threatening situation. What technique should you use to immobilize her spine?
options: Logroll her directly onto the ground; then apply a long spine board.
Have her get out of the car; then immobilize her while standing.
Apply a cervical collar before assisting her out of the car.
Apply a short spine board and then transfer her to a long spine board.
questions.explanation: Immobilize the patient to a short spine board like the Kendrick Extrication Device or other commercial device while she is still seated; then transfer her to a long spine board. If you suspected she was in shock or was unstable, you would perform a rapid extrication by applying a cervical collar and quickly removing her from the vehicle with the assistance of several rescuers while trying to maintain a neutral alignment to her spine.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 68-year-old female with chronic respiratory disease. She is experiencing difficulty breathing in spite of home oxygen delivery. You should
options: increase the flow rate of her oxygen supply.
replace her nasal cannula with a face mask.
treat the patient for the signs of shock.
questions.explanation: Consult medical direction when you encounter a patient who is having difficulty breathing in spite of home administration of oxygen.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In which situation should you assist a patient with using a prescribed inhaler?
options: 47-year-old male: history of severe asthma; -respirations, 28/min; wheezing, unresponsive
6-year-old female: history of upperrespiratory infection; respiratory rate, 24/min; coughing
69-year-old male: history of emphysema; difficulty breathing; inhaler was prescribed for his son
14-year-old female: history of asthma; respirations, 24/min; alert; used the inhaler one time -yesterday –emt-basic practice exam 390
questions.explanation: You can help a patient to use a prescribed inhaler if the patient has signs and symptoms of respiratory emergency, if the inhaler was prescribed for that patient, and if the patient is responsive. Be sure and determine if the patient has used the inhaler already and if it helped lessen the -distress.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is showing signs of possible carbon monoxide poisoning. What should be your first concern on this call?
options: Secure the safety of yourself, other people, and the patient.
Open the airway and administer supplemental oxygen.
Transport the patient rapidly to the closest facility.
Obtain medical direction to administer activated charcoal.
questions.explanation: In cases of inhalation poisoning when the toxin may still be present, your first concern should be to ensure your own safety and that of other people in the area. Move the patient to your ambulance, then begin your assessment and treatment. Activated charcoal is administered in the case of ingested (swallowed) poisons, not inhaled poisons.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which patient’s respiratory rate is normal?
questions.explanation: The normal breathing rate for an adult is between 12–20 breaths per minute.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In order to control the flow rate of oxygen to the patient, you must
options: open the valve on the tank.
questions.explanation: You control the flow of oxygen to the patient by adjusting the regulator on the oxygen tank. The size of the tank has no bearing on the flow rate. Oxygen tubing size is standardized. The valve must be in the open position to deliver any oxygen, but it does not change the rate of flow.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The airway of a child can become easily obstructed because, in comparison with an adult, a child has a
questions.explanation: In comparison with an adult, a child has a larger tongue, which can obstruct the airway, and a narrow airway, which can more easily be blocked by secretions.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The major hazard associated with using and transporting oxygen tanks is that they can
options: fail to function properly if chilled.
explode if dropped or handled roughly.
run out of battery power at any time.
questions.explanation: Because oxygen tanks contain gas at very high pressure, they can explode if they are dropped or otherwise roughly handled.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: All of the following are signs of inadequate breathing EXCEPT
questions.explanation: A respiratory rate of 12–20 breaths per minute is normal; signs of inadequate breathing include cyanosis, increased or decreased breathing rate, shortness of breath, and irregular rhythm.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following lists the correct order for cardiopulmonary circulation?
options: vena cava, left atrium, left ventricle, pulmonary veins, lungs, pulmonary arteries, right atrium, right ventricle, aorta
aorta, right ventricle, right atrium, pulmonary arteries, lungs, pulmonary veins, left ventricle, left atrium, vena cava
aorta, right atrium, right ventricle, pulmonary veins, lungs, pulmonary arteries, left atrium, left ventricle, vena cava
vena cava, right atrium, right ventricle, pulmonary arteries, lungs, pulmonary veins, left atrium, left ventricle, aorta
questions.explanation: This is the correct order for cardiopulmonary circulation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: If a clinical problem is identified in the EMS service, a plan is developed and implemented to reduce further occurrences of the same problem. This is called
questions.explanation: Quality assurance is the process that identifies the problem and focuses on the individual to make corrections. Quality improvement focuses on the system to identify an issue or process that is prone to failure and concentrates on making system corrections to solve the problem.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The artery that can be palpated on the anterior surface of the foot is the
questions.explanation: The dorsalis pedis artery, which can be palpated on the anterior surface of the foot, is used to check peripheral pulses.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 35-year-old female who has been in an automobile accident. She has suspected injuries to the pelvis, right upper leg, and shoulder. Her vital signs are pulse, 96, weak; respirations, 28/min, shallow; BP, 120/80. After checking the airway and administering oxygen, the next thing you should do is
options: splint the injured arm and the leg injury, and then package for transport.
perform a detailed physical assessment for internal bleeding.
treat for signs and symptoms of shock and transport rapidly.
determine the exact mechanism of injury to find all injuries.
questions.explanation: The patient’s pulse and breathing rate indicate that she is in shock; therefore, the first priority is to treat her for shock and transport her to a hospital.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You would evaluate the severity of a patient’s hyperthermia by assessing the
questions.explanation: Patients with hyperthermia with cool skin still retain their ability to eliminate heat; their condition is therefore not as serious as that of patients with hot skin, who have lost this ability. You should also closely monitor the patient’s mental status.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 79-year-old male who is experiencing chest pain. Which question would you ask to investigate the O part of the OPQRST acronym?
options: Have you ever felt this kind of pain before?
What were you doing when the pain started?
How long ago did the pain begin?
What does the pain feel like?
questions.explanation: O stands for onset, or when the pain began.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 67-year-old female with chest pains and a history of heart disease. After helping her take a dose of prescribed nitroglycerin, you find that her blood pressure is 96/68 and she is still in severe pain. Medical direction tells you to administer a second dose of nitroglycerin and transport. You should
options: administer the medication and document the vital signs and the order.
repeat the blood pressure reading and the order to medical direction.
ask to speak to a senior physician.
request advanced cardiac life support (ACLS) backup immediately.
questions.explanation: Nitroglycerin is not administered to a patient whose systolic blood pressure is lower than 100. Repeat the blood pressure reading and the order and request that the physician confirm it.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: In the case of children, airway adjuncts are used when the
options: child is in respiratory distress and needs supplemental oxygen.
usual means of opening and maintaining the airway are ineffective.
child is frightened of the oxygen mask and nasal cannula.
respiratory rate is less than 20/min or greater than 60/min.
questions.explanation: Airway adjuncts, such as nasopharyngeal and oropharyngeal airways, are used only when the jaw thrust and head-tilt/chin-lift are ineffective in opening the airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You should select the correct size oropharyngeal airway for a child by measuring from the
options: angle of the jaw to the corner of the mouth.
central incisor to the angle of the jaw.
central incisor to the tragus of the ear.
corner of the mouth to the cricoid cartilage.
questions.explanation: Select the correct size oral airway for a child by measuring from the angle of the jaw to the corner of the mouth.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Cool, clammy skin that is a sign of shock results from
options: a rise in the patient’s temperature.
the body’s attempt to increase the vascular space.
decreased heart rate and blood pressure.
diversion of blood flow to the vital organs. –emt-basic practice exam 392
questions.explanation: In early shock, the body diverts blood flow away from the skin and toward the body’s vital organs, resulting in decreased peripheral circulation and cool, clammy skin.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Delayed capillary refill is a reliable test of peripheral circulation in
questions.explanation: Capillary refill time is used to determine peripheral perfusion in infants and children only.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your medical patient is experiencing clinical signs of shock. You should
options: lay the patient flat on his stomach.
lay the patient flat on his back.
lay the patient flat on his back with legs elevated.
questions.explanation: If no traumatic injury mechanism is suspected, keeping the patient flat with legs elevated will promote blood circulation back to the critical organs.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The most important treatment intervention for shock is to maintain an open airway and to
options: ventilate the patient with a bag-valve mask.
questions.explanation: The most important intervention for shock is to provide a high concentration of oxygen; this helps prevent cells from dying due to lack of oxygen.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Bleeding caused by a wound to one large artery or vein can usually be controlled by
questions.explanation: Bleeding from a single site can usually be controlled by concentrated direct applied pressure to that site. Diffuse pressure is reserved for -circumstances when concentrated direct pressure is unadvisable, like when controlling bleeding from an open skull fracture. Pressure point -pressure and elevation are steps to take if direct pressure is ineffective.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The proper technique for applying the PASG to a patient with a suspected spinal injury is to
options: immobilize the patient to a long spine board before applying the PASG.
place the PASG on a long spine board and logroll the patient onto it.
apply both the PASG and the long spine board while the patient is standing.
elevate the patient’s legs while applying the PASG, but don’t move the pelvis.
questions.explanation: Because most patients for whom PASG is appropriate also have suspected spinal injury, place the PASG on the long spine board and logroll the patient onto the PASG and the board together.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 43-year-old female pedestrian who was hit by a car. No spinal trauma is -suspected, but the patient is showing signs of early shock and has a tender abdomen. You strongly suspect
questions.explanation: This mechanism of injury, plus the tender abdomen and signs of shock, strongly suggests that the patient has internal bleeding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 14-year-old female is unconscious after a 15-foot fall off a ladder. When evaluating her chest during a rapid trauma assessment, you should assess for
questions.explanation: Paradoxical motion of the chest may indicate broken ribs or flail chest. Softness and distention during assessment generally refers to the abdomen.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 62-year-old male who has survived a serious car crash. He is unconscious, cyanotic, and bleeding profusely from a thigh wound. Breathing is rapid and shallow. Other injuries are suspected. In which order should you provide care?
options: open the airway and provide oxygen, control bleeding, immobilize, transport
immobilize, control bleeding, transport, open the airway and provide oxygen
open the airway and provide oxygen, immobilize, control bleeding, transport
control bleeding, open the airway and provide oxygen, immobilize, transport
questions.explanation: Always open the airway first, regardless of other priorities. Since the patient is bleeding -profusely, the second priority is to control the bleeding from the leg wound. Since the patient is showing signs and symptoms of shock, the next priorities are to immobilize the spine and transport rapidly.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: For which type of wound should you use an -occlusive dressing that is taped on only three sides?
questions.explanation: For a sucking chest wound, apply an occlusive dressing that is taped on only three sides. This will allow air to escape from the chest cavity.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When treating a patient who has suffered an electrical burn, you should be alert for the possibility of
questions.explanation: Electrical burns are frequently more serious than they appear from the entrance and exit wounds; you should be alert for the possibility of cardiac arrest and be prepared to use the AED.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 36-year-old male is a restrained passenger in a car crash. He complains of pain to his right leg. While assessing his leg, you palpate for
questions.explanation: In DCAP-BTLS, the D stands for deformity. Distention may be found on palpation and visualization of the abdomen.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient has a painful deformity of the right lower leg. The pulse in the right posterior tibial artery is missing. Before splinting the injured leg, you should
options: use gentle traction to attempt to align the limb.
apply a tourniquet to stop internal bleeding.
check the right brachial pulse as well.
place the right foot in the position of -function.
questions.explanation: When a pulse is missing distal to an injury, attempt to align the limb with gentle traction. Bringing the limb into alignment may relieve pressure on the blood vessels and restore -circulation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which symptoms are signs of a partial upper-airway obstruction due to the presence of a -foreign body?
options: increased work of breathing during expiration with a wheezy cough
gasping respiratory efforts, and the patient is unable to cough or speak
no effort of breathing, absent chest-wall movement, unable to cough or speak
stridor during inspiration, inability to speak, and dyspnea
questions.explanation: Partial upper-airway obstruction due to a foreign body is differentiated from complete obstruction by the patient’s ability to breathe or speak. Stridor indicates upper-airway obstruction. A wheezing sound (patient a) indicates a lower-airway obstruction. Patients b and c have a total airway obstruction.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A 23-year-old male has suffered a penetrating head wound that is bleeding profusely and a cervical spine injury. During your rapid trauma assessment, you should
options: treat the head wound and continue your rapid assessment.
stop your exam and provide appropriate care for both injuries.
manage the cervical spine injury and continue your rapid assessment.
make a mental note of both injuries and continue the assessment.
questions.explanation: Both life-threatening injuries to the patient must be treated as quickly as possible.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What should you do if you do not have the right size cervical collar to fit a patient with a -suspected spinal injury?
options: Use the next larger or smaller size collar.
Use rolled towels secured with tape.
Leave the neck unsecured and tape the head.
Place the patient on a backboard without a collar.
questions.explanation: If the mechanism of injury of the patient’s signs and symptoms suggest spinal injury, you must immobilize the neck and spine. Use rolled-up towels if you do not have a cervical collar that fits properly.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 12-year-old female fell while skating. She did not strike her head and is alert, complaining of pain to the left wrist. How should you assess this patient?
options: Assess just the areas that the patient tells you are painful.
Assess every body part from head to toe.
Focus on just the patient’s airway and cervical spine.
Complete only the initial and ongoing -assessment.
questions.explanation: Without a serious mechanism of injury being reported, a focused physical assessment of the patient’s injuries is appropriate.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which statement by a patient is most likely to suggest that the patient’s thinking is psychotic?
options: “They sent you to lock me up.” –emt-basic practice exam 394
questions.explanation: This statement reflects paranoia, a psychotic state in which people imagine that other people are conspiring to harm them.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Your patient is agitated and confused and seems to be displaying symptoms of drug use. The best way to prevent the situation from becoming dangerous to the patient, yourself, or others is to
options: restrain the patient as soon as possible.
speak calmly and quietly to the patient.
refuse to treat the patient until he calms down.
inform the patient of your self-defense -techniques.
questions.explanation: You can frequently prevent situations from becoming dangerous by maintaining a calm, professional manner and speaking quietly to a distraught patient.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 20-year-old female complains of leg and hip pain after falling off a 20-foot ladder. You should conduct a
options: an OPQRST on the pain only.
questions.explanation: The mechanism of injury suggests that you should look through the kill zone areas to make sure there are no other underlying injuries.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following patients is showing signs and symptoms of imminent respiratory arrest?
options: 4-year-old male; respirations, 8/min; unresponsive; limp muscle tone
3-year-old female: respirations, 10/min; cyanosis; decreased muscle tone
questions.explanation: Signs of imminent respiratory arrest are a breathing rate of less than 10/min, limp muscle tone, slow or absent heart rate, and weak or absent distal pulses; the patient will also be unresponsive. These patients need artificial ventilation and chest compression. Patients a, b, and d are in respiratory insufficiency and the early stages of respiratory failure, which, if uncorrected, will lead to respiratory arrest.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: When caring for a patient whose baby is delivering in a breech presentation, you should do all of the following EXCEPT
options: position the mother with her knees flexed, drawn up, and widely separated.
administer high-flow oxygen to the mother and begin transport quickly.
pull gently on the infant’s trunk or legs if delivery of the head is delayed.
allow the delivery to occur spontaneously until the trunk is delivered.
questions.explanation: Do not pull on the infant’s trunk or legs to assist delivery. If the baby’s head is not delivered spontaneously, place your hand inside the birth canal to hold the cord and walls of the vagina away from the baby’s face, and transport immediately.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are assessing an awake, alert patient complaining of abdominal pain. He denies any trauma. When conducting a focused history and physical exam, what should you do first?
options: Gather the history of the present illness.
Question the patient about past medical -problems.
questions.explanation: Conducting an OPQRST on the pain and finding out the SAMPLE history will gather a lot of information about the patient’s complaint.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The correct way to stimulate a newborn to breathe is to
options: rub his or her back or flick the soles of his or her feet.
position him or her with the head higher than the body.
suction his or her nose and then the mouth.
smack him or her gently on the buttocks.
questions.explanation: If necessary, stimulate a newborn to breathe by gently rubbing his or her back or flicking the soles of his or her feet. You should always suction the mouth first and then the nose because infants are obligate nasal breathers, and if you suction the nose first, it may stimulate them to take a breath, inhaling any amniotic fluid remaining in the oropharynx into the lungs.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: During your focused history and physical exam of an unresponsive patient, you should first
options: obtain vital signs, and then gather OPQRST from the patient.
conduct a rapid physical assessment, and then obtain vital signs.
gather a SAMPLE history, and then OPQRST from the family.
request ALS and begin a detailed head-to-toe exam. –emt-basic practice exam 395
questions.explanation: Since the patient is unresponsive, the most effective method of obtaining information about the patient’s condition is through a rapid physical assessment.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: To help determine what poison a patient has ingested, you should be alert for chemical burns around the mouth as well as
questions.explanation: Some poisons can be identified by the unusual breath odors they cause; for example, cyanide causes an odor of bitter almonds. Choice a is incorrect because some substances may burn mucous membranes of the mouth but may not burn the skin. Choice b is incorrect because, depending upon the substance ingested, vomit may or may not be discolored or bloody. Choice c is incorrect because the toxin may or may not cause changes in skin temperature or color; it will depend upon the action of the poison and if it causes shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Baseline vital signs in the unresponsive medical patient include the patient’s
questions.explanation: The other three choices are part of the SAMPLE history. Vital signs include pulse, blood pressure, respirations, skin signs, and pupil reactivity to light.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient has accidentally sprayed insecticide in his eye. What should immediate first aid involve?
options: placing an airtight dressing over the eye
administering activated charcoal to the patient
irrigating the eye with clean water for 20 minutes
placing 10 cc of sterile saline solution in the eye
questions.explanation: When toxins come in contact with the eye, irrigate the eye with clean water for 20 minutes, preferably while en route to the hospital.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You just completed a rapid physical exam on an unresponsive 65-year-old female. Your next action should be to
options: take a history of the present illness.
questions.explanation: Since the patient is unresponsive, it will not be possible to elicit a medical history directly. An early set of vital signs may help determine the underlying cause of the patient’s condition.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 14-year-old male who struck his head on a diving board and nearly drowned. Your first reaction should be to
options: remove the patient from the water and warm him.
release gastric distention and provide -artificial ventilation.
immobilize the patient while still in the water.
questions.explanation: Because of the great risk of spinal injury with this type of trauma, the EMT’s first action should be to immobilize the patient while still in the water; the second step is to attend to the airway.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 2-year-old female who has injured her leg. The best way to calm her while you are examining her is to
options: explain what you are doing in technical -language.
tell her that if she doesn’t cooperate, she won’t get better.
examine her while she is sitting on her -parent’s lap.
do any painful procedures first so she can calm down.
questions.explanation: Calm the toddler by allowing her to remain with her parent and by explaining what you are doing in simple language. Warn her if something will hurt, and if possible, perform pain-ful procedures (sometimes blood pressure assessment is painful) at the end of your assessment. Also, keep all your equipment out of sight until you are ready to use it so she can’t get all worked up about it. Kids have incredible imaginations and will invent all sorts of painful scenarios as they look at your equipment and supplies. Sometimes you can let the child briefly examine a piece of equipment before you use it or pretend to use it on the parent to help calm her down.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which statement about suctioning infants and children is correct?
options: Insert the suction catheter until it touches the back of the throat.
Administer oxygen immediately before and after suctioning.
Never suction for longer than 30 seconds at a time.
The vacuum pump should be set no higher than 200 mm Hg. –emt-basic practice exam 396
questions.explanation: When suctioning infants and children, be sure not to stimulate the back of the throat, which can lead to bradycardia. Administer oxygen before and after suctioning, suction for no more than 10–15 seconds at a time, and set the vacuum pump at 80–120 mm Hg.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are caring for a victim of a motor vehicle accident who is approximately seven months pregnant. The mechanism of injury strongly suggests spinal trauma. How should you position the patient during transport?
options: immobilized on a long backboard that is then tilted to the left
immobilized and transported supine on a long backboard
immobilized in whatever position she is most comfortable
seated upright with her torso immobilized in a short spine device
questions.explanation: The patient should be immobilized on a long backboard, but the entire board should be tilted to the left side with padding to reduce the pressure of the fetus on her circulatory systems. This condition is called supine hypotension syndrome.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In order to determine whether an infant is responsive to verbal stimuli, you would
options: ask the child to say his or her name.
have a parent speak to the child.
questions.explanation: An infant who is responsive to voice, or to verbal stimuli, would turn in the direction of the parent’s voice.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The correct procedure for inserting an oral airway in an infant or child is to
options: use the tongue depressor to move the tongue forward, and insert the airway right side up.
tip the head back and open the mouth wide, and then rotate the airway on insertion.
lubricate the tip of the airway with sterile saline, and insert it until you feel resistance.
insert the airway with the bevel toward the base of the throat, pushing gently if you feel resistance.
questions.explanation: When inserting an airway in an infant or child, use a tongue depressor and avoid rotating the airway.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: When caring for a rape victim, it is important to discourage the patient from
questions.explanation: The rape victim should be discouraged from bathing, douching, urinating, or cleaning any wounds, since these activities may destroy evidence.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In order to care for a child who has a partial upper-airway obstruction, you should
options: use a combination of back blows and chest thrusts.
open the airway and attempt to ventilate the child.
provide oxygen, position the child, and transport rapidly.
use back blows and finger sweeps to remove the obstruction.
questions.explanation: Back blows, chest thrusts, and ventilation are performed on infants with complete airway obstructions; care for a child with partial obstruction consists of placing the child in a position of comfort and transporting rapidly.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: In which of the following circumstances should you suspect the possibility of child abuse?
options: A parent tells you the 4-month-old infant’s injuries were caused by rolling off the bed.
A distraught parent promptly reports and requests treatment for a seriously injured child.
A parent tells you that the child walked into a door at school, but the teacher disagrees.
The child and parent both give you an identical account of how an injury occurred.
questions.explanation: In cases of child abuse, different people frequently give different accounts of the same accident, or parents may appear unconcerned.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You should suspect the possibility of shock in a child who has a recent history of
questions.explanation: Shock in children is most frequently associated with fluid loss due to vomiting and diarrhea or to blood loss.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient, a 27-year-old-female who is pregnant with her second child, tells you, “My water broke an hour ago.” This should alert you that the patient
options: has signs and symptoms of shock.
will have her baby fairly soon.
is having a miscarriage. –emt-basic practice exam 397
questions.explanation: It is normal for the amniotic sac to rupture and the fluid it contains to drain out of the woman’s vagina before delivery.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You should place your hand on a pregnant woman’s abdomen during contractions to determine the
questions.explanation: If the mother’s abdomen feels hard during the contractions, they are strong, and birth is more likely to happen quickly.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are assisting with a delivery in the field. As the baby’s head is born, you find that the cord is wrapped tightly around the infant’s neck and cannot be dislodged. You should
options: transport the mother immediately in the knee/chest position.
clamp the cord in two places and cut it between the clamps.
pull hard on the cord to force the placenta to deliver immediately.
exert gentle pressure on the baby’s head to slow the delivery.
questions.explanation: If the cord is wrapped around the infant’s neck and it cannot be placed back over the head, the infant is in danger of suffocation. Immediately clamp the cord in two places and carefully cut between the clamps. Never pull on the umbilical cord as this can cause uterine inversion, which is a life-threatening emergency.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: When is it normal for the baby’s head to rotate from the facedown position to the side?
questions.explanation: The baby’s head is usually born facedown in the vertex position, and the head rotates just after it emerges from the birth canal.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are assessing a newborn who has a pink body but blue extremities, a pulse rate of 98/min, no response to suctioning, moderate flexion of the extremities, and a respiratory rate of 38/min. What is the APGAR score for this infant?
