questions.explanation: At present, the patient is not currently with any signs or symptoms of a severe allergic reaction, so you should continue with your physical exam.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are assessing a patient who was struck by a car. He has pain and swelling over his upper right quadrant and appears to be developing symptoms of shock. You suspect
options: traumatic injury to the large bowel.
questions.explanation: Blunt trauma with pain and swelling over the upper right quadrant should raise a high index of suspicion for a lacerated liver.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The areas for greatest concern of internal blood loss are the chest, abdomen, and
questions.explanation: A patient can lose all his or her blood volume through an open pelvic fracture.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is an early sign of shock?
questions.explanation: An increased pulse rate is an early sign of shock as the body attempts to compensate.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following are the correct four links in the chain of survival, which is recommended by the American Heart Association for successful care of prehospital cardiac arrest victims?
options: early access to emergency care, early ventilations, early defibrillation, and early advanced care
early access to the hospital, early ventilations, early defibrillation, and early evacuation
early access to emergency care, early cardiac monitoring, early defibrillation, and early advanced care
early access to emergency care, early CPR, early defibrillation, and early advanced care
questions.explanation: The American Heart Association has recognized four links in the chain of survival, including early access to emergency care, early CPR, early defibrillation, and early advanced care.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A condition that is caused by the heart’s inability to pump and leads to an overload of fluid in the tissues is
questions.explanation: Congestive heart failure (CHF) is a condition in which the heart losses the ability to adequately pump, allowing fluid to overload in the tissues.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is an indication of cardiac compromise?
questions.explanation: Cardiac compromise will present signs of shock, including increased pulse rate, altered mental status, and agitation.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: When assessing a patient with chest pain, asking the patient to rate the pain on a scale from one to ten would fall under what part of the acronym OPQRST?
questions.explanation: When asking a patient to rate their pain on a one to ten scale, you are attempting to quantify the severity of the pain.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: In order to assist in the administration of nitroglycerine to a patient, his or her systolic blood pressure should be above
questions.explanation: The systolic blood pressure should be above 100 mmHg in order to give nitroglycerin.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The Sellick’s maneuver is performed by applying _____________ pressure on the cricoid cartilage.
questions.explanation: The Sellick’s maneuver is performed by applying downward pressure on the cricoid cartilage.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The CombiTube is a
options: device to be used under direct laryngoscopy.
questions.explanation: The CombiTube is a dual lumen, blind insertion airway device.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: When inserted, the CombiTube will enter the trachea approximately _________ of the time.
questions.explanation: When inserted, the CombiTube will go into the esophagus approximately 80% to 90% of the time and into the trachea 10% to 20% of the time.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The laryngeal mask airway was initially designed
options: for the use of EMTs in the field.
for use in a controlled environment, such as the operating room.
to allow the patient to step down from a more invasive device when healing.
questions.explanation: The laryngeal mask airway (LMA) was originally designed to be used in the operating room.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The mechanical device that uses negative pressure to differentiate tracheal from esophageal intubation is a(n)
questions.explanation: The esophageal detector device (EDD) uses negative pressure to differentiate from intubations in the trachea versus in the esophagus.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The side or part of the body that is farthest from the midline is described as
questions.explanation: The part of the body farthest from the midline is said to be lateral.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The largest organ of the body is part of the
questions.explanation: The integumentary system (skin) is the largest organ in the body.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You respond to a patient who has a behavioral emergency. The patient states that he hears voices talking to him, and the voices are telling him to kill. You should
options: believe him and be alert to possible violence.
try to talk sense into him.
tell him that the voices are not real.
restrain him until law enforcement arrives for assistance.
questions.explanation: Always be alert for the potential of violence on behavioral emergency calls.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A common symptom seen with a diabetic patient is excessive drinking to satisfy extreme thirst. This is also called
questions.explanation: Polydipsia is the medical term for excessive thirst and is frequently seen in patients with high blood sugar.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Patients who do not produce insulin at all
questions.explanation: Patients who do not produce insulin have type 1 diabetes.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The leading cause of maternal death in the first trimester of pregnancy is
options: internal hemorrhage, secondary to an ectopic pregnancy.
questions.explanation: The leading cause of maternal death in the first trimester of pregnancy is internal bleeding, secondary to an ectopic pregnancy.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A multipara woman is one who
options: has had more than one baby born alive.
questions.explanation: Multipara means that the patient has had more than one baby born alive.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You arrive on the scene to find a patient in the beginning of the second stage of labor. You should
options: stay on the scene and deliver the baby.
questions.explanation: If the patient is beginning the second stage of labor, then birth is imminent. As long as there are no complications suspected, you should remain on the scene and deliver the baby.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The presence of meconium
options: is a risk factor for fetal distress.
is only emergent if the baby is in respiratory distress.
questions.explanation: The presence of meconium is a risk factor for fetal distress.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Preeclampsia typically develops after the___________ week of pregnancy.
questions.explanation: Preeclampsia typically develops after the twentieth week of pregnancy.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: When resources and personnel are provided by other jurisdictions, this is called
questions.explanation: Mutual aid occurs when resources and personnel are provided by other jurisdictions.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Prior to the establishment of NIMS, the incident command system was developed and designed for
questions.explanation: The incident command system was originally developed for the wildfires in California.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which of the following is a component of the MSDS?
questions.explanation: Reactivity data, health hazard data, and the product boiling point are all components of the MSDS.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A DOT Hazard Class 1 indicates
questions.explanation: A DOT Hazard Class 1 is an explosive.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Radioactive material can kill or injure by
options: burning the surface of the skin.
questions.explanation: Radioactive material kills or injuries by disrupting cellular functions.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: When applying a bandage or a dressing to a hand, you should put the hand in what position?
questions.explanation: When applying a bandage or dressing to a hand, place the hand in the position of function.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Soft tissue injuries can be
questions.explanation: Soft tissue injuries can be both external and internal.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Using the rule of nines, the posterior trunk accounts for what percentage of body surface area on an adult?
questions.explanation: Using the rule of nines, the posterior trunk accounts for 18% of body surface area of the adult.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Using the rule of nines, each lower extremity in a pediatric patient accounts for what percentage of body surface area?
questions.explanation: Using the rule of nines, each lower extremity in a pediatric patient accounts for 7% of body surface area.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is a high priority (severe) burn injury?
options: full thickness burns affecting less than 2%
burns complicated by an extremity injury
questions.explanation: Any burn that is complicated by an extremity injury is classified as a severe burn.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In which instance is the placement of an AED contraindicated?
options: a choking patient who is losing consciousness
questions.explanation: A choking patient who is losing consciousness should be lowered to the ground and immediately have abdominal thrust and ventilations attempted.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Early advanced care is the ________ link in the chain of survival.
questions.explanation: Early advanced care is the fourth link in the chain of survival.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Besides ventricular fibrillation, the second rhythm that the AED considers shockable is
questions.explanation: An AED will shock ventricular fibrillation and rapid ventricular tachycardia.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Infarction means
questions.explanation: Infarction means tissue death.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Another term for an abnormal cardiac rhythm is
questions.explanation: An arrhythmia is an abnormal cardiac rhythm.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is an indicator of correct endotracheal tube placement?
options: your partner’s assurance that he saw the tube pass through the vocal cords
questions.explanation: Positive waveform capnography is an indicator of correct endotracheal tube placement.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the best indicator of correct tube placement?
questions.explanation: End-tidal CO2 monitoring is optimal for indicating correct tube placement.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The most common complication from the use of a flow-restricted, oxygen-powered ventilation device is
questions.explanation: Gastric distention is the most common complication when using flow-restricted, oxygen-powered ventilation devices.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The medical term for shortness of breath is
questions.explanation: Dyspnea is the medical term for shortness of breath.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Patients who have severe difficulty breathing are usually found in what position?
questions.explanation: Patients who have severe shortness of breath are usually in the tripod position because this helps to maximize air exchange in the lungs.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are assessing a patient who has inhaled a poisonous substance and has no signs of trauma. You should initially
options: move the patient into the fresh air.
questions.explanation: Any patients that have inhaled a toxic substance should be immediately moved to fresh air.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Approximately 80% of all poisonings are by
questions.explanation: Approximately 80% of poisonings are by ingestion.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You are assessing a patient who has a history of behavioral emergencies and hostile tendencies. What should be a major clue for the possibility of danger?
options: the level of the patient’s distress
questions.explanation: The patient’s history, posture, and level of distress are all indicators of the possibility of danger to the EMTs.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The two categories in which the underlying causes of behavioral emergencies fall under are
options: psychogenic brain disorders and neural dysfunctions.
organic brain syndrome and functional disorders.
questions.explanation: The two classifications of behavioral emergencies are organic brain syndrome and functional disorders.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The topographic anatomical term for the front side of the body is
questions.explanation: The anatomical term for the front of the body is anterior.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Bleeding after labor should NOT be managed by
options: administering high flow O2 via an NRB mask.
applying a sanitary napkin into the vagina.
placing the mother left laterally recumbent.
placing the infant on the mother’s chest and allowing him or her to nurse.
questions.explanation: Never place anything inside the vagina in an attempt to control vaginal bleeding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a sign of respiratory distress in children?
questions.explanation: Crying is not a sign of respiratory distress in children; it is usually a good indicator of airway patency.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A carcinogenic effect is
options: an increase in the risk of an individual developing cancer.
an increase in the risk of a developing embryo having physical effects.
a permanent change in the individual’s DNA.
none of the above. –emt-basic practice exam 9234
questions.explanation: A carcinogenic effect is one that will increase an individual’s risk of developing cancer.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Secondary decontamination is performed
questions.explanation: Secondary decontamination is typically performed at the hospital.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A combustible liquid is considered a DOT Hazard Class
questions.explanation: A combustible liquid is a DOT Hazard Class 3.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: When at a landing zone, how should the aircraft be approached?
options: depends on the make and model of the aircraft
only when accompanied by a flight crew member
questions.explanation: An aircraft should never be approached unless you are accompanied by a member of the flight team.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The optimal landing zone should be established on level ground, free of debris, and measure
questions.explanation: The optimal landing zone measures 100 feet by 100 feet and is free of obstacles and debris.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are assessing a patient who has sustained an injury to the elbow. He is unable to straighten his elbow. Which of the following would be correct when splinting this patient?
options: Secure the entire extremity to the body.
Place the extremity in a bent position and apply a sling and swathe.
Have the patient hold his arm in a position of comfort.
questions.explanation: When splinting an elbow, secure the entire extremity to the body.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Where would you find a fractured malar bone?
questions.explanation: The malar bone is in the cheek.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the term that identifies the three signs that are indicative of increased intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: Cushing’s triad consists of increased blood pressure, decreased heart rate, and irregular respirations. In addition, it is a sign of increased intracranial pressure.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Bleeding from a subdural hematoma is
options: a collection of blood between the dura mater and the skull.
questions.explanation: Bleeding from a subdural hematoma is venous in nature.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a component of the Glasgow Coma Scale?
questions.explanation: Respirations are not included in the Glasgow Coma Scale.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The electrode that is placed either on the right arm or just below the right clavicle is what color?
questions.explanation: The white electrode is placed either on the right arm or just below the right clavicle.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What type of bleeding is characterized by slow, dark, red blood?
options: none of the above –emt-basic practice exam 9235
questions.explanation: Venous bleeding is characterized by slow, dark red blood.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are assessing a hypertensive patient with epistaxis. After scene safety and BSI precautions have been taken, you should first
options: apply direct pressure, squeezing both nostrils.
attempt to calm and reassure the patient.
questions.explanation: Control of an epistaxis includes applying direct pressure and squeezing both nostrils.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: There are ________ cranial nerves.
questions.explanation: There are 10 cranial nerves.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The term describing structures that are located closer to the trunk is
questions.explanation: The term proximal describes a structure that is located closer to the trunk.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A normal pupil reaction to shining a bright light into an eye would be the _____________ of the pupil.
questions.explanation: A normal pupil reaction is to constrict when exposed to a bright light.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a route that an indirect vehicle-borne disease is carried on?
questions.explanation: Water is an example of an indirect vehicleborne method for disease transmission.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A ____________ is a microorganism that is capable of causing a disease in a host.
questions.explanation: A pathogen is a microorganism that is capable of causing a disease in a host.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true?
options: Only some infectious diseases are contagious, but all contagious diseases are infectious.
questions.explanation: Only some infectious diseases are contagious, but all contagious diseases are infectious.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is the incubation period for Hepatitis B?
questions.explanation: The normal incubation period for Hepatitis B is 4 to 12 weeks.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Cirrhosis of the liver develops in ____________ of patients with chronic Hepatitis C.
questions.explanation: Cirrhosis of the liver will develop in about one-half of patients with chronic Hepatitis C.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Tuberculosis is a _______________ organism.
questions.explanation: Tuberculosis is a bacterial organism.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The term power lift refers to using muscle groups in your
questions.explanation: The term power lift refers to lifting while using muscles in your legs.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Rapid extrication should be used for which of the following?
options: a patient who is bleeding profusely from the back of the head
a patient who is screaming and stating that she is unable to breathe
an accident where the airbag has not deployed
questions.explanation: Rapid extrication should be used in a vehicle when the airbag has not deployed and there is significant chance of deployment because of damage to the vehicle. Deployment of the airbag can cause injury to responders and to the patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the ventilation rate for children who are experiencing brain herniation?
questions.explanation: Children should be ventilated at a rate of 30 breaths per minute when brain herniation is suspected.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Injuries to the spine that cause the individual vertebrae to pull apart and separate from one another are called
questions.explanation: Distraction injuries are injuries to the spine in which the individual vertebrae pull apart and separate from each other.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Aniscoria is
questions.explanation: The term aniscoria means unequal pupils.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The best way to treat a patient who has a large, spurting laceration on the head is to
options: apply pressure to the carotid artery.
place a gloved hand over gauze while holding constant, firm pressure.
apply a bandage and dressing to the head.
questions.explanation: The best way to treat a patient who has a large, spurting laceration on the head is to place a gloved hand over gauze while holding constant, firm pressure.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are assessing a child who has sustained burns to his entire anterior legs bilaterally and to his anterior right arm. He has a burned body surface area of __________.
questions.explanation: Burns to a child’s anterior legs bilaterally and his anterior right arm cover a body surface area of 18.5%.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is a curved forcep that can be used to remove a foreign body from a patient’s mouth?
questions.explanation: Magill forceps are used to remove foreign objects from a patient’s mouth.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding laryngeal mask airways?
options: They can be used in morbidly obese patients.
They are a single lumen, blind insertion airway device.
They are classified as a high-pressure airway device.
They decrease the risk of aspiration in patients who have a full stomach.
questions.explanation: Laryngeal mask airways are single lumen, blind insertion airway devices. They are not to be used in morbidly obese patients. These patients cannot tolerate high airway pressures, and using this device on them can increase the risk of aspiration of gastric contents.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What does the P stand for in SAMPLE?
questions.explanation: The P in SAMPLE stands for past medical history.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Your partner is obtaining a history from a patient and asked the question, “Can you point to the place where it hurts with one finger?” This question would represent what part of the OPQRST evaluation?
questions.explanation: The question “Can you point to the place where it hurts with one finger” is determining the region or radiation of the pain.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is the medical term for double vision?
questions.explanation: Diplopia is the medical term for double vision.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is another name for the mitral valve in the heart?
questions.explanation: The tricuspid valve is also called the mitral valve.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the name for an emotionally unjustified erection seen with spinal cord injury patients?
questions.explanation: A priapism is an emotionally unjustified erection seen with spinal cord injuries.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: During your assessment of a patient, you note that her skin is very pale. Which of the following would be the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Blood loss would cause pale skin. Fever, hypertension, and carbon monoxide poisoning would typically present with red skin.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the classic signs seen in Cushing’s triad?
questions.explanation: Pupillary response is not one of the three classic signs seen with Cushing’s response.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What does the S stand for in the charting mnemonic SOAP?
questions.explanation: The S in SOAP stands for subjective.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following is the study of relationships among speed, mass, direction of the force, and physical injury?
questions.explanation: Kinetics is the study of relationships among speed, mass, direction of force, and physical injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An explosion has occurred, and your patient sustained injuries when they were thrown into a brick wall. This type of injury is classified as a
questions.explanation: Tertiary blast injuries occur when a person is thrown into an object from an explosion.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the medical term for the passage of dark, tarry stools that is indicative of lower gastrointestinal bleeding?
questions.explanation: Melena is the medical term for the passage of dark, tarry stools and is indicative of lower gastrointestinal bleeding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following would cause obstructive shock?
questions.explanation: A tension pneumothorax would cause obstructive shock.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What type of burn involves the epidermis and varying layers of the dermis?
questions.explanation: A partial thickness burn (second degree) involves the epidermis and varying layers of the dermis.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient has had battery acid splashed in his eyes and now has no vision. Which of the following treatments would be most appropriate?
options: Immediately neutralize the acid with a base, such as baking soda.
Cover both his eyes with a sterile dressing.
Flush the affected eye with normal saline for ten minutes.
Flush both eyes with eyewash or normal saline for ten minutes.
questions.explanation: If a patient has any chemical in his or her eyes, immediately flush both eyes with eyewash or normal saline for at least ten minutes. Never attempt to neutralize a chemical burn.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following would classify as a critical burn?
options: a full thickness burn to the body with 8% body surface area
a partial thickness burn to 5% of one foot
a partial thickness burn to 25% of the body
a superficial burn to 45% of the body
questions.explanation: A partial thickness burn to an extremity is classified as a critical burn.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which one of the meninges is closest to the skull?
questions.explanation: The dura mater is the layer of the meninges that is closest to the skull.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What part of the spinal cord innervates the diaphragm?
options: all the spine innervates the diaphragm
questions.explanation: The cervical spine innervates the diaphragm.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: During the initial phase of an acute stress reaction, which of the following physiological responses will occur?
options: normal vital signs that remain unchanged
increased vital signs that quickly return to normal
increased pulse rate and pupillary dilatation
questions.explanation: Both good stress (eustress) and bad stress (distress) will initially cause sympathetic stimulation such as increased heart and respiratory rate, bronchodilation, dilated pupils, and increased blood flow to the skeletal muscles.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You use an end-tidal carbon dioxide detector as a tool to determine if endotracheal intubation has been correctly obtained. The absence of carbon dioxide in exhaled air after six ventilations indicates that the endotracheal tube has been
options: placed in the right mainstem bronchus.
placed in the left mainstem bronchus.
questions.explanation: No carbon dioxide after six ventilations indicates either that the tube is in the esophagus or that the patient has been dead long enough that no carbon dioxide is being produced.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In which of the following situations should a paramedic perform an initial assessment first?
options: during cardiac arrest at a swimming pool
when the patient is in a toxic environment
when the scene is not yet secured by law enforcement
during a rescue from a fully involved structure fire
questions.explanation: Before assessing airway, breathing, and circulation, it is necessary to remove the patient (and yourself) to a place of relative safety.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The focused history and physical examination of a patient begins after you have
options: controlled immediate threats to the patient’s life.
transported the patient to the hospital.
secured the scene and gained access to the patient.
questions.explanation: The purpose of the focused history and physical is to detect additional problems after you have controlled immediate threats to the patient’s life. The ongoing assessment is typically performed during transport. Medical control may be consulted anytime during the call when you feel it is appropriate or whenever your protocols and standing orders require it.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: This patient is most likely suffering from
questions.explanation: This patient is exhibiting the classic signs and symptoms of an abdominal aortic aneurysm. Further palpation may cause the aneurysm to rupture, so be very careful in assessing this patient. The other choices will not cause abdominal pulsations to occur.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: This patient’s vital signs have been worsening steadily throughout the time he has been under your care. Treatment for this patient should include
questions.explanation: Cardiac monitoring should always be performed when you suspect an aneurysm is present. Rapid infusion of crystalloid solution is often indicated in the treatment of shock, but the fluid must be titrated to patient response. Dopamine is indicated for cardiogenic shock. Shock in this patient would be due to hypovolemia. Dopamine is contraindicated in the presence of uncorrected hypovolemia. PASG/MAST may be indicated for treatment of AAA in some jurisdictions; however, it is not a standardized treatment.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The hypoxic drive is regulated by
questions.explanation: COPD patients can no longer rely upon normal regulatory mechanisms to control their respirations. The hypoxic drive measures for low levels of oxygen in the bloodstream to increase respiratory rate.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The posterior tibial pulse can be palpated near the
questions.explanation: The posterior tibial pulse is assessed just below and posterior to where the ankle bone protrudes medially. The pulse located on the top of the foot is the dorsalis pedis. The popliteal pulse is located behind the knee.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Progressively deeper, faster breathing alternating gradually with shallow, slower breathing is called
questions.explanation: Biot’s breathing is an irregular pattern. Cheyne-Stokes respirations are regular and deep.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which patient should be transported immediately, with minimal on-scene care and any attempts at stabilization performed en route to the hospital?
options: female, age 28, systolic BP 96, respiratory rate 18
questions.explanation: Indications for immediate transport include any signs or symptoms of shock; sustained pulse rate greater than 120 or less than 50; systolic BP less than 90; and respiratory rate less than 10 or greater than 29. Based only on these vital signs, the first patient appears to already be in shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The collective change in vital signs associated with the late stages of increasing intracranial pressure consists of
options: increasing pulse rate, shallow respirations, increasing blood pressure.
slowing pulse rate, deep or erratic respirations, increasing blood pressure.
rapid and shallow pulse, deep respirations, decreasing blood pressure.
quickening pulse rate, shallow respirations, decreasing blood pressure.
questions.explanation: This change in vital signs comprises Cushing’s reflex, a sign of increasing intracranial pressure. Cushing’s reflex is also sometimes called Cushing’s triad or Cushing’s response.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: If your patient has an open abdominal wound with a loop of bowel obtruding, you should treat this with
options: a trauma dressing secured with triangular bandages.
an occlusive dressing secured on only three sides.
a wet sterile dressing and an occlusive dressing.
a clean gauze dressing secured with sterile tape.
questions.explanation: The most appropriate dressing for an evisceration is the application of a wet sterile dressing (which keeps the organs moist) and an occlusive dressing (which provides a barrier against further contamination and heat loss).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following patients is most critical in terms of age and mechanism of injury?
options: an 86-year-old female with a fractured clavicle
a 28-year-old male with a fractured femur
a 43-year-old female with a fractured rib
a 56-year-old male with a pelvic fracture
questions.explanation: Each fracture has a potential blood loss of one or more units per fracture site. Because of its ring shape, the pelvis frequently has two or more fractures present. In addition, nerve and blood vessel damage and injury to genitourinary organ injuries can complicate the severity of this injury. Patients with pelvis fractures are always considered high-priority patients and should be rapidly stabilized and transported. If a patient has bilateral femur fractures, he or she is also a high-priority patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unconscious patient who has one dilated pupil that is reactive to light is showing early signs of
questions.explanation: A unilaterally dilated pupil may be an early sign of increased intracranial pressure. As swelling increases in the brain, it puts pressure on the optic nerve that is located near the area of swelling.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Using your sense of touch during a physical examination is called
questions.explanation: The technique of palpation is using touch during a physical examination to gather information. Auscultation is listening with a stethoscope; percussion is using gentle tapping in order to identify the presence of air or fluid in body tissues; palpitations are heartbeat sensations that feel like your heart is pounding or racing.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A harsh upper-airway sound that can be heard when the patient inhales is called
questions.explanation: Stridor can usually be heard without a stethoscope and emanates from the area of the throat.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You suspect that a patient has a complete airway obstruction when he
options: can exhale only with significant effort.
questions.explanation: Choices c and d both indicate some air exchange. Choices a has no relation to the air passageway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: No breath sounds in one lung field may indicate which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: The other three choices, while serious, generally do not cause complete collapse of a lung.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Why is ventilation of the pediatric patient with a bag-valve mask more difficult than ventilating an adult?
options: The infant needs a lower concentration of oxygen.
It is more difficult to create a good seal in an infant.
The glottic opening is smaller in an infant.
questions.explanation: The bridge of the nose in a pediatric patient may make a mask seal more difficult to achieve. Additionally, the mask size needed to fit the pediatric patient’s face may not be available.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The pharyngo-tracheal lumen airway should be removed if the patient
questions.explanation: If the patient is unconscious and vomits, the PTL will help prevent aspiration. Instead, if a gag reflex returns, the PTL will have to be removed before the patient vomits.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What do orthostatic vital sign changes suggest for a patient with acute abdominal pain?
questions.explanation: A positive tilt test in a patient with acute abdominal pain suggests that the patient is hypovolemic and may have impending shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient with an acute abdomen who shows no signs of hemorrhage and has stable vital signs should be positioned
options: in whatever position is most comfortable for the patient.
in a fetal position with both legs bent.
in shock position with both lower legs -elevated.
sitting upright in a high Fowler’s position.
questions.explanation: Medical patients who are stable should be in a position of comfort.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Focused examination of the abdomen of a patient who is complaining of abdominal pain should consist of
options: auscultation of the area of discomfort.
gentle palpation of the entire abdomen.
questions.explanation: Use only gentle palpation in the field. Properly performed auscultation for bowel sounds takes several minutes and is of little value to your overall treatment regimen. Correctly performed percussion requires a relatively quiet environment and an experienced hand to be of any diagnostic value. Continued assessment for rebound tenderness will aggravate the patient’s discomfort and is unnecessary once you have determined the patient has abdominal distress.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 28-year-old diver who has been using scuba equipment. His diving partner states that he was unconscious when he surfaced after a dive. You should suspect
questions.explanation: Air embolism presents as neurological deficit (including unconsciousness) during or after ascent from a dive, or as sharp pain in the chest.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Drug dosages are lower in elderly patients than in young adults primarily because elderly patients
options: weigh less on average than younger patients.
have a slower rate of elimination of drugs.
forget they took their medication and overdose.
do not respond to drugs as well as the young.
questions.explanation: The dosage of many common medications is up to 50% lower in elderly adults primarily because of a decreased rate of elimination of the drug by the liver and kidneys.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which statement about the pain that accompanies a myocardial infarction is incorrect?
options: Patients often describe the pain as -“crushing.”
The pain is present only during exertion or stress.
The pain is relieved by sublingual -nitroglycerin.
Pain due to AMI radiates like anginal pain.
questions.explanation: The pain of MI is not generally relieved by sublingual nitroglycerin, and intravenous morphine or nitroglycerin, is usually necessary. It may have all of the same characteristics of angina, making a diagnosis by EMS providers relatively difficult.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which set of vital signs is consistent with left heart failure?
options: BP 100/60, P 48 and regular, R 8 and shallow
BP 130/80, P 68 and irregular, R 14 and -normal
BP 160/100, P 108 and irregular, R 26 and labored
BP 170/110, P 76 and irregular, R 22 and shallow
questions.explanation: A patient with left heart failure will present with elevated blood pressure, elevated and sometimes irregular pulse, and labored respirations.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following conditions would NOT result in an increase in a patient’s PaCO2?
questions.explanation: PaCO2 measures carbon dioxide levels in the blood, which are influenced by alterations in CO2 production or elimination. Such levels would be increased by physical exertion of muscles, by hypoventilation, or by an airway obstruction.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The volume of air normally inhaled and exhaled during each respiration is called the
questions.explanation: Tidal volume is the amount of air that moves into and out of the lungs during the respiratory cycle; minute volume is the total amount of air exchanged in the lungs in one minute; inspiratory reserve is the extra air that could be inspired in addition to the tidal volume; total lung capacity is the sum of the inspiratory reserve, tidal volume, expiratory reserve, and residual volume.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of an elderly female who is having difficulty breathing. She has a history of chronic congestive heart failure. Which vital sign pattern is most likely for this patient?
options: shallow rapid respirations; decreased pulse rate; cool, clammy skin
deep labored respirations; decreased pulse rate; hot, dry skin
shallow rapid respirations; increased pulse rate; cool, clammy skin
increased respiratory rate; decreased pulse rate; flushed, dry skin
questions.explanation: The vital signs given in this choice are most likely for a patient with CHF who is complaining of difficulty breathing.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are called to the home of an elderly female who is having difficulty breathing. She has a history of chronic congestive heart failure. Which of the following are common medications associated with patients with chronic CHF?
questions.explanation: Diuretics, potassium, and digoxin are the common medications used to treat CHF.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are called to the home of an elderly female who is having difficulty breathing. She has a history of chronic congestive heart failure. You auscultate the patient’s chest. What lung sounds would you would expect to hear from this patient?
options: basilar wheezes in all lung fields bilaterally
rales and/or rhonchi mainly in the lower lobes
clear but diminished sounds in the upper lobes
rubs with chest wall expansion in the bases
questions.explanation: The pulmonary edema associated with CHF commonly results in rales and/or rhonchi, especially in the lower lobes.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of an elderly female who is having difficulty breathing. She has a history of chronic congestive heart failure. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology of CHF?
options: cardiac muscle failure resulting in pulmonary edema
aortic valve failure resulting in pulmonary edema
superior vena cava failure resulting in pulmonary edema
questions.explanation: CHF results from the cardiac muscle’s inability to pump efficiently.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the purpose of performing the Sellick’s maneuver?
options: to visualize the upper-airway structures during BVM
to prevent the tongue from blocking the airway
to protect a patient with possible spinal injury
to prevent vomiting during attempts at -intubation
questions.explanation: Sellick’s maneuver is used to prevent patients from vomiting during intubation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Overinflating the pilot balloon in an endotracheal tube can cause
questions.explanation: Excessive pressure against the delicate soft tissue of the lower airway may reduce blood flow to the site, causing ischemia.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: To ensure proper placement of the endotracheal tube, you should
options: confirm placement of the tube by two different methods.
suction the end of the tube and observe for vomitus or blood.
check breath sounds in the chest before and after placement.
visualize the open glottis and remove stylet before tube placement.
questions.explanation: To ensure proper placement, always confirm by two different methods: After watching the tube pass through the vocal cords, assess the chest for breath sounds in numerous locations and chest expansion, then check the proximal end of the tube for breath condensation. You may also use one of several commercial confirmation devices that monitor end-tidal CO2 or provide an audible whistling sound to confirm air movement.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The digital intubation method is used for patients who
options: are very old or very young.
questions.explanation: Because the digital method does not require hyperextending the patient’s neck, it is used for patients with suspected spinal or cervical injury.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Distended neck veins, diminishing unilateral breath sounds, and progressively worsening compliance are indications of
questions.explanation: Excessive pressure inside the thoracic cavity due to the tension pneumothorax will result in all of the described symptoms.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: When suctioning a patient, you should always
options: begin suctioning after the catheter is placed in the airway.
limit suctioning attempts to no more than 45 seconds each.
hyperventilate the patient after every three suction attempts.
insert the catheter while the suctioning apparatus is turned on.
questions.explanation: Attempts at suctioning should be limited to no more than 5–10 seconds (depending upon the level of consciousness). You should ventilate the patient after each attempt, and you should not turn on the apparatus until the catheter is placed properly. In the case of a suction catheter that has a hole in the system that allows you to control if suction is being applied or not by occluding the opening, you should only suction upon withdrawal. This system may remain turned on at all times as long as you monitor closely when suction is actually being applied to the patient.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient exhibits cold, clammy skin; air hunger; distended neck veins; tracheal displacement; and absent breath sounds on one side. You should suspect
questions.explanation: The signs and symptoms of tension pneumothorax, the presence of air in the pleural space and medialstinal shifting, are listed.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What are the signs of circulatory overload in a patient who is receiving IV fluids?
questions.explanation: Dyspnea, rales, and rhonchi are classic signs of fluid overload, which is usually first manifested as pulmonary edema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is your first action for an adult patient who is conscious but who has a complete airway obstruction?
options: Deliver rapid abdominal thrusts until cleared or unconsciousness results.
Use the jaw-thrust/chin-lift technique to confirm the obstruction.
Pinch the patient’s nostrils and attempt to give two ventilations.
Ask the patient to lie down on the ground and attempt finger sweeps.
questions.explanation: For the conscious patient, your first action would be abdominal thrusts.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the treatment for someone who is suffering an exacerbation of either emphysema or chronic bronchitis and is not too hypoxic?
options: Transport this patient to the hospital rapidly, as there is little care that can be rendered for this condition in the prehospital setting.
Administer high-flow oxygen, establish an IV lifeline, place the patient on an EKG monitor, and administer bronchodilators.
Establish an airway, position the patient seated or semi-seated, administer low-flow oxygen, establish an IV lifeline, and transport.
Establish an airway, administer oxygen at the highest possible concentration, establish an IV lifeline, and transport rapidly.
questions.explanation: Low-flow oxygen is appropriate for this patient if he or she is not too hypoxic. If a patient with emphysema or chronic bronchitis is hypoxic, he or she needs more oxygen.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the most commonly used drug in the prehospital setting for patients with asthma?
questions.explanation: Albuterol, a bronchodilator sold under the trade names Proventil and Ventolin, is frequently given via inhaler or nebulizer in the field.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which one of the following methods is recommended when measuring respiratory rate?
options: Use a Wright Meter to determine peak expiratory flow rate.
Tell the patient to remain quiet while you count his or her respirations.
Carry on a conversation with the patient to distract him or her while you count.
Count respirations while pretending to take a redial pulse.
questions.explanation: Place your hand on the patient’s wrist as if you were measuring his or her pulse and count for 30 seconds. This will prevent the patient from consciously changing the respiratory rate. Placing the wrist and hand over the patient’s chest wall is called the pledge of allegiance method.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the best treatment for this patient?
options: Perform abdominal thrusts without back blows.
Administer back blows and chest thrusts.
Do nothing as the patient is moving air.
questions.explanation: AHA standards dictate that a conscious patient with a partial obstructed airway should be dealt with by encouraging coughing and continuous monitoring of patient status. Interventions like the Heimlich maneuver are considered -counterproductive, as they may actually worsen the obstruction.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: To perform a needle cricothyrotomy, you should place the patient
options: supine with head and neck in a slightly flexed position.
in the lateral recumbent position with head and neck hyperextended.
in the lateral recumbent position with head and neck in neutral position.
supine with head and neck hyperextended.
questions.explanation: A hyperextended position will place the anatomical structures into a position that can be easily identified by palpation.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following conditions best suggests respiratory failure?
questions.explanation: A patient in respiratory distress is compensating for the underlying condition, thereby preserving oxygenation to the brain. Once compensatory mechanisms have collapsed, the loss of gas exchange at the brain will result in a change in mental status.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following can complicate ventilation in a pediatric patient?
options: a bag-valve mask without a pop-off valve
ventilatory pressure that is higher than is used for adults
questions.explanation: Hyperextending the neck of a small child may result in a unintentional closure of the airway, due to the softer cartilage rings supporting the trachea.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: One breathing pattern is characterized by periods of apnea followed by periods in which respirations first increase then decrease in both depth and frequency. This pattern is called
questions.explanation: Cheyne-Stokes respirations are characterized by periods of apnea lasting 10–60 seconds, followed by periods in which respirations gradually decrease, then increase, in depth and rate.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What condition is the pathophysiological result of near drowning in sea water?
questions.explanation: Because sea water is hypertonic, fluid is drawn from the bloodstream into the alveoli, causing -pulmonary edema. Because of this, all -near-drowning patients should be hospitalized and monitored for a short time.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond along with fire units to the scene of a structure fire. Firefighters have rescued a 25-year-old female who is unconscious and unresponsive to verbal or painful stimuli. The victim was located in a smoke-filled bedroom on the floor above the actual fire. Vital signs are: blood pressure 146/80; pulse 128, strong and regular; and respiratory rate 40. The ECG monitor shows sinus tachycardia. Auscultation reveals generally clear lung sounds and mild expiratory wheezing. You note no burn injuries to the skin or clothing. What would account for the patient’s level of consciousness?
options: She has a drug overdose resulting in -unconsciousness.
She has heat stroke from the hot -environment.
She is suffering from carbon monoxide -poisoning.
She is suffering from carbon dioxide -poisoning.
questions.explanation: This victim of smoke inhalation is exhibiting the classic signs and symptoms of carbon monoxide poisoning.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond along with fire units to the scene of a structure fire. Firefighters have rescued a 25-year-old female who is unconscious and unresponsive to verbal or painful stimuli. The victim was located in a smoke-filled bedroom on the floor above the actual fire. Vital signs are: blood pressure 146/80; pulse 128, strong and regular; and respiratory rate 40. The ECG monitor shows sinus tachycardia. Auscultation reveals generally clear lung sounds and mild expiratory wheezing. You note no burn injuries to the skin or clothing. Pulse oximetry readings should be scrutinized closely because elevated
options: carbon monoxide levels can cause an inaccurately high reading of the percentage of oxygen saturation.
carbon monoxide levels can cause an inaccurately low reading of the percentage of oxygen saturation.
carbon dioxide levels can cause an inaccurately high reading of the percentage of oxygen saturation.
carbon dioxide levels can cause an inaccurately low reading of the percentage of oxygen saturation.
questions.explanation: This is a common finding with the use of pulse oximetry in carbon monoxide poisoning. The CO molecule, which has a high affinity for hemoglobin, is bound to the molecule and is present for detection as “oxygen” in blood passing through the capillary beds, resulting in fairly normal pulse oximetry readings. However, little, if any, of the oxygen is off-loaded from the hemoglobin molecule for use by the tissues.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond along with fire units to the scene of a structure fire. Firefighters have rescued a 25-year-old female who is unconscious and unresponsive to verbal or painful stimuli. The victim was located in a smoke-filled bedroom on the floor above the actual fire. Vital signs are: blood pressure 146/80; pulse 128, strong and regular; and respiratory rate 40. The ECG monitor shows sinus tachycardia. Auscultation reveals generally clear lung sounds and mild expiratory wheezing. You note no burn injuries to the skin or clothing. Treatment for carbon monoxide poisoning should include which of the following?
questions.explanation: If a hyperbaric chamber is available, this patient should be transported there immediately for -“diving.” Hyperbaric oxygen can dramatically improve outcomes for CO poisoning by reducing the time it takes for the CO molecule to unbind from the hemoglobin molecule. Low-flow oxygen and sodium bicarbonate are not indicated for carbon monoxide poisoning. The patient is unconscious, so position of comfort is not an issue. If you suspect the possibility of spinal injury, you should immobilize this patient to the long backboard.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond along with fire units to the scene of a structure fire. Firefighters have rescued a 25-year-old female who is unconscious and unresponsive to verbal or painful stimuli. The victim was located in a smoke-filled bedroom on the floor above the actual fire. Vital signs are: blood pressure 146/80; pulse 128, strong and regular; and respiratory rate 40. The ECG monitor shows sinus tachycardia. Auscultation reveals generally clear lung sounds and mild expiratory wheezing. You note no burn injuries to the skin or clothing. Signs of CO exposure include all of the following except
questions.explanation: Hyperactivity is not a sign of carbon monoxide exposure. Generally, patients are lethargic due to being hypoxic.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond along with fire units to the scene of a structure fire. Firefighters have rescued a 25-year-old female who is unconscious and unresponsive to verbal or painful stimuli. The victim was located in a smoke-filled bedroom on the floor above the actual fire. Vital signs are: blood pressure 146/80; pulse 128, strong and regular; and respiratory rate 40. The ECG monitor shows sinus tachycardia. Auscultation reveals generally clear lung sounds and mild expiratory wheezing. You note no burn injuries to the skin or clothing. Which of the following is a common source of CO?
questions.explanation: Engine exhaust is a common source of carbon monoxide. Improperly ventilated space heaters are another source. Cellular metabolism and cellular respiration result in the formation of carbon dioxide.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You have arrived on scene to find a 75-year-old female in respiratory distress. Your assessment reveals: BP 138/90, pulse 136/minute, and RR
questions.explanation: Because the patient appears fatigued, respiratory failure is imminent. Inadequate tidal volume may not permit good gas exchange without manual support.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is a disease that is associated with cigarette smoking and is related to, but distinct from, emphysema?
questions.explanation: In addition to emphysema, chronic bronchitis is associated with cigarette smoking. Either condition can lead to CHF. A simple pneumo-thorax can be caused by cigarette smoking, especially in young and thin males, but the disease process is unrelated to emphysema.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is the primary drug for the management of acute anaphylaxis?
questions.explanation: To manage acute anaphylaxis, epinephrine is the first medication used, followed by diphenhydramine. Epinephrine is a potent antihistamine and immediately reverses the physiological effects of the reaction (vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, and airway swelling), while diphenhydramine slows and stops the reaction itself.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You have just started an IV lifeline, but the fluid is not flowing properly. What is the first thing you should do to troubleshoot this situation?
options: Remove the cannula and try another site.
Make sure the constricting band has been removed.
Ensure that the right-size drip-set is attached.
Lower the IV bag below the level of the patient’s arm.
questions.explanation: Proper flow cannot be achieved if the constricting band (tourniquet) is not removed.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following patients would benefit most from the application of the PASG/MAST?
options: 10-year-old male, suspected spinal fracture, no blood loss
72-year-old female, suspected cardiogenic shock, no blood loss
40-year-old male, suspected lower extremity fracture, low blood pressure
67-year-old female, suspected ankle sprain, high blood pressure
questions.explanation: Indications for use of the PASG are to control bleeding, stabilize fractures, and raise blood pressure. Although its use is currently controversial, of the choices listed, choice c has the indications called for if PASG is to be used.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Care for the patient with cardiac contusion is similar to care for the patient with which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Patients with cardiac contusion can present with the symptoms of myocardial infarction, including life-threatening dysrhythmias. Care is similar to care of any cardiac patient.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which statement about use of Nitronox in the field is correct?
options: Nitronox is a short-acting agent that is administered via inhalation.
Nitronox is used to manage pain in patients with COPD and asthma.
Nitronox may be given to patients with acute abdominal distension.
Nitronox may be safely used for patients with head injury.
questions.explanation: Nitronox is a short-acting agent that the patient self-administers by mask. Its use is limited to patients who do not have the potential for having air-filled spaces within their tissues, thus eliminating patients with COPD, pneumothoracies, or air-filled abdominal organs.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The most common cause of upper-airway obstruction is
questions.explanation: The lost of lingual control during unconsciousness occurs more commonly than the other conditions.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding treatment for the care of a patient who is suffering from complications of dialysis is correct?
options: If possible, obtain a blood pressure reading on the arm on which the shunt is located.
Watch for narrow complex tachycardia to develop as the patient becomes hypoxic.
Monitoring for dysrhythmias is frequently unnecessary in a hemodialysis patient.
To prevent exacerbation of the problem, start an IV only if ordered by medical -control.
questions.explanation: Fluid administration in dialysis patients should be under the direct authority of medical control. Dysrhythmias are common and if present, are generally caused by electrolyte imbalances. To prevent accidental damage to the shunt, a BP should never be assessed on the arm with the shunt.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Knee, femur, and hip dislocation and fracture caused by the knees hitting the firewall and absorbing initial impact are common in frontal collisions with a
questions.explanation: “Up and over” refers to injuries sustained in the head, neck, and chest region. In the “down and under” pathway, the body slides forward and downward. This can be limited by the correct use of seat belts.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A single-lead ECG tracing is useful for obtaining information about the heart. Which of the following can be determined from a single-lead ECG tracing?
options: the presence of a myocardial infarct
questions.explanation: A single-lead ECG, used for routine monitoring, can be used to determine the heart rate, -regularity, and the length of time it takes for the impulse to travel through the heart. It tells you nothing about the mechanical response of the heart, of which stroke volume is a part. You need additional lead views to verify the presence of an MI.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What does the actual drawing of the QRS complex on an ECG tracing show?
options: impulse travel through the atrioventricular junction
questions.explanation: The QRS complex reflects the underlying ventricular depolarization and buried within it is the artial repolarization.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A QRS complex is considered abnormal if it lasts longer than how many seconds?
questions.explanation: The QRS complex normally lasts 0.04 to 0.12 seconds; anything longer than 0.12 seconds is considered to be abnormal.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What does the treatment for a patient whose ECG shows premature atrial contractions include?
options: observation only as long as the patient remains asymptomatic
vagal maneuvers and 6 mg adenosine rapid IV push over 1–3 seconds
1–1.5 mg/kg lidocaine via slow IV push, and consider sedation
questions.explanation: If the patient is asymptomatic, this arrhythmia requires observation only. Other treatments described are for patients with varying degrees of supraventricular tachycardia.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Traumatic aortic rupture usually occurs as a result of transection of the aorta at the
questions.explanation: Ligamentum arteriosum is the most common area for an aortic rupture secondary to trauma.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the clinical significance of a firstdegree AV block?
options: It signals the onset of rapid cardiovascular decompensation.
It indicates that the heart rate may drop if action is not taken.
It can lead to syncope and angina if not corrected quickly.
It may foreshadow development of a more advanced dysrhythmia.
questions.explanation: First-degree AV block in itself calls for observation only; however, it may indicate the -development of a more advanced heart block.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome is characterized by which of the following waveform abnormalities?
options: short P-R interval and long QRS complex
inverted P waves and normal QRS complex
questions.explanation: Wolff-Parkinson-White, a pre-excitation syndrome, is characterized by a short P-R interval and lengthened QRS complex. Often, a delta wave is present as well. This condition occurs in 3 of 1,000 individuals.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: In which situation would you consider having the patient perform a Valsalva maneuver to slow the heart rate?
questions.explanation: When PJT is caused by stress or excessive caffeine intake in a patient with no history of heart disease, the Valsalva maneuver can be successful at slowing the heart rate.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which rhythm is likely to foreshadow the development of other, more serious dys-rhythmias?
questions.explanation: Because the underlying cause is usually ischemia, accelerated junctional rhythm can deteriorate into more serious dysrhythmias.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What does a prolonged sinus tachycardia accompanying an acute myocardial infarction suggest?
options: Damage to the heart is minimal.
The diagnosis of MI is incorrect.
questions.explanation: In a patient with acute MI, sinus tachycardia suggests that cardiogenic shock may develop.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: How should you position the patient to check for jugular vein distention?
options: lying flat on his or her back
questions.explanation: Check for jugular vein distention with the patient elevated at a 45° angle. Most patients will have observable jugular veins when supine.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What does a carotid artery bruit indicate?
questions.explanation: A bruit, noisy blood flow in a vessel, indicates partial obstruction due to plaque buildup or the presence of an embolus.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Hyperextension of the neck, followed by hyperflexion, is common in
questions.explanation: A strong force striking the car from behind causes the head to move backward. If the head restraint is not properly placed, it can actually act as a fulcrum, causing the neck to hyperextend. Then the head snaps forward, with the chin pointing toward the chest. This results in a severe hyperflexion of the neck.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The pain of stable angina is brought on by
questions.explanation: Attacks of stable angina are brought on by exercise or by stress and are usually easily managed.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: In the care of a cardiac arrest patient, epinephrine is used to
options: decrease the container size to increase BP.
increase the effects of the other drugs.
questions.explanation: Epinephrine is used to coarsen dysrhythmias such as ventricular fibrillation, improving the chance of converting to a more viable rhythm.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient’s signs and symptoms include orthopnea, spasmodic coughing, agitation, cyanosis, rales, jugular vein distention, and elevated blood pressure, pulse, and respirations. What condition should you suspect?
questions.explanation: Listed are classic signs and symptoms of left heart failure with pulmonary edema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is exhibiting the signs and symptoms of right-sided heart failure. There is no evidence of left-sided failure. In addition to ECG monitoring and high-flow oxygen, what additional treatments should he or she receive?
options: IV of D5W with minidrip set, dopamine, and norepinephrine, and rapid transport
IV of D5W with macrodrip set, morphine sulfate, and furosemide, and rapid transport
IV of D5W with minidrip set, monitor vital signs closely, and normal transport
IV of D5W with macrodrip set, shock position, PASG, and normal transport
questions.explanation: Right heart failure, unless it is accompanied by left heart failure with pulmonary edema, is not a true medical emergency and does not require rapid transport.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is sodium nitroprusside (Nipride) used in the treatment of?
questions.explanation: Sodium nitroprusside is used in the treatment of hypertensive emergency.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following drugs is an antidysrhythmic agent?
questions.explanation: Lidocaine is a first-line drug in the treatment of dysrhythmias.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: In performing emergency synchronized cardioversion, you would synchronize the electrical shock with which of the following wave forms?
questions.explanation: Emergency synchronized cardioversion is synchronized with the R wave in order to avoid -firing during the relative refractory period, which could induce ventricular tachycardia.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The P wave on an ECG strip reflects what event inside the heart?
questions.explanation: The P wave reflects atrial depolarization at the beginning of the cardiac cycle.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What does treatment for a patient who is experiencing stable angina consist of?
options: oxygen and defibrillation if the QRS complex is wide
reassurance, oxygen, ECG monitoring, and morphine sulfate
seating patient upright and starting IV, ECG, and furosemide
questions.explanation: The correct protocol for stable angina is rest, oxygen, and nitroglycerin.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The wheezing associated with left-sided heart failure results from
questions.explanation: The wheezing, due to bronchoconstriction of smooth muscle in the lung, is a reaction to fluid in the lung spaces.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A patient is found in a back bedroom lying supine on the bed with a hunting knife embedded in her anterior chest, midline below the right breast. The patient is in obvious respiratory distress. She is cold, clammy, and diaphoretic with flat neck veins. You hear no breath sounds on the right side. This patient most likely is suffering from
questions.explanation: Flat neck veins while the patient is supine indicate a lower than normal pressure inside the vasculature, most likely due to blood loss. This would indicate a hemothorax as the primary cause of the described signs and symptoms.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A primary reason for administering oxygen to a patient with AMI is to
questions.explanation: Oxygen can help limit the size of the infarct by increasing oxygen delivery to the heart -muscle.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for an adult patient who has a head injury and is seizing?
options: nasal intubation with a 6.5 endotracheal tube
direct laryngoscopy and intubation with a 7.5 endotracheal tube
questions.explanation: Diaxepam given through the IM route is ineffective. Oral intubation may be difficult to achieve through clenched teeth. Nasal intubation may raise intracranial pressure even higher.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to a 22-year-old male who is complaining of rapid onset of chest pain. The patient states that the pain is tearing and sharp and that it started when he surfaced from a scuba dive from 60 feet down. The patient’s diving partner states that the patient surfaced too rapidly. What is this patient most likely suffering from?
questions.explanation: A too-rapid ascent from a scuba dive may result in a pulmonary embolism due to lung overinflation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to a 22-year-old male who is complaining of rapid onset of chest pain. The patient states that the pain is tearing and sharp and that it started when he surfaced from a scuba dive from 60 feet down. The patient’s diving partner states that the patient surfaced too rapidly. What does treatment for this patient consist of?
options: IV, high-flow oxygen, and rapid transport to the nearest emergency department
IV, high-flow oxygen, and rapid transport to a recompression chamber
IV, orotracheal intubation, and transport to the nearest emergency department
IV, orotracheal intubation, and transport to a recompression chamber
questions.explanation: An IV, 100% oxygen via a nonrebreather mask, and transport to a recompression chamber are essential for this patient.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to a 22-year-old male who is complaining of rapid onset of chest pain. The patient states that the pain is tearing and sharp and that it started when he surfaced from a scuba dive from 60 feet down. The patient’s diving partner states that the patient surfaced too rapidly. Due to his rapid ascent, this patient may also be suffering from which other diving-related emergency?
questions.explanation: Due to the depth of the dive and the rapid ascent, this patient may also be suffering from decompression sickness.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to a 22-year-old male who is complaining of rapid onset of chest pain. The patient states that the pain is tearing and sharp and that it started when he surfaced from a scuba dive from 60 feet down. The patient’s diving partner states that the patient surfaced too rapidly. What is an additional possible problem associated with this injury?
options: an increased level of carbon dioxide in the interstitial spaces
nitrogen bubbles entering tissue spaces and smaller blood vessels
an increase in oxygen levels in the trachealbronchial tree
excess accumulation of lactic acid collecting in the alveolar space
questions.explanation: In this patient, nitrogen gas bubbles may have entered tissue spaces and blood vessels.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The normal gestational period is
questions.explanation: Full-term delivery usually occurs within the 40th week of pregnancy.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: During delivery, a loop of umbilical cord presents from the birth canal. You should
options: cover the cord with a moist and sterile dressing.
have the mother stand to assist in delivery.
clamp the cord if possible and cut it.
reinsert the cord into the vaginal opening.
questions.explanation: Covering the exposed cord will minimize drying of the cord. Additionally, you should try to insert two fingers into the birth canal and try to keep pressure from the baby’s head away from the cord.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: For which of the following conditions would you be likely to receive an order to administer intravenous thiamine?
questions.explanation: Intravenous thiamine is used to reverse the effects of acute thiamine deficiency, which may lead to seizures and encephalopathy in alcoholics.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: During delivery, you notice that the amniotic fluid is discolored and has a foul odor. What should you do first?
options: Suction the upper airway using a meconium aspirator.
Intubate the child and give positive pressure ventilations.
Dry, warm, position, suction, and stimulate the child to breathe.
Provide five back blows and then five chest thrusts.
questions.explanation: Suction the newborn before stimulating it to breathe. Endotracheal suctioning may be warranted if meconium is noted in the upper airway.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: How should you control bleeding after the normal delivery of an infant?
options: Pack the vagina with sterile gauze.
Apply direct pressure to the genitalia.
questions.explanation: Massaging the top of the uterus stimulates it to contract, and promotes control of normal postpartum bleeding.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: A greenstick fracture is one that is
questions.explanation: A greenstick fracture is a partial fracture that is on only one side of the long bone. These fractures are noted most frequently in children but may also be seen in adults.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What should you do to care for a patient with a suspected pelvic fracture?
options: Apply the PASG/MAST, titrate two IVs to effect, and monitor for signs of shock.
Immobilize the patient to a long spine board, start two IVs, and transport immediately.
Gently align lower limbs, soft splint with a blanket and cravat, and administer analgesics.
Do not immobilize, monitor distal pulse and sensation, and transport immediately.
questions.explanation: This is the current field treatment regimen for pelvic fractures. The PASG/MAST is used as an air-splint to contain the fractured pelvis and prevent further injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient has a chemical burn to her face and eyes. How should you treat this?
options: Cover the eyes and face with a dry sterile dressing.
Apply a paste made of baking soda and alcohol to the eyes.
Apply a neutralizing agent to counteract any chemical reaction.
Brush the chemical away and then flush the area with copious amounts of clean cool water.
questions.explanation: The correct treatment for most chemical burns is to flush the area with cool water immediately and to continue this treatment even during transport.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The first step in managing a burn patient is to
options: estimate the percentage and degree of burns.
prevent contamination by applying sterile burn dressings.
questions.explanation: The first step in managing a burn is to stop the burning process or the burn will continue to destroy more tissue. Then attention should turn toward ensuring and maintaining an adequate airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A burn wound that blisters is an example of a
questions.explanation: Blister formation is characteristic of seconddegree burns.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An adult who has burns over both sides of one arm and both sides of one leg would be estimated to have total burns over what percentage of body surface area?
questions.explanation: Using the rule of nines, this patient has burns over 27% of her body surface area (both sides of one arm = 9%; both sides of one leg = 18%).
questions.subject: Trauma
