questions.text: Your patient is a comatose 56-year-old male. His breath smells fruity and sweet, and his respirations are very deep and rapid. After the initial assessment, you should provide which of the following treatments?
options: Draw blood, start an IV of 0.9% NaCl, and give a 500 ml fluid bolus.
Draw blood, start an IV of normal saline, and administer IM glucagon.
Administer oxygen, start an IV of D5W, and transport immediately.
Administer oxygen, monitor vital signs, and give 25 gm IV Dextrose 50%.
questions.explanation: This patient is showing signs and symptoms of diabetic ketoacidosis. Avoid the use of glucose administration if at all possible. At the minimum, you should obtain a glucometer reading before adminstering any glucosecontaining solutions. The fluid bolus will help dilute the glucose contained within his blood.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Signs of hypoglycemia include which of the following?
options: nausea and vomiting; tachycardia; abdominal pain; deep, rapid respirations
weak, rapid pulse; cold, clammy skin; headache; irritability; coma
shallow, rapid breathing; low blood pressure; warm, dry skin; irritability
decreased blood pressure, pulse, and respirations; loss of consciousness
questions.explanation: This option lists the classic signs of hypoglycemia.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Naloxone is given to patients who are suspected of having which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Naloxone (Narcan) is a narcotic antagonist given to patients suspected of narcotic ingestion.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Patients who are found in a hazardous-materials incident should be initially treated in which containment zone?
questions.explanation: Major decontamination and treatment for life-threatening conditions should be conducted by properly protected personnel in the warm zone.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient presents with symptoms of flushing, itching, hives, difficulty breathing, decreased blood pressure, and dizziness. What should you suspect?
questions.explanation: Hives, accompanied by difficulty breathing, strongly suggest anaphylaxis.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: After administration of epinephrine, what may be given to help epinephrine stop an allergic reaction?
questions.explanation: Epinephrine is the first-line drug for patients with severe allergic reactions. Oxygen, albuterol, and turbutaline are all respiratory drugs, but they do not have any antihistamine properties. Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) is an antihistamine and will stop the production of histamine, the chemical responsible for the exaggerated immune response.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is the reason for giving inhaled beta agonists to patients with severe allergic reactions?
options: to increase heart rate and contractile force
to block histamine receptors so edema is lessened
to help suppress the inflammatory response
to reverse bronchospasm and relax airways
questions.explanation: Beta agonists such as albuterol help in the treatment of severe allergic reactions by relaxing the airway and thus relieving bronchospasm.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A patient is covered with alpha-radioactive material after an accidental spill. An adequate level of shielding would be
questions.explanation: Alpha particles carry very little energy. A uniform is adequate protection against such a contaminant.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What are the classic symptoms of narcotic overdose?
options: altered mental status, euphoria, and dilated pupils
questions.explanation: Respiratory depression and constricted pupils (“pinpoint pupils”) are classic symptoms of narcotic overdose.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a farmer who has employed a crop duster to spray his fields.The fields were sprayed earlier today and now the farmer has teary eyes, nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, and excessive salivation. What was he most likely poisoned with?
questions.explanation: The symptoms of organophosphate absorption are described by the acronym SLUDGE: excessive salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, gastrointestinal distress, emesis.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Shivering ceases in a hypothermic patient when the body temperature drops below
questions.explanation: Shivering is the body’s attempt to regulate body temperature. Shivering continues until the core body temperature reaches about 86° F. Lack of shivering in a hypothermic patient indicates significant hypothermia.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which patient presents the signs and symptoms of moderate hypothermia?
questions.explanation: Moderate hypothermia is characterized by a core temperature between 86° F and 94° F.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the correct field treatment for a frostbitten body part?
options: Transport the patient to the hospital.
Rub the affected part with crushed ice or snow until warmed.
Warm the affected part in water maintained at 100–106° F before transporting.
Cover the frozen part tightly in wet occlusive dressings.
questions.explanation: The correct treatment is gradual warming in a water bath maintained between 100° F and 106° F, although this treatment should not be attempted in the field because of the danger of refreezing. Pain management is essential because the procedure is extremely painful.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following areas of physical examination of an elderly patient is often the most difficult to accurately perform?
questions.explanation: Assessing the mental status of an elderly patient is often difficult. It is often necessary to enlist the help of family and caregivers to accurately determine if a patient’s mental status is different from the norm.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Signs and symptoms of radiation sickness include
questions.explanation: Hair loss, nausea, and vomiting are common signs and symptoms of radiation sickness.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 46-year-old male with a long history of mental illness. He appears depressed and withdrawn. Suddenly, he begins to sob uncontrollably. What should you do?
options: Administer a sedative to help him calm his emotions.
Attempt to calm him by putting your arms around him.
Maintain a quiet, listening, and -non-judgmental attitude.
Motion to your partner to go to the unit and get the restraints.
questions.explanation: Allow the patient to express emotion and do not interrupt his expression with questions or comments. You should always be on your guard for safety issues when on a scene, but restraints are not necessary in this situation. It would not be advisable to touch this patient too closely as this may be perceived by him as an invasion of his space or privacy and is not a safe gesture.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Depression is an example of a(n)
questions.explanation: Depression is a mood disorder; depressed patients feel hopeless and helpless and manifest many physical symptoms as well.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What would be the reason for a paramedic to give Benadryl (diphenhydramine) to a schizophrenic patient in the field?
options: to help control his or her manic symptoms
to raise blood pressure and respiratory rate
questions.explanation: Benadryl is used to counter extrapyramidal reactions in patients who are taking antipsychotic drugs. Effects noted are involuntary movement, changes in muscle tone, and abnormal posture.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following represents a significant mechanism of injury?
options: A small child is in a 40 mph car accident.
An adult falls from a 6-foot-high ledge.
An adult pedestrian is hit by a bicycle.
A child falls from a 6-foot-high ledge.
questions.explanation: A 40 mph car accident is a significant mechanism of injury for a child but not for an adult unless other significant findings (lack of seatbelts, another person killed in the same car) are present.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 10-year-old boy who has fallen off his bicycle. What should you do when obtaining the history of the accident?
options: Insist on speaking only to the responsible adult.
Suspect child abuse until the possibility is eliminated.
Obtain as much information as possible from the child.
questions.explanation: With a child of this age, obtain as much information as possible from the patient himor herself; this will allow the child to feel respected and mature. The adult caretaker can fill in relevant details.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You arrive to find a 6-year-old boy on the floor of his classroom, unconscious, incontinent, and responsive to pain only. The school nurse states that the child shook violently for approximately two minutes and has been unconscious ever since. She knows that he takes phenobarbital because she gave him one at lunch, but she is unable to provide further medical history. This child most likely suffers from
questions.explanation: The clinical presentation is one of seizures that could occur for a variety of reasons, including diabetes. However, the use of the drug phenobarbital is commonly associated with seizure disorders.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You arrive to find a 6-year-old boy on the floor of his classroom, unconscious, incontinent, and responsive to pain only. The school nurse states that the child shook violently for approximately two minutes and has been unconscious ever since. She knows that he takes phenobarbital because she gave him one at lunch, but she is unable to provide further medical history. Phenobarbital is an example of what class or type of medication?
questions.explanation: Phenobarbital is a sedative/anticonvulsant.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You arrive to find a 6-year-old boy on the floor of his classroom, unconscious, incontinent, and responsive to pain only. The school nurse states that the child shook violently for approximately two minutes and has been unconscious ever since. She knows that he takes phenobarbital because she gave him one at lunch, but she is unable to provide further medical history. If this child is on medication, why did he have this episode at school?
options: Medications only limit the number of seizures a person has; they do not always eliminate the seizures.
Medication for this disorder does not control the amount of glucose available for cellular metabolism.
This child may have had too much to eat, overriding the medication’s ability to regulate blood sugar.
The school nurse must have been mistaken in administering the medication to the child at lunch.
questions.explanation: Anticonvulsants serve to limit the number of seizures a patient has, but they do not stop them from occuring altogether.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You arrive to find a 6-year-old boy on the floor of his classroom, unconscious, incontinent, and responsive to pain only. The school nurse states that the child shook violently for approximately two minutes and has been unconscious ever since. She knows that he takes phenobarbital because she gave him one at lunch, but she is unable to provide further medical history. Treatment for this patient should include
questions.explanation: The medications listed in choices a, b, and c are not indicated for postictal patients who have stopped seizing.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You arrive to find a 6-year-old boy on the floor of his classroom, unconscious, incontinent, and responsive to pain only. The school nurse states that the child shook violently for approximately two minutes and has been unconscious ever since. She knows that he takes phenobarbital because she gave him one at lunch, but she is unable to provide further medical history. This patient should be transported to a hospital because
options: he needs a lumbar puncture to determine if he has meningitis.
medication levels need to be determined by laboratory analysis.
glucose levels need to be determined by laboratory analysis.
questions.explanation: This patient needs to be transported to a hospital because medication levels should be assessed by laboratory methods. Seizures may or may not continue to occur.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The ideal helicopter landing zone should
options: contain a slope of no more than 15°.
be an area of at least 100 feet by 100 feet.
be an area of at least 90 feet by 90 feet.
be in a fenced area for safety against onlookers.
questions.explanation: The ideal helicopter landing zone should be at least 100 feet by 100 feet square. Larger helicopters may require more room, so consult local air medical services if you are unsure of the size of aircraft used in your area. The ground slope should be no more than 10° and the area should be clear of debris, fences, wires, or other obstructions.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the approximate weight of the average 6-year-old?
questions.explanation: The average 6-year-old weighs about 45 pounds.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is the paramedic’s primary goal in cases of suspected child abuse?
options: Gather up any physical evidence to take to the hospital.
Ensure the abuser is arrested upon arrival at the hospital.
Ensure that the child is removed from family custody.
Make sure that the child receives necessary treatment.
questions.explanation: In many states, medical personnel are legally required to report all cases of suspected abuse and neglect, but a paramedic’s first responsibility is to ensure that the child is transported to the hospital to receive necessary treatment.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: All pediatric patients who have had seizures should be
options: given diazepam rectally or IV or IM.
transported to a hospital for evaluation.
evaluated for signs of abuse or neglect.
questions.explanation: The cause of seizure activity can be determined only in the hospital.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is 10 months old. He has tachypnea and wheezing and a fever of 100.6° F. What do you suspect is wrong with your patient?
questions.explanation: Wheezing and tachypnea in a child younger than age 1 is most often due to bronchiolitis brought on by the RSV virus. Asthma in children this young often presents as coughing. Epiglottitis will often have a fever higher than 100.6° F, and the epiglottitis patient will be drooling as respiratory distress worsens. Croup will present with junky-sounding airways and the classic seal-barking cough.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Cardiac arrest in young children is most commonly associated with which of the following?
questions.explanation: Most childhood cardiac arrests are the result of preventable accidents that result in respiratory compromise.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Assessment and care of a patient who is a victim of sexual assault should include which of the following?
options: Ask detailed questions about the assault.
Place sterile dressings on any wounds.
Allow the patient to bathe and douche.
questions.explanation: Do not perform a vaginal exam, ask detailed questions about the assault in the field, or allow the patient to change clothes or bathe. You should not overly clean any wounds you encounter, but instead wrap them up with dry sterile dressings. Place any clothing or other evidence removed from the patient in a clean paper bag and take it with you to the hospital.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A patient begins to have a generalized seizure while running a marathon on a hot day. Which of the following procedures should you do first?
options: Move the patient into the ambulance.
Establish an airway and ventilate the patient.
Place cold packs around the neck and under the arms.
questions.explanation: While the other procedures are applicable to the treatment of a possible heat stroke victim, securing the airway and ensuring respirations should occur first.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which statement characterizes normal physiologic changes that occur in vital signs during pregnancy?
options: Blood pressure falls; pulse rate rises.
Blood pressure falls; pulse rate falls.
Blood pressure rises; pulse rate rises.
Blood pressure rises; pulse rate falls.
questions.explanation: During pregnancy, a woman’s blood pressure usually falls during the first two trimesters and her pulse rate rises.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are recording vital signs for a 34-year-old woman who is eight months pregnant. Her blood pressure is 100/70, pulse rate is 80, and respirations are 17 per minute and normal. Upon -auscultation of her chest, you hear a mild -systolic flow murmur. How should you treat this patient?
options: Transport her immediately to a facility with ob/gyn services for her imminent delivery.
Establish an IV, administer high-flow oxygen, and monitor vital signs every two minutes.
The murmur is not a finding of concern; document these findings on the patient-care report.
Continue to assess the patient for additional signs or symptoms of left-sided heart failure.
questions.explanation: A mild systolic murmur in a pregnant patient whose vital signs are normal is not a cause for concern.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 29-year-old woman who is nine months pregnant with her third child. She reports the onset of painless bright red vaginal bleeding in the last half hour. How should you treat this patient?
options: Perform a vaginal exam to determine if this is a placental abruption.
Check for crowning, perineal bulging, or other signs of impending delivery.
Wait for delivery and then transport both mother and child to the hospital.
Treat her for signs and symptoms of shock and transport immediately.
questions.explanation: Bright red bleeding in late pregnancy is assumed to be placenta previa, which is a true medical emergency that is life threatening to both mother and baby. Treat the mother for shock and transport immediately.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is a 35-year-old woman who is eight months pregnant. You note that her blood pressure is 140/90 and edema is present all over her body. The patient is anxious and complains of seeing spots and having a headache. From this information, what condition should you suspect is present?
questions.explanation: The patient shows signs and symptoms of preeclampsia (or toxemia of pregnancy) and should be transported to the hospital. The distinction between eclampsia and preeclampsia is the presence of seizures and/or coma.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are assisting in a delivery in the field. As the baby’s head is born, you realize that the umbilical cord is wrapped around the baby’s neck. What is your first step in the management of this problem?
options: Attempt to slip the cord over the baby’s head.
Apply two clamps and cut the cord -immediately.
Moisten the cord and transport immediately.
Position the patient as for a prolapsed cord.
questions.explanation: First, attempt to slip the cord over the baby’s head. If this is impossible, you should clamp the cord in two places and carefully cut it between the clamps, then continue with the delivery.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are assessing a neonate who has a pink body and blue extremities, a pulse rate of 90, positive grimace response, active motion, and irregular respiratory efforts. What is the Apgar score for this infant?
questions.explanation: The score would consist of one point each for appearance, pulse rate, grimace, and respiratory effort, and two points for activity.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the order of care for a newborn born with evidence of meconium staining?
options: Suction with the bulb syringe first, then remove remaining meconium under direct visualization.
Suction with bulb syringe, then follow that with resuscitation with the bag-valve-mask unit.
Report the presence of meconium to the medical control physician while administering oxygen.
Deep suction the newborn with a nasogastric tube, followed by administration of high-flow oxygen.
questions.explanation: Do not resuscitate or stimulate further until the meconium is cleared from the respiratory tree by direct visualization of the cords.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following characteristics is the criterion for administering positive pressure ventilations to a newborn?
options: Apnea was corrected with blow-by oxygen use.
The heart rate rose from 90 to 110 in two -minutes.
Central cyanosis persists while oxygen is given.
questions.explanation: Apnea and tachycardia may initially be present in the newborn, but once they are corrected, you do not need to continue to progress further into the inverted pyramid. Meconium staining is treated first with suction. The child may or may not require ventilatory support once it is cleared away.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to the home of a 2-year-old girl who is experiencing labored and difficult breathing. The child’s mother states that she has had a cold for the past several days and a seal-like bark for the past 20 minutes. Physical exam reveals she has a fever of 102° F and hot, dry skin. Inspiratory stridor is heard upon auscultation of lung sounds. Vital signs are blood pressure 100/70, pulse rate of 100, and respiratory rate of 40 that is labored and with sternal retractions noted. This patient is most likely suffering from
questions.explanation: This patient is exhibiting the classic signs and symptoms of croup.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to the home of a 2-year-old girl who is experiencing labored and difficult breathing. The child’s mother states that she has had a cold for the past several days and a seal-like bark for the past 20 minutes. Physical exam reveals she has a fever of 102° F and hot, dry skin. Inspiratory stridor is heard upon auscultation of lung sounds. Vital signs are blood pressure 100/70, pulse rate of 100, and respiratory rate of 40 that is labored and with sternal retractions noted. What is the appropriate treatment for this child?
options: examination of the oropharynx with a tongue blade
direct visualization of the vocal cords for swelling
nebulized albuterol and cooling measures en route
saline given by nebulizer treatment and -oxygen
questions.explanation: A nebulized saline mist is the appropriate treatment for croup. Do not interfere with the airway in case there is any tissue swelling present.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to the home of a 2-year-old girl who is experiencing labored and difficult breathing. The child’s mother states that she has had a cold for the past several days and a seal-like bark for the past 20 minutes. Physical exam reveals she has a fever of 102° F and hot, dry skin. Inspiratory stridor is heard upon auscultation of lung sounds. Vital signs are blood pressure 100/70, pulse rate of 100, and respiratory rate of 40 that is labored and with sternal retractions noted. A related disease or condition that can result in rapid and total airway obstruction is
questions.explanation: Epiglottitis, a condition whereby the patient’s airway can become totally obstructed, is related to croup.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The term ethics refers to
questions.explanation: Ethics refers to rules, standards, and morals that govern actions in a profession.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: A tiered response system is one that does which of the following?
options: dispatches responders at various levels, depending on the incident
dispatches ALS responders to arrive first to all medical emergencies
has ALS responders respond on fire trucks instead of ambulances
uses chase vehicles staffed with ALS providers to drive ambulances
questions.explanation: In a tiered system, responders are dispatched to calls depending upon the nature of the incident as stated by the 911 caller and evaluated by the dispatcher.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A victim is unresponsive after possible exposure to carbon monoxide in a closed garage. Which of the following procedures should you do first?
options: Wait for properly trained personnel to enter and evacuate the garage.
Open the windows of the garage to ventilate the environment.
Provide high-flow oxygen to the patient via positive pressure ventilations.
Remove the patient from the environment.
questions.explanation: Safety first! Of the three extrication options, properly trained and protected rescuers can remove the patient safely.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: With whom does ultimate responsibility for patient care in the field always rest?
options: the highest trained provider on scene
the regional or state EMS director
questions.explanation: No matter who is actually providing care or giving directions to the responder, ultimate responsibility always rests with the medical control physician.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: The rhythm that appears in the ECG shown in Figure 3.1 is which of the following?
questions.explanation: The rhythm identified in Figure 3.1 is thirddegree AV block.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following situations represents expressed consent?
options: The patient says, “Help me. My chest hurts.”
The patient is eight years old with no parent present.
The patient is unconscious and un-responsive.
The patient says, “I don’t need any help. Just let me die.”
questions.explanation: Expressed consent means that the patient gives you permission to treat him or her, either verbally or in writing.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: An intoxicated person refuses treatment or transport. How should you proceed?
options: Do as the patient wishes and leave the scene immediately.
Try to find a family member to get consent for treatment.
Try to persuade the person to accept your assistance.
Immediately document the refusal, then leave the scene.
questions.explanation: If a person who needs help refuses to accept it, you should try to persuade the person to accept aid and explain the consequences of refusing it. Only after doing so should you accept and document the refusal of care.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Your radio report to the hospital about the patient’s medical condition should include which of the following?
options: name, age, race, sex, and weight
estimated time of arrival on the scene
questions.explanation: Although some details of the medical history, such as allergies, surgeries, and medications, are relevant, a detailed history is not. Do not say the patient’s name over the radio. Estimated arrival at the hospital is important, but the time of your arrival on the scene is generally not important.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: What is the function of a safety officer at a multicasualty incident?
options: to teach the unit members to work together safely
to decide when a scene is safe enough to enter
to stand in for the transportation officer as necessary
to ensure patient safety before the BLS unit arrives
questions.explanation: The function of the safety officer is to evaluate the scene and make the “go/no go” decision for the operation. The safety office is also responsible for continuing to monitor scene safety -during the operation.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Patients who have overdosed and have altered mental status are best transported
questions.explanation: There is significant opportunity for the loss of airway control for this patient. A lateral recumbent position will allow passive draining of the airway in case of vomiting, as well as easy access for suctioning.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: At a mass-casualty incident (MCI), which sector should the incident commander establish first?
questions.explanation: Triage must be done before treatment can be properly performed. In many cases, triage and scene-assessment activities are in progress by the crew of the first arriving unit. If it is not ongoing, triage should be established first.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which sector officer will coordinate with police to block streets and provide access at an MCI?
questions.explanation: The staging officer’s responsibilities include coordinating with police, ensuring access for vehicles, maintaining a log of available units, and coordinating requests for resources. The transportation office will establish the staging area if the incident commander has not already ordered one.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: What is the purpose of the START method?
options: to coordinate the efforts of multiple response units to a MCI
to ensure safe access of responding units to the MCI site
to rapidly triage large numbers of patients quickly and efficiently
to communicate with medical personnel as efficiently as possible
questions.explanation: START stands for Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment and is designed to triage large numbers of patients as quickly as possible.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: The first step in triage at an MCI is to
options: direct the walking wounded away from the scene.
assess the victims’ respiratory status and pulse rate.
assess the victims’ hemodynamic status and AVPU.
evaluate the victims’ mental status and ABCs.
questions.explanation: Regardless of the triage method utilized, the first step is to direct the walking wounded to a safe place where they can be cared for and reassessed.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: The three primary parameters assessed when using the START triage system are
questions.explanation: Using the START triage system, the three primary parameters assessed are respiration, perfusion, and mentation (RPM). The parameters are a respiratory rate over or under 30 per minute, capillary refill under or over two seconds or presence of a radial pulse, and mentation (e.g., whether the patient is able to follow basic commands).
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Continual reexperiencing of a traumatic event is a characteristic of which of the following?
questions.explanation: Delayed stress reaction, or post-traumatic stress disorder, is characterized by reexperiencing of the traumatic event and diminished responsiveness to everyday life, as well as physical and cognitive symptoms.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What communications system has the capability to send and receive voice and telemetry simultaneously?
questions.explanation: Duplex can send and receive voice only. UHF and VHF are bandwidth frequencies.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describes pulsus paradoxus?
options: an abnormal decrease in the systolic pressure during inspiration compared with expiration
an abnormal increase in the systolic pressure during inspiration compared with expiration
an abnormal decrease in pulse rate between the radial and carotid pulses during inspiration compared with expiration
an abnormal increase in pulse rate between the radial and carotid pulses during inspiration compared with expiration
questions.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus is an abnormal decrease in systolic blood pressure that drops more than 10 to 15 mmHg during inspiration compared with expiration. Normally, the systolic pressure decreases 5 to 10 mmHg during inspiration, however, in patients with asthma, COPD, pericardial tamponade, pulmonary embolism or tension pneumothorax, the systolic pressure may decrease 10 to 20 mmHg or more during inspiration.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Ventilating a patient at 30 breaths per minute with a bag-valve mask and high-flow oxygen may result in
options: a decrease in the normal serum blood pH.
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation may result in respiratory alkalosis, a harmful condition to the patient.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You can reduce gastric distention during artificial ventilations by
options: pressing on the stomach during ventilations.
providing ventilations deep enough to cause chest rise only.
positioning the patient at a 15° sideways incline during ventilations.
squeezing the bag-valve mask quickly during ventilations.
questions.explanation: Quickly squeezing a BVM may cause enough pressure to force air into the esophagus. Pressing on the stomach may in fact compromise the airway by causing vomiting to occur.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: To adequately ventilate a patient with a partial laryngectomy through a stoma, you should
options: use more pressure to produce adequate chest rise.
pinch the nose and close the mouth.
suction the stoma with a soft-tip suction catheter first.
use a special bag-valve mask designed to ventilate a stoma.
questions.explanation: A patient with a partial laryngectomy has an ability to exhale through the mouth and nose. Therefore, you will have to close them in order to direct air into the lungs while providing artificial ventilations.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which patient is most likely to require immediate transport?
questions.explanation: Because of the possibility of severe blood loss, patients with fractures of the pelvis are most likely to need immediate transport.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You respond to a 25-patient mass-casualty incident at a store. The 911 caller stated that she smelled something “funny” and then started to feel weak and -nauseous. You are the second unit on the scene. The initial unit is nowhere to be seen, and they do not answer their radios. What should you determine first in this situation?
options: whether the other crew is treating patients inside and needs your help
whether this is a potential hazardous-materials incident in progress
whether the other crew has been overcome and needs your immediate assistance
whether you need more oxygen and supplies than your ambulance carries
questions.explanation: This is a hazardous-materials incident until proven otherwise. Do not rush in after fallen rescuers because you may become a victim, too.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You respond to a 25-patient mass-casualty incident at a store. The 911 caller stated that she smelled something “funny” and then started to feel weak and -nauseous. You are the second unit on the scene. The initial unit is nowhere to be seen, and they do not answer their radios. After you are instructed by the HAZMAT team to begin treating decontaminated patients, you should
options: take universal precautions and wear protective equipment.
begin your efforts with the least symptomatic patient first.
get ready to treat the patients as you would at any other scene.
thoroughly question the first patient for purposes of documentation.
questions.explanation: Always wear personal protective equipment to help avoid becoming personally contaminated. Unless you are trained to work in the hot zone, you should not treat anyone until they are properly decontaminated.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: For which of the following procedures is a gown most necessary?
questions.explanation: Wearing a gown is considered part of your BSI precautions with emergency childbirth. It may be recommended in some circumstances when cleaning instruments or drawing blood, but is not generally needed.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following requires intermediatelevel disinfection through the use of a solution of bleach and water?
options: routine housecleaning measures in your station and bunkroom
any items that have come into contact with mucous membranes
any instruments that were used in any invasive procedures
all items that have come into contact with intact skin
questions.explanation: Intermediate-level disinfection is used for all instruments and supplies that have come into contact with intact skin.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which infection is transmitted through contact with blood or body secretions?
questions.explanation: Hepatitis B is a blood-borne disease that is transmitted through contact with blood or body secretions.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is a major concern when dealing with a patient with organophosphate poisoning?
options: exposure of rescuers to the poison
questions.explanation: Exposure to organophosphate is a major concern. Proper isolation procedures are paramount to rescuer safety. Dispose of all patient clothing according to Environmental Protection Agency guidelines.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following medications is commonly used to treat patients who are victims of organophosphate poisoning?
questions.explanation: A large dose of atropine sulfate is used to counter-act cholinergic poisoning from organo-phosphates and carbamates.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Your patient is a 27-year-old male who has fallen from a 24-foot ladder. As you are approaching and forming your general impression, you note that he is conscious and talking. What should you do first?
options: Look at his chest to begin assessing the airway.
Manually stabilize his neck in a neutral -position.
Palpate for a radial pulse in an uninjured arm.
Palpate for a carotid pulse on one side of his neck.
questions.explanation: The airway is always given first priority, but in this case, since the patient is talking, the first step in his assessment and care would be to stabilize the cervical spine as you begin your ABC assessment.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In a motor vehicle crash, which of the following would be most important to understand in relation to the force of collision and the potential for injury?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = mass (weight)  velocity (speed)2/2. This means that speed plays a greater role in the force changes during a motor vehicle crash. While the other items are important when evaluating an accident scene, speed has the greatest influence on the potential for damage and injury.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You note snoring sounds during your initial assessment of a semiconscious trauma patient. What is your next step?
options: Measure and insert an oropharyngeal airway.
questions.explanation: Snoring indicates that the airway is partially obstructed by the patient’s tongue. Clear the -airway first by positioning with the chinlift/jaw-thrust maneuver or by inserting a nasopharyngeal airway. An oropharyngeal airway is not indicated due to the patient’s LOC. Cervical stabilization takes place prior to beginning your ABC assessment and head tilt/chin lift is not advisable due to the possibility of c-spine injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A patient with left shoulder pain may have a
questions.explanation: Bleeding within the abdominal cavity can irritate the abdominal surface of the diaphragm, causing referred pain to the shoulder.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: With the START triage method, several vital signs are quickly assessed to determine the order to care for and transport patients. According to the START method, which of the following patients would receive immediate treatment to support hemodynamic status without further assessment?
options: male, radial pulse present, skin warm and dry
female, radial pulse present, capillary refill time 1 sec
male, radial pulse present, skin pale, and cyanotic
female, radial pulse present, capillary refill time 0.5 sec
questions.explanation: The START method quickly reviews three categories to determine patient priority: respirations, pulse, and mental status. After respiratory status is assessed, the basis for judging a patient’s hemodynamic status is presence or absence or a radial pulse or skin color and temperature. This patient is showing signs of shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient in compensatory hemorrhagic shock would most likely present with which set of vital signs?
questions.explanation: By definition, compensatory shock means that in the presence of a hypoperfusing state, the body maintains its blood pressure to the brain. One way to maintain this condition is to increase heart rate. Choice a satisfies both of these conditions.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient has fallen off a 20-foot ladder, striking his back on a railing. He is experiencing pain at the injury site and a loss of bladder control. Which part of the spinal cord is most like affected by this mechanism?
questions.explanation: Nerves that assist in bladder control exit from the sacral spine, located above the coccyx.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unrestrained driver of a small car struck a tree at high speed. He has a distended abdomen that is tender when palpated. Vital signs are pulse, 120 beats per minute; respirations, 20 per minute; and blood pressure, 116/90 mmHg. What would be the most likely cause of the abdominal tenderness?
questions.explanation: Organ damage from the mechanism of injury may result in inflammation and bleeding, resulting in the compensatory vital signs.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Where would you expect to see a wound that is described as distal to the knee?
questions.explanation: Distal refers to a location that is further from the trunk of the body than the reference point.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You respond to a 63-year-old male who is complaining of sudden onset of extreme substernal chest pain that, he says, “feels like my insides are tearing.” The patient states that the pain radiates to the middle of his back between his shoulder blades. Which condition is this patient most likely suffering from?
questions.explanation: This patient is exhibiting classic signs and symptoms for a dissecting aortic aneurysm. The tearing sensation occurs as the intimal linings of the aorta are separated as blood collects between the tissues.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to a 63-year-old male who is complaining of sudden onset of extreme substernal chest pain that, he says, “feels like my insides are tearing.” The patient states that the pain radiates to the middle of his back between his shoulder blades. Which of the following is a predisposing factor for this patient’s condition?
questions.explanation: Hypertension is present in 75–85% of dissecting aortic aneurysm cases.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to a 63-year-old male who is complaining of sudden onset of extreme substernal chest pain that, he says, “feels like my insides are tearing.” The patient states that the pain radiates to the middle of his back between his shoulder blades. Which of the following medications may be ordered by medical control to treat this patient?
questions.explanation: Morphine sulfate is the appropriate medication for this patient. Medications that increase cardiac rate, output, function, or contractile force are contraindicated while the dissection is occuring.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to a 63-year-old male who is complaining of sudden onset of extreme substernal chest pain that, he says, “feels like my insides are tearing.” The patient states that the pain radiates to the middle of his back between his shoulder blades. Progression of this condition may cause
options: stroke, pericardial tamponade, acute myocardial infarction.
acute arterial occlusion, acute pulmonary embolism, encephalitis.
deep venous thrombosis, varicose veins, arterial atherosclerotic disease.
arterial atherosclerotic disease, pulmonary embolism, acute arterial occlusion.
questions.explanation: These are all consequences of further dissection. Other conditions also include syncope, heart failure, and absent or reduced pulses and death.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient has survived a vehicle rollover in which another passenger in the vehicle died. He is alert and not complaining of pain. His vital signs are: pulse, 100; systolic BP, 90; respirations, 28. What additional procedures are required prior to initiating transport?
options: Complete a detailed physical exam and stabilize his injuries, and then transport.
Quickly immobilize him using the long backboard as a splint, and then transport.
Perform a focused physical exam, splint all extremity fractures, and then transport.
Immobilize first, complete a focused physical exam of any injuries, and then transport.
questions.explanation: Even though the patient’s condition appears to be stable, the mechanism of injury indicates that serious underlying injuries, such as internal bleeding, may be present. Immobilize him quickly using the long board as a full-body splint. Transport immediately in this case. You can perform additional assessments or treatments while en route.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which statement about motorcycle crashes is correct?
options: They seldom result in severe trauma, unless the motorcycle is operated at high speeds.
Helmet use can reduce the incidence and severity of head injury.
Helmet use can reduce the incidence and severity of spinal injury.
Leather clothing cannot protect the rider against soft-tissue injury.
questions.explanation: Use of a helmet can protect the rider against head injury but not against spinal injury. Leather clothing is helpful in reducing the amount of soft tissue injury. Because the energy of the accident is mostly absorbed into the rider, severe trauma is noted even with low-speed crashes.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is a positive Battle’s sign an indication of?
questions.explanation: Battle’s sign is noted as discoloration of the mastoid area behind the ear. It is an indication that blood has collected there following a basal skull fracture. Periorbital ecchymosis (raccoon’s eyes) is noted with facial and orbital trauma and fractures. Subarachnoid bleeding may have no -external manifestations or signs.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A contrecoup contusion is a brain injury that
options: results from cerebral edema at the site of impact.
causes subdural or epidural hematoma formation.
is on the opposite side of the head from the impact site.
results from open skull fracture and brain bruising.
questions.explanation: A contrecoup injury is an injury to the brain opposite the impact site; it results from the brain’s rebounding movement against the skull wall following the initial impact. The coup injury is noted at the site of the actual impact.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a car-crash victim who was unconscious prior to your arrival but is now awake. As you examine the patient, you notice that he is becoming disoriented. What should you suspect?
questions.explanation: The symptoms are indicative of a massforming lesion in the head, such as an epidural or subdural hematoma. When a patient has a simple concussion, his or her mental status will continue to improve with time.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your trauma patient opens her eyes, pulls her hand away when pinched, and speaks only in garbled sounds. What is her Glasgow Coma Scale score?
questions.explanation: The score is 9: 2 points for eye opening, 5 points for motor response, and 2 points for speech.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your trauma patient is agitated and apprehensive. She is increasingly cyanotic, and breath sounds are rapidly diminishing over her left lung. She is exhibiting signs and symptoms of shock. You should suspect
questions.explanation: The signs and symptoms of tension pneumothorax are given.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A child has third-degree burns over the front of her trunk and the entire front of her right leg. According to the rule of nines, what percentage of her body surface area is affected?
questions.explanation: According to the rule of nines as applied to pediatric injuries, the front or back of the trunk each represents 18% of the body surface and the front of one leg represents 7% with the entire leg counting for 14%.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is a 34-year-old woman who has been in an automobile crash. Her respiratory rate is 34 with normal chest wall expansion; systolic blood pressure is 78; capillary refill is delayed; and her Glasgow Coma Scale score is
questions.explanation: Using the Revised Trauma Score, 3 points for the GCS, 3 points for the systolic blood pressure, and 3 points for the respiratory rate.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which group of vital sign changes is associated with Cushing’s reflex?
options: decreased blood pressure, decreased pulse rate, decreased respiratory rate, decreased temperature
increased blood pressure, decreased pulse rate, decreased respiratory rate, increased temperature
decreased blood pressure, increased pulse rate, increased respiratory rate, decreased temperature
increased blood pressure, increased pulse rate, increased respiratory rate, increased temperature
questions.explanation: This option lists the vital sign changes associated with Cushing’s syndrome.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following is the best way to examine a patient with abdominal pain?
options: Begin palpation at the spot indicated as being the most painful.
Begin palpation of all four quadrants ending with the painful area.
Begin auscultation and percussion away from the site of the pain.
Begin auscultation and percussion at the site of the pain.
questions.explanation: Palpate all quadrants of the abdomen, ending where the patient says it hurts. Because of the need for a quiet environment and several minutes to perform this successfully, you should not perform abdominal auscultation or percussion in the field.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You suspect that a trauma patient has a pelvic injury. She is cool and diaphoretic, with a heart rate of 134 and blood pressure of 100/72. Which of the following procedures is most appropriate in the management of her condition?
options: two large bore IVs run wide open
Transport in a position of comfort.
Perform the detailed assessment prior to transport.
questions.explanation: The use of the MAST/PASG as a splint in the stabilization of suspected pelvic fractures is indicated for this patient. Since the systolic blood pressure is greater than 90–100 mmHg, any IV fluid should be restricted to a slow, or “keep open,” rate. This may reduce any additional bleeding from the dilution of clotting factors.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When caring for a patient with a pulmonary contusion, it is essential not to
options: place the patient in a supine position.
overload the patient with IV fluids.
questions.explanation: Pulmonary contusion involves bruising of the lung tissue. It is essential not to overload a patient suffering from pulmonary contusion with fluids, as this can quickly lead to pulmonary edema. The patient should be given high-flow oxygen and intubated if necessary. Immobilization on a backboard may be necessary depending on the mechanism of injury. If so, the head of the board may be elevated slightly to improve respiratory effort.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What does ascites refer to?
questions.explanation: Ascites refers to an accumulation of fluid in the abdomen.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which type of hepatitis is spread via the fecal or oral route?
questions.explanation: Hepatitis A is spread by the fecal-oral route and is most commonly acquired from eating contaminated food. Hepatitis B, C, and D are all blood-borne diseases.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 60-year-old woman who has fallen down her front steps and has possibly fractured her ankle. Which assessment finding may be considered abnormal in this patient?
questions.explanation: Altered mental status is an abnormal finding in healthy elderly patients. The pulse rate does decrease somewhat with age but should remain within the normal range of 60–100. Blood pressure often increases with age and respiration rates increase sightly as patients use less of their lung tissues.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following drugs does not commonly cause toxicity in elderly patients?
questions.explanation: All the other drugs listed are commonly associated with toxicity in elderly patients.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which dysrhythmia is considered normal in an athletic adult?
questions.explanation: Sinus bradycardia, or a heartbeat slower than 60 BPM, is often considered normal, particularly in an athletic adult.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which statement about the vital signs of a patient with an AMI is correct?
options: Respiratory and pulse rates will be elevated, while blood pressure will be depressed.
Vital signs are insignificant because management depends on the underlying heart rhythm.
Vital signs vary greatly since they are related to the area and extent of cardiac damage.
Elevated respiratory and pulse rates are generally associated with a favorable prognosis.
questions.explanation: Vital signs in MI patients depend on the location and extent of underlying heart damage and the patient’s response to the insult.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient has been stabbed in the back. Which of the following signs would most likely make you suspect that the patient has a kidney injury?
questions.explanation: Frank blood in the urine is a strong sign of injury to the kidney. Abdominal tenderness is unlikely, since the kidneys are located in the retroperitoneal space. Bruising may provide indirect information about organ involvement but is not as specific as hematuria.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is a 24-year-old female who shows signs and symptoms of pelvic inflammatory disease. What is the goal of prehospital care for this patient?
options: Begin definitive therapy on the scene prior to initiating your transport.
Perform a complete physical exam to identify associated medical problems.
Make the patient as comfortable as possible and transport to the hospital.
Begin the identification of all the patient’s previous sexual contacts.
questions.explanation: The goal of prehospital care for patients with PID is to provide comfort. There is no need to -perform a vaginal exam or ask any questions regarding sexual contacts.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following factors would normally cause a decrease in a patient’s respiratory rate?
questions.explanation: A patient will breathe more slowly when asleep than when awake; all the other factors listed increase respiratory rate.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: While ventilating a patient with a bag-valve mask, you note decreasing compliance. How should you react to this finding?
options: Stop using the bag-valve mask and intubate the patient.
Request permission to sedate the patient with morphine.
Assess the cause of this finding and try to correct it.
Continue ventilation with BVM; this is a normal finding.
questions.explanation: Compliance refers to how easily air flows into and out of the lungs. If compliance is decreasing, look for the cause by first reassessing (with look, auscultate, and feel) the airway and head position and then looking for signs that the patient is developing a tension pneumothorax; once you find the cause, try to correct it.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What would a pulse oximetry reading of 88% indicate for your patient with acute respiratory distress?
questions.explanation: A pulse oximetry reading around 90% for a patient in a normal atmosphere indicates that severe hypoxia is present. It corresponds to a PaO2 reading of around 60 mmHg. Normal PaO2 is between 80 and 100 mmHg. Normal pulse oximetry readings are from 93% to 100%, with 93–95 considered the lower end of normal.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The primary use of the Magill forceps in the field is to
options: directly remove a visible foreign-body obstruction.
open the airway of a patient with suspected neck trauma.
move the tongue aside during attempts at intubation.
aid in removal of an esophageal obturator airway in the field.
questions.explanation: Magill forceps are used to remove an obstructing foreign body that is visible during laryngo-scopy after abdominal thrusts have been unsuccessful.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following signs most likely indicates imminent birth?
options: urge to have a bowel movement
questions.explanation: When the fetus is close to the entrance of the birth canal, the head presses down on the internal anal sphincter, resulting in the urge to have a bowel movement. Painful uterine contractions can start well in advance of physical delivery.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is a sign of esophageal intubation?
options: air leak heard over the trachea
breath sounds absent on the left
questions.explanation: Right-side-only breath sounds are a sign of right mainstem intubation, ruling out choice b. Air leak over the trachea may be the sign of an improperly inflated cuff, ruling out choice a. Bilateral chest wall expansion is a normal finding, ruling out choice c. These findings point to the need to have a good baseline assessment of lung sounds and respiratory status prior to performing any interventions.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following conditions is this patient most likely suffering from?
questions.explanation: Rapid onset, rales, accessory muscle use, and dyspnea are classic symptoms for a patient with acute pulmonary edema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In addition to oxygen, this patient should also be treated with which of the following -medications?
questions.explanation: Morphine sulfate would be an appropriate treatment for acute pulmonary edema. It increases peripheral venous capacitance and decreases venous return, improving ventilation and decreasing myocardial oxygen demand.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A pregnant patient complains of painful, irregular labor contractions. The patient states that she is 37 weeks pregnant and her amniotic membranes ruptured two days ago. Based on this information, you would
options: inform the patient that she is experiencing Braxton-Hicks contractions.
tell the patient to call when her contractions become regular.
questions.explanation: Immediate transport is indicated for this patient’s condition. Without the intact protective amniotic sac, the fetus is at risk of being infected.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the primary treatment of metabolic acidosis?
options: having the patient rebreathe his or her own carbon dioxide
determining and treating the underlying cause
questions.explanation: Treatment of metabolic acidosis consists mainly of adequate ventilation. Administration of sodium bicarbonate is rarely needed. Rebreathing CO2 is a treatment for respiratory alkalosis caused by simple hyperventilation. Identification of the cause of acidosis is not as critical as identifying that it is present and beginning corrective measures to prevent its worsening.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which drug is an example of a beta 2 agonist?
questions.explanation: Albuterol is a beta agonist that is used to cause bronchodilation in patients with asthma and other respiratory emergencies. Aminophylline is a methylxanthine in the same class as theophylline. Ipratropium is an anticholinergic bronchodilator. Labetalol is an alpha and beta adrenergic blocker.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the condition that is present when the pleural space expands because air enters from an interior wound?
questions.explanation: Closed pneumothorax occurs when air enters the pleural space from an interior wound. An open pneumothrax occurs when the chest wall is open so that air can enter directly into the chest from the outside. A tension pneumothorax develops when a simple pneumothorax becomes large enough to cause pressure and structural changes within the chest.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true in regard to the prehospital management of a very ill neonate?
options: Early placement of a tracheal tube is critical in early respiratory distress.
High-flow oxygen carries a risk of oxygen toxicity.
Interventions should be reassessed at 30-second intervals.
Allow the parent to hold the infant during treatment.
questions.explanation: Interventions during a neonatal resuscitation should be assessed often, so that changes are noted as soon as they occur. The parents should not hold the infant, although it may be helpful to have them close by.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What does the term stridor refer to?
options: a rattling sound associated with fluid in the upper airway
a whistling sound heard upon expiration in asthma patients
a gurgling sound resulting from fluid in the lower airways
a high-pitched sound upon inspiration from airway obstruction
questions.explanation: Stridor is a sound made during inspiration and is associated with croup and upperairway obstruction.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A patient who is 37 weeks pregnant is having contractions and states that her water broke about 20 minutes ago. The vaginal exam reveals that the baby’s foot is present in the birth canal. Your next action would be to
options: move the patient to the cot and initiate rapid transport.
turn the mother onto her stomach and prepare for a field delivery.
apply downward pressure on the symphysis pubis to delay delivery.
place the mother on her left side and prepare for a field delivery.
questions.explanation: A single-limb presentation is considered nondeliverable vaginally and requires rapid cesarean section to successfully deliver the infant. Rapid transport is indicated.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the primary treatment for a patient with chronic emphysema who is not severely hypoxic?
options: ventilating with high-flow oxygen via mask
questions.explanation: Patients with emphysema or chronic bronchitis benefit from administration of lowflow oxygen and constant monitoring.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Epinephrine 1:1,000 may be indicated in
questions.explanation: As a bronchodilator, epinephrine 1:1,000 is sometimes indicated in younger (< 35 years old) asthma patients.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You are on the scene of a vehicle crash involving a bus. As triage officer, the first patient you encounter is sitting on the ground, conscious, confused, and breathing 40 times per minute. She states that she was thrown out of her seat and struck the side of her head. Your next action would be to
questions.explanation: A patient who is conscious and breathing over 30 times per minute is classified as “Immediate.” There is no need to check for the presence or rate of the pulse. While she may need oxygen therapy, now is not the time to do so.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 17-year-old female who was found by her parents hanging from a rope in the garage. The patient was cut down by her father approximately six minutes prior to your arrival, but she is unconscious and unresponsive to pain or voice. She is breathing spontaneously but has coarse inspiratory stridor. What is the most probable cause of the inspiratory stridor?
options: a trachea that is crushed or torn
a cervical spine lesion from trauma
questions.explanation: The inspiratory stridor most likely indicates an edematous crushed or torn trachea.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 17-year-old female who was found by her parents hanging from a rope in the garage. The patient was cut down by her father approximately six minutes prior to your arrival, but she is unconscious and unresponsive to pain or voice. She is breathing spontaneously but has coarse inspiratory stridor. Upon examination of the patient’s upper chest and neck, you note the presence of a large swollen area that extends from her throat to her shoulder. When you press on this area, you feel crackles under the skin. What is this condition called?
questions.explanation: This patient’s symptoms indicate subcutaneous emphysema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 17-year-old female who was found by her parents hanging from a rope in the garage. The patient was cut down by her father approximately six minutes prior to your arrival, but she is unconscious and unresponsive to pain or voice. She is breathing spontaneously but has coarse inspiratory stridor. Which of the following is an appropriate treatment for this patient?
options: airway management by the least invasive means
larygoscopy to determine the extent of -damage
administer epinephrine to reverse airway swelling
questions.explanation: It will be difficult to determine the actual extent of injury, so the least invasive airway technique is appropriate. Monitor vital signs and pulse oximetry readings to determine if perfusion is adequate.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your partner and you are evaluating a patient experiencing a behavioral emergency. Your patient is visibly upset and is pacing back and forth. He is verbally confrontational, and his hands are in fists. At times, he displays aggressive actions, both physical and verbal. Which of the following actions is most appropriate for this situation?
questions.explanation: Given the circumstances, not enough personnel are available to restrain this patient if he becomes violent. Conducting a hands-on assessment may not be possible given the state of the patient’s behavior. Constantly being aware of your surroundings will provide the greatest level of safety.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: A critically traumatized 18-year-old male is entrapped. It will be at least 15 minutes before he is freed. The community hospital is 20 minutes away by ground. A medical helicopter can be to the scene in eight minutes. A Level I trauma center is one hour away by ground. Given the situation, which is the best mode of transport for this patient?
options: Transport the patient by ground to the community hospital, and then transfer the patient to the trauma center by air.
Transport the patient by ground to the Level I trauma center.
Fly the patient from the scene to the community hospital.
Fly the patient from the scene to the Level I trauma center.
questions.explanation: Aeromedical transport offers the advantage of rapid transport to specialized facilities such as the Level I trauma center. The amount of time needed to extricate the patient, coupled with the time to transport the patient to either the closest hospital or the trauma center by ground, is too much. Flying the patient directly to the trauma center is most appropriate.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: What is the first sign of laryngeal edema in a patient suffering from anaphylaxis?
questions.explanation: The first sign of laryngeal edema is usually a hoarse voice.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What does the disease process of emphysema cause within the lung tissues?
options: a buildup of fluid due to increased capillary permeability
deflation of a portion of the lung due to the rupture of a bleb
bronchoconstriction due to increased airway resistance
a loss of elasticity in the alveoli due to prolonged insult
questions.explanation: Patients with emphysema have a loss of elasticity in the alveoli due to prolonged insult. Bleb formation results in decreased ability of the alveoli to expand and contract and an overall decreased surface area of the lungs. Ruptured blebs do not result in lung “deflation.”
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 78-year-old male who is having difficulty breathing. The patient is sitting upright in a tripod position and you note profound accessory muscle use. His skin is pale, cool, and clammy. Vital signs are: blood pressure, 180/72; heart rate, 90; and respiratory rate, 40. Breathing is shallow and labored with a coarse rattling sound during expiration. Auscultation reveals coarse rales to the nipple line with no air movement in the bases. The patient can speak only in oneor two-word sentences. Family members inform you that the patient was sleeping when this episode began and that this has happened several times since his AMI one year ago. He has mild pedal edema and neck veins are nondistended. His family first noticed the patient having dyspnea about 25–30 minutes ago. This patient is exhibiting the signs and symptoms of which of the following diseases?
questions.explanation: This patient is exhibiting the classic signs and symptoms of congestive heart failure. His history of AMI indicates that he may have permanent damage to the heart and raises the possibility that he is now having an acute episode of failure.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 78-year-old male who is having difficulty breathing. The patient is sitting upright in a tripod position and you note profound accessory muscle use. His skin is pale, cool, and clammy. Vital signs are: blood pressure, 180/72; heart rate, 90; and respiratory rate, 40. Breathing is shallow and labored with a coarse rattling sound during expiration. Auscultation reveals coarse rales to the nipple line with no air movement in the bases. The patient can speak only in oneor two-word sentences. Family members inform you that the patient was sleeping when this episode began and that this has happened several times since his AMI one year ago. He has mild pedal edema and neck veins are nondistended. His family first noticed the patient having dyspnea about 25–30 minutes ago. Which of the following medication should be used for this patient?
questions.explanation: Oxygen, morphine, nitroglycerin, and furosemide are all used in the treatment of CHF patients.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 78-year-old male who is having difficulty breathing. The patient is sitting upright in a tripod position and you note profound accessory muscle use. His skin is pale, cool, and clammy. Vital signs are: blood pressure, 180/72; heart rate, 90; and respiratory rate, 40. Breathing is shallow and labored with a coarse rattling sound during expiration. Auscultation reveals coarse rales to the nipple line with no air movement in the bases. The patient can speak only in oneor two-word sentences. Family members inform you that the patient was sleeping when this episode began and that this has happened several times since his AMI one year ago. He has mild pedal edema and neck veins are nondistended. His family first noticed the patient having dyspnea about 25–30 minutes ago. A patient with the symptoms and history described may exhibit which of the following symptoms prior to an acute onset?
questions.explanation: Proxysmal nocternal dyspnea (PND), dyspnea upon exertion, and increased dyspnea are all signs of worsening CHF.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 78-year-old male who is having difficulty breathing. The patient is sitting upright in a tripod position and you note profound accessory muscle use. His skin is pale, cool, and clammy. Vital signs are: blood pressure, 180/72; heart rate, 90; and respiratory rate, 40. Breathing is shallow and labored with a coarse rattling sound during expiration. Auscultation reveals coarse rales to the nipple line with no air movement in the bases. The patient can speak only in oneor two-word sentences. Family members inform you that the patient was sleeping when this episode began and that this has happened several times since his AMI one year ago. He has mild pedal edema and neck veins are nondistended. His family first noticed the patient having dyspnea about 25–30 minutes ago. Priorities for the management of this patient include all of the following except
options: decreasing venous return to the heart.
increasing venous return to the heart.
questions.explanation: Priorities for managing this patient are indicated by choices b, c, and d.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 78-year-old male who is having difficulty breathing. The patient is sitting upright in a tripod position and you note profound accessory muscle use. His skin is pale, cool, and clammy. Vital signs are: blood pressure, 180/72; heart rate, 90; and respiratory rate, 40. Breathing is shallow and labored with a coarse rattling sound during expiration. Auscultation reveals coarse rales to the nipple line with no air movement in the bases. The patient can speak only in oneor two-word sentences. Family members inform you that the patient was sleeping when this episode began and that this has happened several times since his AMI one year ago. He has mild pedal edema and neck veins are nondistended. His family first noticed the patient having dyspnea about 25–30 minutes ago. This patient would most likely benefit from which of the following?
options: moving him to a supine position
assisting him in walking to the ambulance
having him breathe deeply into a paper bag
giving him intermittent positive pressure ventilation
questions.explanation: Increased ventilatory pressures assist in driving off some of the pulmonary edema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 78-year-old male who is having difficulty breathing. The patient is sitting upright in a tripod position and you note profound accessory muscle use. His skin is pale, cool, and clammy. Vital signs are: blood pressure, 180/72; heart rate, 90; and respiratory rate, 40. Breathing is shallow and labored with a coarse rattling sound during expiration. Auscultation reveals coarse rales to the nipple line with no air movement in the bases. The patient can speak only in oneor two-word sentences. Family members inform you that the patient was sleeping when this episode began and that this has happened several times since his AMI one year ago. He has mild pedal edema and neck veins are nondistended. His family first noticed the patient having dyspnea about 25–30 minutes ago. If this patient were experiencing right-side heart failure, you would expect to find all of the following symptoms except
questions.explanation: Choices a, b, and c are all typically associated with CHF; syncope is not.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: When performing water-related rescues, use of personal flotation device is required when
options: the water is greater than three feet deep.
the rescuer weighs less than 150 pounds.
the rescuer enters any and all water.
questions.explanation: No matter the competency level of the rescuer, personal safety is absolute.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: An isotonic solution is one that
options: has an electrolyte composition like that of blood plasma.
is used only in patients who are severely dehydrated.
has a higher solute composition than the body cells.
has a lower solute composition than the body cells.
questions.explanation: Isotonic solutions such as Ringer’s lactate or normal saline have electrolyte compositions similar to that of blood plasma although they lack the large protein molecules found within blood.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the most common cause of cardiogenic shock?
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock results most often from left ventricular failure following acute MI.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The use of PASG/MAST is indicated for which of the following conditions?
options: to control uterine bleeding in a pregnant patient with abruptio placentae
to prevent shock in a patient with uncontrollable bleeding in the neck
to support the respiratory efforts of a patient with severe dyspnea
to stabilize lower-extremity fractures in a hypotensive patient
questions.explanation: Third-trimester pregnancy, impaled objects, and dyspnea are all contraindications for use of the PASG/MAST. In addition, the PASG/ MAST is contraindicated with any uncontrolled bleeding occuring above the site of the garment.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A patient has been violently beaten. Which of the following statements is true surrounding the care provided by EMS?
options: Evidence preservation is the first priority.
Latex gloves will prevent you from smudging fingerprints.
Statements made by the patient should be recorded in the report.
Bloody clothing should be placed in plastic bags.
questions.explanation: Managing the patient’s injury is the first priority of the EMS provider. Any statements made by the patient should be recorded as possible evidence of the crime.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An injury to the ligaments surrounding a joint that is marked by pain, swelling, and bruising is called
questions.explanation: A sprain is a partial tearing of a ligament caused by a sudden twisting or stretching of a joint beyond its normal range of motion. It results in swelling and discoloration caused by bleeding into the tissue. A strain is an injury to the muscle or tendon and usually does not result in discoloration. A dislocation occurs when the normal articulation of two bones is disrupted. Arthritis is inflammation of joints characterized by pain and swelling but does not result in discoloration.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When administering IV fluids to a trauma patient, it is critical to continuously monitor which vital sign?
questions.explanation: Breath sounds are particularly important to monitor during IV fluid administration because of the danger of fluid overload, which will initially manifest as pulmonary edema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Why is morphine sulfate used in the management of AMI patients?
options: to help distinguish between angina pectoris and AMI
to relieve pain and reduce myocardial oxygen demand
to decrease the likelihood of ventricular dysrhythmias
as an alternative therapy for patients allergic to lidocaine
questions.explanation: Morphine relieves pain, decreases venous return, and reduces the oxygen demand of the myocardium.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing
