questions.text: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND) is -commonly a sign of which of the following -conditions?
questions.explanation: Left-side heart failure with pulmonary edema is often associated with PND. PND manifests as difficulty breathing when the patient lies flat. As the condition worsens, many patients will report the need to sleep sitting up in a recliner.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Management of left-side heart failure includes high-flow oxygen, IV of crystalloid solution, ECG monitoring, and the administration of which pharamcological agents?
questions.explanation: The accepted protocol for management of left-side heart failure is given.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are inserting the tracheal tube when you begin to hear the sound of the patient’s breathing. Your next action would be to
options: inflate the distal cuff and secure the tube.
wait for the patient to inhale and insert the tube farther.
pull back on the tube and secure with tape.
attach an end-tidal CO2 detector to confirm placement.
questions.explanation: As the tip of the nasotracheal tube reaches the glottic opening, you should hear the respiratory effort of the patient. Insertion of the tube past the vocal cords is timed to the inhalation phase in order to minimize resistance against the tube itself.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the initial electrical treatment for a patient with third-degree AV block who is symptomatic?
questions.explanation: The preferred field treatment is transcutaneous pacing. Definitive treatment is pacemaker insertion. Drug therapy indicated for this patient is aimed at increasing cardiac output by improving contractility, force of contractions, or increasing rate.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient suspected of having an abdominal aortic aneurysm will receive oxygen, an IV, ECG monitoring, and rapid transport as part of his or her treatment. What else should you do when treating such a patient?
questions.explanation: Treat the patient for shock and transport rapidly. Do not palpate the abdomen. This is one of the few medical conditions that may still -benefit from the use of PASG/MAST as the garment may tamponade any bleeding that may be occurring. Medications which stimulate the cardio-vascular system should be avoided.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 52-year-old male has been ejected from a car. He is apneic, with a slow pulse palpated at the femoral artery. Which of the following procedures would best manage this patient’s airway?
options: Ventilate with the bag-valve mask and attach to high-flow oxygen.
Apply a nonrebreather mask with high-flow oxygen.
questions.explanation: This patient needs immediate oxygenation and ventilation. Using a bag-valve mask will accomplish this task most effectively.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Vagal maneuvers are used to treat which type of dysrhythmias?
questions.explanation: PSVTs (paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia) may be managed by vagal maneuvers, such as the Valsalva maneuver or ice-water immersion.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which part of the ECG tracing reflects repolarization of the ventricles?
questions.explanation: The T wave reflects repolarization of the ventricles.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The length of the normal P-R interval is
questions.explanation: The length of a normal P-R interval is 0.120.20 seconds, or 3–5 small boxes on the ECG strip.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 3-year-old female is unresponsive and not breathing. Parents state that she was eating grapes when she suddenly made a high-pitched whistling noise and turned blue. Your immediate action would be
options: provide back blows and chest thrusts.
questions.explanation: The high-pitched whistling noise and cyanosis indicates the patient is suffering from an airway obstruction. Back blows and chest thrusts are performed only on infants. Since the patient is unresponsive, your immediate step should be to start CPR. Abdominal thrusts are performed only on conscious choking victims. For unresponsive victims suspected of having an airway obstruction, you should begin CPR but look in the mouth to see if you visualize the object prior to each ventilation attempt.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: While assessing a patient complaining of difficulty breathing, you note an altered mental status, stridor, chest tightness, and tachycardia. Based on these symptoms, you should suspect which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Stridor indicates an upper-airway obstruction, in this case most likely from an allergic reaction. A patient with emphysema would exhibit difficulty breathing with wheezing and rhonchi; a patient with asthma will exhibit wheezing respirations; a patient suffering from a CVA would have an altered mental status but would not have stridor.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In a patient with ventricular tachycardia, the QRS complex is
options: greater than 0.12 seconds and bizarre in shape.
questions.explanation: Patients with ventricular dysrhythmias often manifest lengthened and bizarre QRS complexes.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient with nonperfusing ventricular tachycardia would receive the same treatment as a patient with which other rhythm?
questions.explanation: Treatment for both conditions consists of immediate defibrillation; continued treatment includes CPR and drugs. Additional therapy depends upon whether a normal rhythm is -initiated.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following indicates that an AMI patient is developing cardiogenic shock?
questions.explanation: Falling blood pressure, especially a systolic pressure lower than 80 mmHg together with decreasing level of consciousness, is a sign of cardiogenic shock in an AMI patient. Reflex tachycardia may develop as the patient’s body attempts to compensate for the shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following are signs and symptoms of right-side heart failure?
options: tachycardia, peripheral edema, and jugular vein distention
bradycardia, carotid bruits, and falling blood pressure
chest pain, pulmonary edema, and anxiety
questions.explanation: The classic signs and symptoms of right-side heart failure are given.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is often the first sign of the onset of the development of a potentially lethal dysrhythmia in an MI patient?
questions.explanation: A change in the pulse rate may be the first sign that a dysrhythmia is developing; this is why recording baseline vital signs is particularly important.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Why are easing anxiety and relieving pain considered major goals for prehospital care of MI patients?
options: The onset of either may signal the development of a lethal dysrhythmia.
Anxiety and pain often mask underlying symptoms of cardiogenic shock.
They may prevent the patient from cooperating with additional treatments.
Both anxiety and pain increase heart rate and myocardial oxygen demand.
questions.explanation: Anxiety and pain can increase the heart rate and, therefore, the oxygen demand of the myocardium.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Dopamine can be used to
questions.explanation: Dopamine is a potent sympathomimetic agent, and in cases of cardiogenic shock, it may be used to increase cardiac output.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient is short of breath after impact with the steering wheel in a motor vehicle crash. Breath sounds are diminished on the left. Which of the following conditions is most likely the cause of the patient’s complaint?
questions.explanation: The question does not provide any indication of a tension pneumothorax. A pulmonary contusion or cardiac tamponade should affect lung sounds.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following is a possible cause of pulseless electrical activity?
questions.explanation: Pulseless electrical activity may occur secondary to a variety of conditions and carries a grave prognosis. Comon causes, as listed in the AHA PEA algorithm, include pulonary embolus, tension pneumothorax, acidosis, cardiac tamponade, hypovolemia, hypoxia, hypothermia, hyperkalemia, AMI, and drug overdose. Hypokalemia may cause dysrhythmias but does not usually cause PEA. Tachycardia is not considered a cause of PEA simply because the definition of PEA is a pulseless patient with a rhythm that you would normally expect to find a pulse with. (In other words, tachycardia without a pulse is PEA.)
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Some treatments for suspected MI patients mask the elevated cardiac enzyme levels that are used to diagnose MI in the hospital setting. To prevent this, you should not administer
options: any drugs via the IM route.
questions.explanation: Intramuscular injections can injure muscle tissues, causing the release of enzymes that may mask the cardiac enzymes that are looked for to confirm a diagnosis of MI.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 67-year-old male who is complaining of chest pain. The chest pain continues after two doses of nitroglycerin. He reports a history of angina and says that all his previous attacks have been relieved by nitroglycerin. How should you treat this patient?
options: Take a detailed history to determine why this episode is different.
Treat the patient as though he is having an AMI and transport rapidly.
Look for signs and symptoms of decreased perfusion or cardiogenic shock.
Assume that his medication has expired and give two more doses from yours.
questions.explanation: A patient with angina whose pain does not respond to nitroglycerin is most likely suffering from MI and should be transported without delay.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the most common complication of an acute myocardial infarction?
questions.explanation: The most common complication of MI is dysrhythmia, and some dysrhythmias are or become life threatening. Cardiogenic shock may develop following an AMI, especially when the site of infarction is the left ventricle. Angina is a complication of artersclerosis and coronary artery vasoconstriction or vasospasm.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: In addition to oxygen, what is the first-line pharmacologic agent used for the treatment of malignant PVCs?
questions.explanation: Lidocaine is the first drug used to treat malignant PVCs or nonmalignant PVCs in patients who are symptomatic or who have a history of cardiac disease. All the other agents are used if V fib or V tach develops, or they may be used to treat PVCs after a trial of lidocaine has been unsuccessful.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of these following rhythms might indicate a need for cardioversion?
options: atrial fibrillation at a rate of 120
junctional tachycardia at a rate of 120
wandering atrial pacemaker at a rate of 120
ventricular tachycardia at a rate of 120
questions.explanation: Based on rhythm alone, ventricular tachycardia is the most likely to require cardioversion. The other rhythms most likely will remain asymptomatic at a heart rate of 120.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient, who has had a recent tracheostomy, tried to remove himself from the ventilator and dislodged the trach cannula. Subcutaneous emphysema is now evident. What should you do next?
options: Remove the tracheostomy and attempt to reinsert it.
Remove the tracheostomy tube and insert an endotracheal tube.
Deflate the tracheostomy cuff and ventilate around it with a BVM.
Continue to ventilate the patient through the tracheostomy tube.
questions.explanation: If the trach tube has been dislodged, it may not be easy to reinsert, so rest it in its original position. Placing an endotracheal tube into the stoma and inflating the cuff will help rapidly establish a patient’s airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called by the police department to a neighbor-hood where you encounter a male patient approximately 20–30 years old. The police officer states that neighbors called because the patient was “freaking out.” Witnesses say they saw him smoking something just before he started acting in a bizarre manner. During your assessment, you notice that the patient is hyperactive and anxious. His pupils are dilated, and he is hypertensive and tachycardic. This patient is most likely experiencing
questions.explanation: Dilated pupils, hyperactivity, tachycardia, and hypertension are classic signs of cocaine use. Narcotic use would result in lethargy, stupor, and respiratory depression.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are called by the police department to a neighbor-hood where you encounter a male patient approximately 20–30 years old. The police officer states that neighbors called because the patient was “freaking out.” Witnesses say they saw him smoking something just before he started acting in a bizarre manner. During your assessment, you notice that the patient is hyperactive and anxious. His pupils are dilated, and he is hypertensive and tachycardic. The appropriate treatment for this patient consists of oxygen, IV, ECG monitoring, and
questions.explanation: Transport is the only other treatment required for this patient. Be prepared to provide respiratory support if needed. Naloxone is used with narcotic ingestion to restore respirations and is not indicated in this patient. Activated charcoal and ipecac are indicated for poisoning or overdose via the oral route. This patient was reported to have inhaled (smoked) something that appears to be a stimulant.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You are called by the police department to a neighbor-hood where you encounter a male patient approximately 20–30 years old. The police officer states that neighbors called because the patient was “freaking out.” Witnesses say they saw him smoking something just before he started acting in a bizarre manner. During your assessment, you notice that the patient is hyperactive and anxious. His pupils are dilated, and he is hypertensive and tachycardic. When treating this patient, you should be prepared for which of the following complications?
questions.explanation: Dysrhythmias and seizures are both serious possible complications of stimulation effects from cocaine use.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is in respiratory distress. He is exhibiting jugular venous distension. Crackles are auscultated throughout his lung fields. He is tachycardic, hypertensive, and tachypneic. Which of the following sets of treatment is indicated for this patient’s presentation?
options: oxygen, morphine sulfate IM, intravenous line at 30 cc/hour, and 40 mg furosemide IV
oxygen, intravenous line at 30 cc/hour, nitroglycerin sL, and 40 mg furosemide IV
oxygen, saline lock, and 2 mg morphine sulfate IV
oxygen, sublingual nitroglycerin, and 40 mg furosemide IM
questions.explanation: This patient appears to be experiencing acute pulmonary edema. Of the choices provided, choice b has both the correct doses and routes of the indicated medications.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A patient is complaining of chest pressure and shortness of breath. He has jugular venous distension and pedal edema. His lung sounds are clear and equal. Which of the following conditions most likely cause these findings?
questions.explanation: Reduced pumping capacity of the right ventricle causes blood flow to “back up” into the systemic vascular system, causing congestion as evidenced by the pedal edema and JVD. Lung fields that are past the cardiac insufficiency remain free from fluid.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient has a suspected hand injury. How can you best immobilize the hand in the position of function?
options: Tape it flat against the chest with the fingers extended outward together.
Put the arm into a sling and swathe and allow the hand to dangle naturally.
Place a roll of gauze bandage into the palm and secure the hand to a splint.
Secure the hand to a padded splint with the hand clenched into a fist.
questions.explanation: The neutral position, or position of function, for the hand is achieved by using a gauze roller bandage (or similar material) placed inside the palm with the fingers curled around it. The hand, wrist, and forearm should then be splinted with a board, wire ladder, or vacuum-type splint.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When splinting a limb with a suspected fracture, one caregiver applies the splint while another
options: holds the limb and monitors distal pulse, motor, and sensation.
calms, reassures, and provides emotional support to the patient.
checks for limb alignment and administers high-flow oxygen.
gets the stretcher or other supplies and monitors vital signs.
questions.explanation: Immobilization of a suspected fracture is best accomplished with two rescuers. After positioning the limb properly, one EMS provider applies the splint, while the other holds the limb in position and monitors the distal pulse, motor, and sensory responses.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are caring for a patient whose finger was just cut off in an accident. What should you do with the amputated finger?
options: Place the severed finger in a plastic bag and immerse the bag in cold water.
Immerse the severed finger directly into a pail of ice-cold normal saline solution.
Bandage the severed finger in sterile gauze and place it in a plastic bag with ice.
Bandage the hand with the severed finger placed back into its normal position.
questions.explanation: Do not allow the severed digit to get wet because the tissues will begin to draw in the hypotonic fluid and will swell up, which may make reimplantation impossible. The cold environment will help reduce oxygen demand by the cells of the severed digit and will help keep it alive longer.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following is not characteristic of a mild or moderate pit viper envenomation?
options: bruising located around the wound site
systemic effects like nausea or vomiting
localized edema at the wound site
little or no pain felt by the patient
questions.explanation: Pit viper envenomation is generally very painful. Little or no pain is characteristic of coral snake (neurotoxic) envenomation.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: How should you treat a patient who has sustained dry lime burns to the hand and arm?
options: Use a neutralizing acid to offset the effect of the chemical burn.
Brush the lime away and then flood the skin with cool water.
Immediately immerse the injured limb in a pail of cold water.
Use alcohol to dissolve the lime, and then flood with cool water.
questions.explanation: Brush away as much of the lime as possible, then flood the burned area with water. Neutralizing chemicals are generally not recommended because the use of one chemical to neutralize another usually results in the release of heat and the formation of a third chemical.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient in a very early stage of hypoglycemia may complain of
questions.explanation: The earliest manifestations of hypoglycemia are hunger, anxiety, and restlessness.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Diabetic patients may develop hypoglycemia if they take too much insulin or if they
options: exercise too much with limited food intake.
overeat and do not exercise enough.
sit in a chair for prolonged periods of time.
inject their insulin directly into a vein.
questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia develops in patients with diabetes when they take too much insulin or get too much exercise for the amount of food they eat.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: With which of the following conditions is central neurogenic hyperventilation commonly associated?
questions.explanation: Central neurogenic hyperventilation is characterized by rapid, deep, noisy breathing and is associated with lesions of the central nervous system. Cheyne-Stokes is the pattern commonly seen with diabetic emergencies.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A patient is in respiratory distress. She has a valid DNR order. Which of the following treatments is correct?
options: Nothing should be done to the patient in this situation.
Provide oxygen and a nebulized albuterol treatment.
Administer 2 L of oxygen via a nasal cannula.
Initiate a direct laryngoscopy and intubation.
questions.explanation: “Do Not Resuscitate” is not the same as “do not treat.” Under these conditions, management of the condition is warranted.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You have secured the airway and immobilized the cervical spine for your patient with an altered mental status. What is your next priority of care?
options: Draw blood for glucose assessment and establish an IV.
Assess DCAP-BTLS, looking for any hidden injuries.
Administer naloxone via the IV and IM glucagon.
Hyperventilate with O2 and administer dexamethasone.
questions.explanation: The first priority for patients with altered mental status of unknown cause is a blood glucose determination to rule out hypoglycemia as a potential cause.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 36-year-old man who is having a seizure. His wife reports that he has not taken his “seizure pills” lately and that he has now had three seizures in a row without regaining consciousness. You have secured the airway and are now ventilating with the bagvalve mask. What should you do next?
options: Draw blood, administer dextrose, and transport immediately.
Monitor blood glucose level and administer naloxone and thiamine.
Secure the patient to a long spine board until the seizures are over.
Begin an IV, monitor cardiac rhythm, and administer diazepam.
questions.explanation: For a patient in status epilepticus, treatment consists of establishing an IV, monitoring cardiac rhythm, and administering diazepam to stop the seizures.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the primary treatment for severe anaphylaxis in an adult?
questions.explanation: This is the standard adult dosage and route for a patient with severe anaphylaxis. The SC route is recommended unless the patient is in life-threatening distress. Services may recommend the use of 1:1,000 instead 1:10,000.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: An adult patient has overdosed on an unknown medication in a suicide attempt. She is alert, oriented, and refusing treatment and transport. What is your most appropriate next action?
options: Obtain a signed refusal from the patient.
Advise the patient that she will die if not treated.
Restrain her and transport to the emergency department.
Consult with medical direction to transport the patient.
questions.explanation: This patient may not have the right to refuse treatment and transport due to the actions she has taken. Consulting with medical direction on how to best approach this patient may be helpful.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following patients is considered to be at high risk for a heat-related emergency?
questions.explanation: The very young, the very old, those undernourished, and those with chronic illness are all predisposed to heat illnesses for a variety of reasons.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A late sign of hypoxia in children is
questions.explanation: Bradycardia in a child is an ominous sign of a hypoxic brain.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the most important treatment consideration for a patient who is suffering from decompression sickness?
options: Have suction equipment ready because vomiting is common.
Monitor the ECG and be prepared to defibrillate as necessary.
Provide high-concentration oxygen with a nonrebreather mask.
Administer Nitronox or morphine sulfate as needed to control pain.
questions.explanation: Provide oxygen at 100% concentration and intubate if the patient is not breathing spontaneously.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: When interviewing patients who are distraught or potentially violent, you should do all of the following except
options: remove the patient from the crisis situation as quickly as possible.
encourage the patient to explain the situation in his or her own words.
firmly tell the patient whenever he or she is distorting reality.
avoid arguing with or shouting at the person who is distraught.
questions.explanation: The purpose of the interview is to calm the patient and to obtain as much information as possible, not to tell the patient what you think. There is a time to reorient patients to reality, but first you must work to calm them down and gain their trust.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What term best describes a patient who talks nonstop and is restless and overactive?
questions.explanation: A patient who is displaying manic symptoms is restless or extremely active and talks constantly. The patient may be extremely -suspicious or violent. If the patient has bi-polar disorder, he or she may swing between periods of mania and depression.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Lifting improperly may most likely result in an injury to which part of the back?
questions.explanation: The lumbar spine will be most impacted from an unevenly distributed load; the -cervical spine is also at risk for injury, although such injuries are less likely.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: After experiencing a sudden syncopal episode, a 41-year-old female is complaining of pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath. Her vital signs are RR = 28, P = 126, and BP = 88/60. The pulse oximeter reads 89% on high-flow oxygen. Her breath sounds are clear. Which of the following conditions best describes the patient’s signs and symptoms?
questions.explanation: The patient’s presentation points to a pulmonary embolism as the likely cause. The other conditions are not normally associated with sudden syncope, and adventitious lung sounds like crackles or wheezes should be evident, unlike the patient’s clear ones.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the management for a patient with a head injury and an unusual respiratory pattern?
options: positioning in the left lateral recumbent position
rapid transport because of possible brain stem injury
intubation and hyperventilation to reduce ICP
questions.explanation: Unusual respiratory patterns indicate the possibility of brain stem injury and call for rapid transport of the patient. Hyperventilation should be used only when you strongly suspect herniation is occuring. It is not indicated for the routine treatment of increasing ICP. You should ventilate the patient at a normal rate with 100% oxygen.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Given this situation, you should
options: let him sign a refusal form and return to -service.
call medical direction for advice and -guidance.
transport the patient against his will in restraints.
bandage the laceration, then leave the scene.
questions.explanation: Medical direction should be sought if at all possible for any suspected substance-abuse patient refusing treatment or transport. Because you are required to be an advocate for the patient, it is never advisable to simply transport someone against his or her will, even if you suspect impairment due to drug or alcohol use. It is also not advisable to allow him or her to sign a refusal form when it is obvious that they are in need of medical attention. Treating the patient, then leaving the scene without transport, leaves you open to legal liability and a possible charge of abandonment.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Regardless of any of the options considered in the previous question, the patient continues to insist he does not need medical attention. In this situation, which of the following is most important?
options: Obtain a signed refusal form and return to service as soon as possible.
Continue to insist that the patient accompany you to the hospital for care.
Properly document your advice to the patient and his continued refusal.
Restrain the patient in the supine position and transport him immediately.
questions.explanation: It is important in this situation to make a complete documentation of the patient’s refusal to accept treatment. Documentation should include the steps you took to convince him to seek medical attention, the potential consequences of his refusal, and your assessment findings.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You are called to treat a patient who is unconscious and responsive to painful stimuli only. Which of the following treatment modalities is appropriate for this patient?
options: 25 gm of 50% dextrose slow IV push, 2 mg naloxone IV, and transport to a detoxification center
blood glucose test, dextrose (if indicated), thiamine, monitor, oxygen, IV, and rapid transport
oxygen, IV fluid bolus titrated to a systolic BP of 100–110 mmHg, naloxone, and rapid transport
1,000 cc Ringer’s lactate IV, oxygen, ECG monitoring, and transport to an emergency department
questions.explanation: This choice gives the most appropriate treatment protocol for this patient. Because he is unconscious, he may be treated under implied consent. Treatment in this case is aimed at -ruling out the most treatable cause for coma—diabetes.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: During a basketball game, an 18-year-old male is complaining of sudden shortness of breath and sharp chest pain that increases with inspiration. He is alert, warm, and diaphoretic. His vital signs are RR = 24, P = 100, and BP = 122/84. His breath sounds are diminished in the lower right lung field. He denies any medical history, does not smoke, and takes no medication. Which of the following conditions best describes the patient’s signs and symptoms?
questions.explanation: While it is possible that he could have a pulmonary embolism, the more likely culprit is a simple pneumothorax. He has few risk factors for a PE, and lung sounds usually remain present unless a large area of pulmonary tissue is not perfused for some period of time.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A 64-year-old male complains of weakness and dizziness. He is anxious, with a patent airway with adequate breathing. The pulse is rapid and skin cool and diaphoretic. Auscultation of the lung reveals bilateral rales. His vital signs are pulse, 156; respirations, 28 per minute; blood pressure, 82/60 mmHg; and SpO2 92%. His ECG is depicted in Figure 4.1. The best treatment for this patient is to
questions.explanation: The patient is experiencing an unstable atrial fibrillation. He is hypotensive and tachypneic. In this situation, it is imperative to capture the atrial rate as soon as possible. The ventricular rate will decrease once the atria are under control.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding a spontaneous pneumothorax is true?
options: It usually requires prehospital chest decompression.
It is usually limited to only 20% of the lung and is well tolerated by the patient.
It often progresses to a tension pneumothorax if not relieved.
It usually occurs in individuals more than 50 years of age who are otherwise healthy. Figure 4.1
questions.explanation: A spontaneous pneuothorax occurs when a bleb (cystic lesion on the lobe of the lung) ruptures, allowing air to enter the pleural space from within the lung. It usually occurs in otherwise healthy individuals ages 20 to 40. They are usually well tolerated and occupy less than 20% of a lung.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The best place to assess for cyanosis in an infant or child is the
questions.explanation: The best place to assess for cyanosis in an infant or child is the oral mucosa, lips, or tongue. Nail beds of an infant may not be an accurate indicator of central circulation status even when they appear cyanotic.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is a 4-year-old girl who awoke in the middle of the night with a cough that her mother describes as sounding “like a seal barking.” The patient feels more comfortable sitting up. Vital signs are: respirations, 26/min; pulse, 100; temperature, 101° F. On physical exam, you hear stridor on inspiration. From this scenario, you should suspect which condition?
questions.explanation: The signs, symptoms, and assessment findings of croup are described. The seal-bark cough is a classic presentation with croup.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What volume of fluid bolus should be given initially to a severely dehydrated child?
questions.explanation: Give a severely dehydrated child an initial bolus of 20 cc/kg of normal saline or Ringer’s solution. Reassess for response and repeat with 10 or 20 cc/kg boluses as long as the child continues to improve and you do not detect any signs of fluid overload.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 21-year-old female in active labor. She is two weeks from her expected due date and is having contractions of 1.5 minutes duration, which are three minutes apart. This is her second pregnancy. Her first child was delivered vaginally at full term. What is your first course of action for this patient?
options: Place her on the stretcher in high Fowler’s or comfortable position and examine her for crowning.
Place her in the left-lateral recumbent position, apply oxygen, and transport her -immediately.
Call her obstetrician and advise him or her that the birth is imminent.
Place her in position of comfort with a sanitary napkin over her vagina and transport rapidly.
questions.explanation: In this situation, the first step would be to examine the patient for crowning to determine if you need to assist with delivery on the scene or if you can attempt to transport her.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 21-year-old female in active labor. She is two weeks from her expected due date and is having contractions of 1.5 minutes duration, which are three minutes apart. This is her second pregnancy. Her first child was delivered vaginally at full term. Your patient suddenly tells you she feels something slippery between her legs. Upon visual examination, you notice a two-inch segment of the umbilical cord protruding from the vagina. What is this condition called?
questions.explanation: The protruding umbilical cord is known as a prolapsed cord.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 21-year-old female in active labor. She is two weeks from her expected due date and is having contractions of 1.5 minutes duration, which are three minutes apart. This is her second pregnancy. Her first child was delivered vaginally at full term. Which of the following is NOT an appropriate treatment option for this patient?
options: Provide high-flow oxygen and rapidly transport the mother in the knee-chest position.
Take pressure off the cord by placing your fingers into the vagina and gently lifting the infant.
Wrap the cord in a moist sterile dressing, provide supplemental oxygen, and transport quickly.
Place the mother in the right-lateral recumbent position, and -transport immediately.
questions.explanation: Choices a, b, and c are all appropriate treatment options for this patient. If you opt to -position the patient for transport, you should place her in the left-lateral recumbent position to improve uterine blood flow and return.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are called to the home of a 21-year-old female in active labor. She is two weeks from her expected due date and is having contractions of 1.5 minutes duration, which are three minutes apart. This is her second pregnancy. Her first child was delivered vaginally at full term. You are ready to transport this patient. If the umbilical cord is still exposed, how can you use it to evaluate the infant’s perfusion?
options: Gently feel the cord for pulsations to determine the infant’s heart rate.
Determine the infant’s temperature by feeling the cord for changes.
Look at the umbilical cord for color changes, with blue indicating hypoxia.
Attach a pulse oximetry lead to the cord and determine the oxygen saturation.
questions.explanation: The umbilical vein, found within the umbilical cord, provides oxygenated blood to the infant. The vein is large enough for you to feel the pulsations as blood flows from the placenta to the fetus. If the cord cools down, an arterial vaso-spasm may occur, resulting in the cessation of blood flow to the infant, but the cord temperature does not correlate to the infant’s -temperature, ruling out choice b. The umbilical cord has various color gradations from red to purplish and the relationship between color and oxygen level is difficult to determine, ruling out choice c. Pulse oximetry is not appropriate for fetal monitoring in the method described, ruling out choice d.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You arrive at the scene of an imminent delivery in the field. The first responder, who called for assistance, reports that the patient is a 32-yearold female who is “G4, P3.” What does this mean?
options: The patient is pregnant for the seventh time and has three living children.
The patient’s cervix has dilated a total of four centimeters in three hours.
The patient has been pregnant four times and delivered three live children.
The patient has had four rounds of contractions timed three minutes apart.
questions.explanation: G4, P3 refers to a woman who has been pregnant four times and delivered three live infants.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is a 28-year-old female who reports that she is nine weeks pregnant. She is complaining of severe abdominal pain, shoulder pain, and vaginal bleeding. Vital signs are within normal limits, and a physical exam reveals tenderness in the lower-left quadrant. What should you suspect is occuring?
questions.explanation: The signs and symptoms of an ectopic pregnancy are given. Most ectopic pregnancies implant within the fallopian tube and have attained a large enough size around nine weeks to rupture the tube, resulting in intense pain and bleeding. The bleeding may or may not be present from the vagina. Most cases of spontaneous abortion are undetected by the mother, who often doesn’t even know she is pregnant, and present as an “abnormal menstrual cycle.”
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What does the term effacement refer to?
options: the direction the fetus is facing during the birth
the position of the fetus in the uterus prior to birth
thinning of the cervix during the first stage of labor
opening of the cervix during the last stage of labor
questions.explanation: Effacement refers to the stretching and thinning of the cervix, which occurs during the first stage of normal labor.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is a 33-year-old woman who is nine months pregnant. She complains of severe abdominal pain and abdominal tenderness. She reports there is no vaginal bleeding at this time. What should you should suspect?
questions.explanation: The signs and symptoms of abruptio placentae, or premature separatation of the placenta, are given.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following signs and symptoms would be present in a pregnant patient with preeclampsia?
options: high blood pressure, normal pulse rate, and respiratory rate
high blood pressure, headaches, edema, and visual disturbances
high blood pressure, edema, excessive weight gain, and seizures
high blood pressure, abdominal pain, and bright-red bleeding
questions.explanation: Patients with preeclampsia (or toxemia of pregnancy) manifest all the signs and symptoms of the hypertensive disorders of pregnancy except seizures. Once the patient begins to experience seizures, the condition has changed from preeclampsia to eclampsia.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: When does the third stage of labor begin?
options: when contractions are five minutes apart
when the cervix is fully dilated
immediately upon the birth of the baby
questions.explanation: The third stage of labor begins with the birth of the fetus and ends with the delivery of the placenta.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the correct procedure for cutting the umbilical cord after the birth of the baby?
options: Milk the cord of all blood and cut it no more than 5 cm from the infant.
Clamp the cord close to the infant and cut it between the infant and the clamp.
Clamp the cord in two places and cut it near the infant and near the placenta.
Clamp the cord in two places 5 cm apart and cut it between the clamps.
questions.explanation: Clamp the cord in two places, approximately 5 cm apart, and cut the cord between the clamps. Generally, you want to place the first clamp several (5–7) inches away from the infant.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the appropriate range for the heart rate of a healthy neonate immediately after birth?
questions.explanation: Heart rate at birth is normally 150–180 beats per minute, slowing to 130–140 within a few minutes.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What does the presence of meconium on the neonate or in the amniotic fluid indicate?
options: The infant may have been distressed.
questions.explanation: The presence of meconium indicates that the fetus may have been distressed before birth.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You would perform chest compressions on any newborn whose heart rate is less than how many beats per minute?
questions.explanation: Chest compressions are required when a newborn’s heart rate is less than 60, or between 60 and 80 after 30 seconds of positive-pressure ventilation. Remember to perform each intervention for approximately 30 seconds, then reassess for the need to continue resuscitation.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Medications and drugs are most often delivered to a newborn through the use of which circulatory vessel?
questions.explanation: The umbilical vein, located in the umbilical cord, is used for this purpose. If the cord is left untreated at the hospital, it may be cannulated for a week or even longer. It enters immediately into the hepatic circulation.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You respond to a 2-year-old female who is postictal following seizure activity. The patient’s parents report that the child was sleeping when she began to shake and turn blue. She has had a runny nose, but she has had no medications lately. There is no history of seizures. This patient is most likely suffering from which condition?
questions.explanation: Fever-induced seizures are common in young children with only minor illnesses. Once a child has a febrile seizure, he or she is prone to repeat episodes, which can occur at lower temperatures than the first seizure.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to a 2-year-old female who is postictal following seizure activity. The patient’s parents report that the child was sleeping when she began to shake and turn blue. She has had a runny nose, but she has had no medications lately. There is no history of seizures. Which of the following vital signs would you expect this patient to have?
options: increased body temperature, tachycardia, and tachypnea
normal temperature, normal blood pressure, and bradycardia
increased temperature, increased blood pressure, and bradycardia
normal body temperature, tachycardia, and bradypnea
questions.explanation: Increased body temperature, tachycardia, and tachypnea are common in a child who is recently postictal from febrile seizures.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to a 2-year-old female who is postictal following seizure activity. The patient’s parents report that the child was sleeping when she began to shake and turn blue. She has had a runny nose, but she has had no medications lately. There is no history of seizures. What would be the appropriate treatment if this patient continues in a prolonged postictal state?
options: Obtain blood glucose readings and administer 25% dextrose as needed.
Apply ice packs to the armpits and groin.
Remove the child’s excess clothing, administer oxygen and an IV, and transport.
Administer high-concentration oxygen, diazepam, and naloxone, and transport.
questions.explanation: Oxygen, an IV, and transport is an appropriate treatment for this patient. Remove excess clothing from the patient to passively cool him or her, but do not allow the patient to get chilled. Sponge the child with roomtemperature water if the temperature is excessively high. Never use alcohol on the skin as a cooling agent. Alcohol can be absorbed directly through the skin.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are caring for a 63-year-old male patient with renal failure. He is pulseless and apneic. Figure 4.2 depicts his ECG. Appropriate treatment for this patient would include all of the following EXCEPT
options: administration of dextrose and sodium bicarbonate.
administration of sodium bicarbonate and calcium gluconate.
questions.explanation: This patient is in cardiac arrest with a cardiac rhythm of Ventricular Tachycardia (VTach). Because the patient has renal failure, the initial approach should consist of rapid defibrillation and antidisrhythmics. All such patients should be assumed to be hyperkalemic and should be treated with a combination of sodium bicarbonate and calcium gluconate, which work together to move the potassium into cells and out of the bloodstream.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which situation would constitute a moral dilemma for a paramedic?
options: a female rape victim who insists on being cared for only by a female paramedic or EMT
a patient who has sustained a potentially serious head injury but refuses care or transport
a patient who signed a Do Not Resuscitate order who is now unconscious and dying
a patient who is found unconscious with no family member present to authorize care
questions.explanation: This situation constitutes a dilemma because the paramedic would have to choose between the duty to provide care and the duty to obtain consent. If a patient has a signed DNR order, you should honor his or her order. When a patient is unconscious, you treat him or her under the doctrine of implied consent. The rape victim is simply requesting an all-female crew, and you should try to accommodate her wishes.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: A patient with a PMH of emphysema experiences a sudden onset of respiratory distress and sharp chest pain. His skin is warm and dry. His breath sounds are diminished on the left side. Your strongest suspicion is
questions.explanation: The patient’s rapid onset reduces the possibility of pneumonia. No information indicates any other medical condition such as asthma or chronic bronchitis. Patients with emphysema are prone to experiencing simple pneumothorax.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are called to a physician’s office to care for a patient who is experiencing symptoms of a myocardial infarction. The physician tells you that she will stabilize the patient herself and that she is assuming responsibility for care. She does, however, wish for you to transport the patient to the hospital. How should you proceed?
options: Defer to the on-scene physician and take her to the hospital with you.
Request online medical direction from your jurisdictional physician.
Ignore her request and assume responsibility for the patient immediately.
Insist that the on-scene physician communicate with medical control.
questions.explanation: Paramedics should defer to a physician who is present on scene provided this individual is assuming responsibility for the continued care of this patient. Make sure you know the rules and regulations for your jurisdiction regarding on-scene physicians.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What does a type II ambulance usually consist of?
options: standard van usually with a raised roof
special cab-forward van with an integral body
modified suburban vehicle that can provide -transport
questions.explanation: A type II ambulance is an integral unit consisting of a standard van, usually with a raised roof.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: What do you call a legal document that specifies what type of treatment a patient does and does not want to receive?
questions.explanation: The living will and DNR order are both -examples of legal documents called advanced directives. They specify the kind of healthcare a person does and does not want to receive in the event of their imminent death.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: At what age is a person considered capable of giving consent to treatment?
questions.explanation: In most states, consent for treatment must be obtained from all patients who are 18 years old or older. This can be modified in situations of extenuating circumstances, such as when an underage minor is pregnant or has legal custody of a minor child in his or her care.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: An unconscious patient has snoring respirations. When should this condition be corrected?
options: on completion of the initial assessment
on assessing for the presence of a pulse
questions.explanation: Snoring respirations are indicative of an airway issue and should be corrected before further assessment is completed.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the process of transmitting physiological data from the field to the hospital over the phone lines called?
questions.explanation: Transmitting physiological data over phone lines is called biotelemetry.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are caring for a female patient who was struck by an automobile. She opens her eyes to voice command only, localizes pain when you pinch her arm, and is awake but confused. Her Glasgow Coma Scale is
questions.explanation: The GCS for this patient is 12: three points for opening her eyes to verbal stimuli, five points for localizing pain, and four points for speaking clearly although her thought processes is confused.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: You are the first paramedic unit to arrive on the scene of a multi-injury bus crash. What is your first responsibility?
options: Assume command of the incident and give a preliminary report to dispatch.
Wait until an incident commander arrives on scene and then follow his or her direction.
Extract patients from the bus and triage them into categories by color or priority.
Review and evaluate the efficiency of site operations up until your arrival.
questions.explanation: The first paramedic unit to arrive at the scene of a mass-casualty incident would immediately assume command and transmit a report to dispatch, alerting them to the need for more units. As other units begin to arrive, they may be detailed to perform triage or some other duty.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: A patient presents with shallow breaths at a rate of six per minute. What should you do next?
options: Check for carotid and radial pulses.
Administer positive-pressure ventilation with a BVM.
Identify the specific cause of the respiratory distress.
questions.explanation: The respiratory rate is too slow and must be corrected immediately with ventilatory assistance.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Under the START triage method, which patient would receive immediate respiratory treatment without further assessment?
questions.explanation: In the START system, a patient with respirations greater than 30 per minute would receive immediate attention. Patient a would not receive any care beyond the initial opening of the airway. Patients b and c would receive additional assessment before treatment.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What does a red tag mean in the METTAG triage system?
questions.explanation: When using the METTAG system, a red tag indicates a patient with critical injuries who needs rapid transport.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Your friend experiences severe anxiety when crossing bridges. She refuses to cross any bridges and alters her travel routes accordingly. This is an example of which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: An intense fear of something is called a phobia. A phobia can be disabling and its cause is often unknown.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the difference between bulimia and anorexia nervosa?
options: They are basically the same disorder.
Bulimia is an intense fear of obesity, whereas anorexia is the insatiable craving for food.
Anorexia is less severe and rarely life threatening.
Bulimia is more often associated with binge eating.
questions.explanation: Bulimia and anorexia nervosa are both common eating disorders. They both have similar features such as misperceptions of body image. Bulimia is characterized by binge eating followed by self-induced vomiting.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: On reaching the scene of a single-motor-vehicle accident, you note that the driver is pinned behind the steering wheel. You also note the presence of two sets of spiderweb patterns on the windshield. What does this alert you to?
options: Look for a second victim in this accident.
Suspect multiple injuries for this driver.
Assume a high-speed collision has occured.
Expect that hit-and-run injuries have occured.
questions.explanation: The spiderweb pattern is made when a victim’s head hits the windshield. Two spiderweb patterns indicate that there is a second victim somewhere on the scene.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which patient is likely to need rapid transport to a trauma center rather than assessment and stabilization on the scene?
options: male, age 56, ejected from a crashed vehicle with a flail chest
female, age 60, burns to 10% BSI on her chest and abdomen
male, age 28, fell 10 feet from a platform onto a pile of mulch
female, age 46, struck by car traveling 10 mph, no penetrating injuries
questions.explanation: All the others do not have critical mechanisms, so they should not receive rapid transportation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A male patient is complaining of crushing chest pressure and shortness of breath. His vital signs include RR = 20, P = 54, and BP = 132/74. The EKG shows a sinus bradycardia with a first-degree AV block. Which of the following treatments would be most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Although his heart rate is slow, the patient is not hypotensive. The other three answer choices address symptomatic bradycardia, not the situation this patient presents in.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: For which procedure is it necessary to wear a mask and protective eyewear?
options: giving an IM or SC injection
questions.explanation: Commonly accepted infection-control guidelines call for all personnel to wear masks and -protective eyewear for any procedure that carries the risk of splashing of blood, vomitus, or other fluids.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the most common job-related source of HIV infections among healthcare workers?
options: direct contact with a patient’s skin
questions.explanation: Accidental needle sticks are the most common source of work-related HIV and hepatitis B infections in healthcare workers.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is the leading cause of death among the elderly?
questions.explanation: Because cardiac disease is so common, you should administer medications commonly prescribed for other types of emergencies with extreme caution.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What should you do when confronted with a patient whom you suspect to be a victim of elder abuse?
options: Ask the patient if he or she wants or needs your help.
Quietly confront the family with your suspicions.
Report your suspicions to the appropriate authority.
Search the family’s medical records for prior signs of abuse.
questions.explanation: Report your suspicions promptly. Many states have laws requiring you to report any suspicions of child abuse, elder abuse, or domestic violence.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Your patient is 2 years old. How can you reassure her before listening to her chest with your stethoscope?
options: Explain in detail exactly how the stethoscope works.
Gain her trust by letting her listen to your chest first.
Hold up the stethoscope so she can see it won’t hurt her.
Let her take the stethoscope apart so she isn’t afraid.
questions.explanation: Toddlers can often be reassured by being allowed to handle unfamiliar objects. They will not understand detailed explanations and should not be allowed to disassemble equipment.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Battle’s sign and periorbital ecchymosis are classic signs of which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Battle’s sign is the black-and-blue discoloration just behind the ears. Periorbital ecchymosis is black-and-blue discoloration around the eyes, also known as raccoon eyes. Both are classic signs of a basilar skull fracture.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: There are many factors to consider when assessing a patient for suicide risk. Which of the -following accurately accounts for one suicide risk factor?
options: Young women commit suicide more often than young men do.
Generally, men attempt suicide more often than women do.
Caucasian males over 85 years of age have the highest suicide rate.
Suicide is the leading cause of death in Hispanic women under 30.
questions.explanation: Women attempt suicide more often, but men are more successful at it. Also, men choose more deadly means. About 60% of the people who -successfully commit suicide have a history of previous attempts.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What sort of communication system would you need to be able to carry on a two-way conversation with a physician while also transmitting telemetry?
questions.explanation: A multiplex system allows for a two-way conversation and simultaneous transmission of telemetry readings.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is not a component of the start triage method?
questions.explanation: Neuromuscular function is not part of the START algorithm. START assesses respirations, pulse, and mental status.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Which organs are contained in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen?
options: spleen, tail of pancreas, stomach, left kidney, and part of the colon
liver, gall bladder, head of pancreas, part of duodenum, and part of the colon
appendix, ascending colon, small intestine, right ovary, and fallopian tube
small intestine, descending colon, left ovary, and fallopian tube
questions.explanation: This choice lists the organs that can be palpated in the right upper quadrant (liver, gall bladder, head of pancreas, part of duodenum, and part of the colon).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Chest pain associated with stable angina may be caused by
options: a buildup of lactic acid and CO2.
questions.explanation: Stable angina is caused by a temporary lowflow state in the coronary arteries that does not lead to cell injury or death (infarction). During hypoperfusion, lactic acid and excessive carbon dioxide are not carried away, causing irritation and pain.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 65-year-old female complaining of chest pressure. The EKG shows a wide complex tachycardia at a rate of 200 beats per minute. The patient’s vital signs are P = 200, RR = 26, and BP 90/60. Which of the following answers is the best treatment for this patient?
questions.explanation: Due to the low blood pressure, this patient would be classified as unstable. Immediate intervention will be needed to correct this condition. Delivering medications may take too long to administer. Defibrillation may invoke ventricular fibrillation.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What does the P of the AVPU acronym mean?
options: level of pain they are feeling
questions.explanation: In the AVPU acronym, used to assess level of consciousness, the P stands for the patient’s response to painful stimuli.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What are the components of the focused history and physical exam?
options: ABC and LOC assessment and vital signs
head-to-toe check and treatment of injuries
vital signs and detailed physical exam
questions.explanation: The focused history and physical exam, undertaken only after immediate threats to life have been corrected, consists of ascertaining the nature of illness or injury, previous history (via SAMPLE), vital signs, and focused exam.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What does pulse pressure refer to?
options: diastolic blood pressure reading  the systolic reading
systolic  the diastolic blood pressure reading
difference between the systolic and diastolic readings
systolic blood pressure as measured by a Doppler device
questions.explanation: Pulse pressure refers to the difference between the diastolic and systolic blood pressure readings. A narrowing pulse pressure indicates increasing diastolic pressure and decreasing systolic pressure. Perfusion will stop once both pressures come together.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 56-year-old male experienced a syncopal episode and is now complaining of intense chest pressure. His vital signs are RR = 26, P = 110, and BP = 80/64. Crackles can be auscultated in both lower lung fields. Which of the following conditions best defines the patient’s presentation?
questions.explanation: The patient’s heart is not pumping efficiently, possibly due to an MI. This would result in a loss of blood pressure throughout the cardiovascular system, including the pulmonary vessels. The lack of pressure causes plasma to leak into the interstitial space of the lungs, causing the adventitious breath sounds.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: An elderly male is complaining of a sudden onset of severe pain in his right leg. The affected extremity is cool to the touch and pale. The temperature and pulse in the patient’s left leg is normal. You suspect
questions.explanation: A sudden loss of blood flow to the leg within the arterial bed would account for the sudden change in skin color and temperature.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You arrive at a golf course to find a 45-year-old male unconscious and responsive to pain but only with movement. The patient was struck in the head by a golf ball traveling at high velocity. His eyes are closed; pupil examination reveals his left pupil is 2 mm, and the right is 8 mm and not reactive to light. This patient moves upper extremities to localized pain and moves lower extremities spontaneously. He is breathing full deep respirations at a rate of 24 per minute. You would expect the vital signs of this patient generally to follow which of the following groupings?
options: RR increased, HR decreased, BP decreased
RR increased, HR decreased, BP increased
RR decreased, HR decreased, BP decreased
RR decreased, HR increased, BP increased
questions.explanation: This set of vitals, known as Cushing’s triad (respiratory rate increased, heart rate decreased, blood pressure increased), is common in closed head injuries.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You arrive at a golf course to find a 45-year-old male unconscious and responsive to pain but only with movement. The patient was struck in the head by a golf ball traveling at high velocity. His eyes are closed; pupil examination reveals his left pupil is 2 mm, and the right is 8 mm and not reactive to light. This patient moves upper extremities to localized pain and moves lower extremities spontaneously. He is breathing full deep respirations at a rate of 24 per minute. Treatment of this patient would include which of the following?
options: spinal immobilization to a long board with cervical collar
IV fluid boluses titrated to a systolic BP of 100–110 mmHg
opening the patient’s airway using the headtilt/chin-lift technique
placing the patient in the sniffing position to facilitate airflow
questions.explanation: Placing the victim in the sniffing position manipulates the head and neck, facilitating airflow.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You arrive at a golf course to find a 45-year-old male unconscious and responsive to pain but only with movement. The patient was struck in the head by a golf ball traveling at high velocity. His eyes are closed; pupil examination reveals his left pupil is 2 mm, and the right is 8 mm and not reactive to light. This patient moves upper extremities to localized pain and moves lower extremities spontaneously. He is breathing full deep respirations at a rate of 24 per minute. A patient with a closed head injury should be closely monitored for all of the following except
questions.explanation: Choices a, b, and c are all common in closed head injuries; choice d (hemopneumothorax) is not.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient, a car accident victim, complains of seeing “a dark curtain” in front of one eye. What should you suspect?
questions.explanation: A patient with a detached retina will often complain of seeing a dark curtain in front of part of the field of vision.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Treatment for a patient in cardiogenic shock who is complaining of chest pressure should include
questions.explanation: Although the patient may be complaining of chest discomfort, administering nitroglycerin would only worsen an already low blood pressure. Even with fluid leaking into the interstitial lung tissue secondary to the low blood pressure in the pulmonary circulation, a diuretic would also carry the same risk of reducing pressure even more. This patient requires a vasoactive medication such as dopamine to increase blood pressure.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Signs and symptoms of traumatic asphyxia includes which of the following?
options: paradoxical chest motion, pain on inspiration, and increased respiratory rate
dyspnea, bloodshot eyes, distended neck veins, and a cyanotic upper body
agitation, air hunger, distended neck veins, shock, and tracheal displacement
shock, cyanosis, absent breath sounds over one lobe, and flat neck veins
questions.explanation: Traumatic asphyxia occurs when serious rib injury pushes the chest wall inward, resulting in severe hypoventilation and backflow of venous blood. Important signs and symptoms of this include dyspnea, bloodshot eyes, distended neck veins, and a cyanotic upper body.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: An adult patient has burns covering her head and upper back. Using the rule of nines, this patient’s burns cover what percentage of her body surface area?
questions.explanation: The head and upper back are each equal to 9% of body surface area.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: How would you classify a burn that is pearly white and almost painless?
questions.explanation: This is often the appearance of a third-degree burn, which is painless because of destruction of nerve cells.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following conditions indicates the need for rapid transport?
options: isolated penetrating trauma by a knife in the upper forearm
a pedestrian struck by a motor vehicle traveling about 10 mph
first-degree and second-degree burns to the anterior chest
a pulse rate, 130; blood pressure, 90/60; and respiratory rate, 36/min
questions.explanation: The vital signs listed in choice d indicate the presence of shock. An isolated injury of penetrating trauma to the upper arm is not generally life threatening. A 10-mile-per-hour impact to a pedestrian is not generally a significant MOI. Burns to the entire chest cover a body surface area of approximately 9%; this would not in itself require rapid transport unless other problems existed.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the Glasgow Coma Scale Score for a patient who opens her eyes in response to pain, speaks incomprehensibly, and withdraws in response to pain?
questions.explanation: Eye opening = 2; verbal response = 2; motor response = 4.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Your patient is a 23-year-old man who complains of abdominal pain. The patient states that the pain began suddenly and was originally located only in the area around the umbilicus. Now, however, it has moved to the right lower quadrant. The patient also complains of nausea and vomiting, and he has a fever of 102° F. Examination displays rebound tenderness. What condition should you suspect?
questions.explanation: The classic signs and symptoms of appendicitis are given.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 42-year-old male complains of sudden, intense pain that is centered in his lower back. He is pale, cool, and diaphoretic, especially below the level of his umbilicus. He is tachycardic and hypotensive. Which of the following conditions best describes the patient presentation?
questions.explanation: The abdominal aorta is located in the retroperitoneal space. A sudden loss of pressure due to an aortic aneurysm will result in loss of perfusion below the site of injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your patient is an 76-year-old male who is complaining of malaise. He has no medical history and takes no medications. His BP is 112/70, pulse is 60 and irregular, respirations 20, lungs clear and equal bilaterally, skin is warm and dry. Figure 5.1 depicts his ECG. Prehospital management of this patient includes which of the following?
questions.explanation: This patient is suffering from second-degree AV block type 1 (Wenckebach), which is an intermittent block at the AV node. There is generally no treatment necessary for stable patients with second-degree AV heart block type I other than oxygen and monitoring.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What does the initial symptom of infection with HIV primarily consist of?
questions.explanation: Although symptoms of full-blown AIDS include Kaposi’s sarcoma and opportunistic infections, initial symptoms of infection with the AIDS virus often consist only of mild fatigue and fever.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 63-year-old male is complaining of substernal chest pain radiating to his left arm and jaw. He is diaphoretic and cool to the touch. The cardiac monitor shows sinus tachycardia. The patient’s vital signs are BP = 136/70, P = 118, and RR = 16. You have administered oxygen and established an IV. What should you do now?
options: Prepare to administer atropine IV push.
Sedate the patient and then proceed with synchronized countershock at 50 joules.
Administer sublingual nitroglycerin and evaluate the patient’s response to the medication.
questions.explanation: The patient’s heart rate is not fast enough to be the primary source of the chest pain. Therefore, administration of nitroglycerin may resolve the chief complaint, which in turn may slow the heart rate down.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 78-year-old woman who is complaining of diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Physical examination reveals abdominal distention and absent bowel sounds. You should suspect she has which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: The patient is displaying signs and symptoms of bowel obstruction.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Atypical signs and symptoms of myocardial infarction that are commonly seen in elderly patients include all of the following except
questions.explanation: There are many atypical signs and symptoms of MI in elderly patients; choices a, b, and d list three of them. A tearing sensation in the chest generally indicates a dissecting aneurysm is occuring.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which breathing pattern is characteristic of diabetic ketoacidosis or other types of metabolic acidosis?
questions.explanation: Kussmaul’s breathing is characteristic of diabetic ketoacidosis.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Normal vesicular breath sounds are usually described as having which of the following characteristics?
options: medium pitch, medium loudness, with equal inspiratory and expiratory phases
high pitch, soft, with a long inspiratory phase and a long expiratory phase
low pitch, soft, with a long inspiratory phase and a short expiratory phase
medium pitch, soft, with a short inspiratory phase and a long expiratory phase
questions.explanation: The correct description of normal vesicular breath sounds (low in pitch, soft, with a long inspiratory phase and a short expiratory phase) is given.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: What is the purpose of the OPQRST mnemonic?
questions.explanation: The OPQRST mnemonic is used to define the chief complaint associated with medical conditions such as pain, dyspnea, dizziness, and vague sensations. It is not usually used in trauma or actual unconsciousness.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 70-year-old male. He complains of chest pain that began while he was raking leaves. You perform an initial assessment and a focused history and physical examination and administer oxygen and nitroglycerin. The patient then states that he feels much better. What is he most likely suffering from?
questions.explanation: The pain of stable angina is brought on by -exertion and relieved by rest, oxygen, and nitroglycerin.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A 25-year-old female complains of diffuse lower-abdominal pain, vaginal discharge, and low-grade fever. Which of the following conditions best describes the patient’s presentation?
questions.explanation: PID causes lower-abdominal discomfort that is difficult to localize and often has an associated fever. Pain associated with an ectopic pregnancy is more localized, as is the pain associated with solid organ involvement.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What do the signs and symptoms of hypertensive crisis include?
options: pitting edema, tachycardia, tachypnea, and venous congestion
paralysis, seizures, headache, and altered level of consciousness
severe respiratory distress, apprehension, cyanosis, and diaphoresis
restlessness, confusion, blurred vision, nausea, and vomiting
questions.explanation: The most common signs and symptoms of hypertensive emergency (restlessness, confusion, blurred vision, nausea, and vomiting) are given.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Why are vital sign changes not a good early indicator of shock in a young healthy adult?
options: Patients often display false-positive vital sign readings during shock.
The vital signs are often too low to be measured accurately during shock.
The body attempts to compensate by maintaining normal vital signs.
Signs and symptoms of shock are based on neurological findings only.
questions.explanation: The body’s physiological mechanisms compensate for the insult that causes shock. Therefore, although changes in vital signs are ominous late signs in patients with poor tissue perfusion, they are unlikely to occur in a young healthy adult who has just entered a state of shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: During an emergency delivery, the newborn’s head presents in the canal. After suctioning, how can you assist with the delivery of the anterior shoulder?
options: Gently pull on the infant’s head.
Gently guide the infant’s head upward.
Gently guide the infant’s head downward.
Rotate the infant’s head to the transverse position.
questions.explanation: The head will tend to drop down as the shoulders begin to pass through the birth canal. The paramedic can gently guide the head to help with the process.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: The physiological cause of the anxiety and restlessness that make up the classic early signs of shock are a direct result of which of the following phenomena?
questions.explanation: The release of catecholamines that results from the initial drop in blood pressure causes the feelings of anxiety and restlessness.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You arrive to find a 65-year-old male in acute respiratory distress. You hear wheezes from across the room, and you note extreme accessory muscle use. The patient has assumed a tripod position and is breathing through pursed lips. Your physical exam reveals a barrel chest and stained fingernails. Vital signs are blood pressure, 160/90; pulse, 100, strong and irregular with atrial fibrillation on the cardiac monitor; and respiratory rate, 40, with shallow and labored breathing. Ausculation of the chest reveals wheezes and diminished lung sounds throughout all fields. What is this patient most likely suffering from?
questions.explanation: This patient is exhibiting the classic signs and symptoms of emphysema.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You arrive to find a 65-year-old male in acute respiratory distress. You hear wheezes from across the room, and you note extreme accessory muscle use. The patient has assumed a tripod position and is breathing through pursed lips. Your physical exam reveals a barrel chest and stained fingernails. Vital signs are blood pressure, 160/90; pulse, 100, strong and irregular with atrial fibrillation on the cardiac monitor; and respiratory rate, 40, with shallow and labored breathing. Ausculation of the chest reveals wheezes and diminished lung sounds throughout all fields. Which of the following medications would not be used to treat this patient’s condition?
questions.explanation: Epinephrine 1:1,000 is not indicated for this patient.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You arrive to find a 65-year-old male in acute respiratory distress. You hear wheezes from across the room, and you note extreme accessory muscle use. The patient has assumed a tripod position and is breathing through pursed lips. Your physical exam reveals a barrel chest and stained fingernails. Vital signs are blood pressure, 160/90; pulse, 100, strong and irregular with atrial fibrillation on the cardiac monitor; and respiratory rate, 40, with shallow and labored breathing. Ausculation of the chest reveals wheezes and diminished lung sounds throughout all fields. Why is this patient breathing through pursed lips?
options: to provide positive pressure to inflate the alveoli
to minimize mouth movement during ventilation
to try and retain carbon dioxide in the lung fields
to “blow off” carbon dioxide to increase blood pH
