questions.explanation: Breathing through pursed lips is a common compensatory mechanism COPD patients use to provide positive end-expiratory pressure, which forces more alveoli to inflate.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Under what circumstance could the pulse oximetry reading show a falsely elevated reading in a patient?
options: when the patient is exposed to radioactivity
when the patient is exposed to carbon monoxide
when the patient is exposed to pyrexins
when the patient is exposed to carbon tetrachloride
questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide poisoning can cause a falsely high pulse oximetry reading.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which statement about airway obstruction caused by the tongue is incorrect?
options: The tongue is the most common cause of airway obstruction in an unconscious patient.
Airway blockage does not depend on the position of the patient’s head, neck, and jaw.
The esophagus and epiglottis can contribute to airway blockage in an unconscious patient.
The tongue can block the airway only when the patient is recumbent or supine.
questions.explanation: Blockage of the airway by the tongue can occur when the patient is in any position.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which technique should you use to open the airway of a trauma patient?
questions.explanation: The jaw thrust is used to open the airway of patients with suspected cervical spine injury. Any trauma patient with questionable or unknown mechanism of injury should be assumed to have a cervical spine unjury until it is ruled out.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is bronchiolitis caused by?
questions.explanation: The respiratory syncytial virus, which causes only mild upper-respiratory infections in older persons, causes bronchiolitis, a serious respiratory infection, in infants and young children.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 30-year-old female who is complaining of a generalized rash and dyspnea after eating shellfish. The patient has small itchy, red welts all over her body and says her tongue feels like it is swollen. She complains of difficulty moving air in and difficulty catching a full breath. This patient’s vital signs show a blood pressure of 110/60; a pulse of 100, strong and regular; and a respiratory rate of This patient is exhibiting the signs and symptoms of
questions.explanation: This patient’s blood pressure is still compensating for the allergic reaction; therefore, the patient is not in anaphylactic shock.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 30-year-old female who is complaining of a generalized rash and dyspnea after eating shellfish. The patient has small itchy, red welts all over her body and says her tongue feels like it is swollen. She complains of difficulty moving air in and difficulty catching a full breath. This patient’s vital signs show a blood pressure of 110/60; a pulse of 100, strong and regular; and a respiratory rate of Medications used to treat this patient may include which of the following?
questions.explanation: Isuprel, haldol, and dopamine are not -indicated for this patient. Hydroxyzine is an anti-histamine that can be used to treat the puritis (itchy red welts).
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 30-year-old female who is complaining of a generalized rash and dyspnea after eating shellfish. The patient has small itchy, red welts all over her body and says her tongue feels like it is swollen. She complains of difficulty moving air in and difficulty catching a full breath. This patient’s vital signs show a blood pressure of 110/60; a pulse of 100, strong and regular; and a respiratory rate of This patient needs close monitoring because she could progress into which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: This patient needs to be monitored for possible anaphylactic shock.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: After placing an endotracheal tube, you note that breath sounds are much stronger on the right side of the chest than on the left. What does this suggest?
options: The ET tube has been inserted into the right mainstem bronchus.
The patient has developed a pneumothorax on the right side.
The ET tube has not been inserted far enough into the trachea.
The patient has probably aspirated vomitus into the trachea.
questions.explanation: If breath sounds are stronger on one side than on the other, or absent on one side, this suggests that the tube has been inserted too far and is resting in one bronchus.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You have just delivered a baby girl. Evaluation reveals that the infant cries loudly and has a heart rate of 140, her body is pink but the extremities are blue, and she is actively moving all extremities. Her APGAR score is
questions.explanation: With the exception of the blue extremities, the infant scores a “2” on Activity, Pulse, Grimace, and Respirations. Her “Appearance” scores as a 1.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Causes of third-trimester bleeding include
options: ectopic pregnancy, uterine rupture, pelvic inflammatory disease.
abruptio placentae, placenta previa, uterine rupture.
questions.explanation: A ruptured ectopic pregnancy usually occurs within the first trimester. Eclampsia and PID are not associated with bleeding.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is an assessment finding of pulsus paradoxus associated with?
questions.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus, or a drop in blood pressure with each respiratory cycle, is associated with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: The term tracheal tugging refers to which of the following?
options: the use of accessory muscles during respiration
retraction of intercostal muscles during inspiration
cyanosis and nasal flaring with exhalation
retraction of neck tissues during respiration
questions.explanation: Tracheal tugging refers to retraction of neck tissues during respiratory effort.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A dull sound heard during chest percussion may be associated with which condition?
questions.explanation: A dull sound on chest percussion may be -associated with pneumonia, hemothorax, or pulmonary edema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Pulse oximetry allows you to continuously record which of the following?
questions.explanation: Once it is attached to the patient’s finger, the pulse oximeter will continuously record the pulse rate and oxygen saturation level.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 66-year-old man who is extremely thin but has a noticeably distorted barrel-shaped chest. He reports a history of dyspnea that has recently gotten worse. You note that he purses his lips when breathing, but hypoxia is not apparent. In addition to monitoring vital signs, breath sounds, and the ECG, starting an IV, and transporting the patient, what other treatments should you give to this patient?
options: Administer low-flow oxygen and a broncho-dilator.
Administer oxygen via nonrebreather mask and furosemide.
Orally intubate the patient and assist -ventilations.
questions.explanation: The patient is showing signs and symptoms of emphysema. Administer low-flow oxygen to preserve his hypoxic drive.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A 28-year-old woman is complaining of a sudden onset of severe abdominal pain that radiates to her shoulder. Her BP = 88/60, P = 110, and R = 20. Her skin is cool, pale, and clammy. She states that her last normal menstrual period was 6–8 weeks ago. Which of the following conditions best describes the patient presentation?
questions.explanation: A sudden rupture of an ectopic pregnancy would result in a loss of blood that would place the patient in compensatory shock.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What respiratory pattern is characteristic of Kussmaul’s respiration?
options: an increase in both rate and depth
an increase in respiratory rate only
a decrease in both rate and depth
questions.explanation: Kussmaul’s respirations, which are associated with diabetic ketoacidosis, are characterized by increased rate and depth of respirations.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following factors increases the amount of energy necessary for the patient to expend for respiration?
questions.explanation: Loss of pulmonary surfactant, which can occur in pneumonia and other conditions, increases the tendency of the alveoli to collapse and, thus, increases the work necessary for respiration.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What does it mean if the pH of normal urine is 6.0 and the pH of distilled water is 7.0?
options: Urine is twice as acidic as distilled water.
Urine is 10 times as acidic as distilled water.
Urine is twice as alkaline as distilled water.
Urine is 10 times as alkaline as distilled water.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen ion concentration, or pH, is measured on a scale of 1 (highly acidic) to 14 (highly alkaline), with 7 being neutral. For each unit on the scale, the solution is ten times more acidic or alkaline. These measurements are very important in the consideration of the oxygen-diffusing capacity of the blood, where very small changes in pH are highly significant.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 6-year-old child who is conscious but not breathing because of an airway obstruction. What is the first thing you should do for this patient?
options: Give five back blows followed by five chest thrusts.
Visualize the airway and perform a finger sweep.
Open the airway with a head-tilt/chin-lift.
questions.explanation: The first step in treating a conscious child of this age with a complete airway obstruction is to perform the Heimlich maneuver (i.e., subdiaphragmatic abdominal thrusts). Continue until the obstruction is relieved or the child becomes unconscious.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You should attempt to remove foreign material from a patient’s airway with forceps only in what situation?
options: You do not have access to laryngoscopy equipment.
You have tried but failed to suction the airway.
You are unable to insert an endotracheal tube.
You are able to visualize the obstruction directly.
questions.explanation: To prevent tissue damage, you should attempt to physically remove foreign material only if you can actually see the obstruction with a laryngoscope.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 29-year-old female complaining of the sudden onset of severe shortness of breath and chest pain. She indicates that she is recovering from surgery to her left femur after an automobile crash. What is this patient most likely suffering from?
questions.explanation: This patient has symptoms of a pulmonary embolism.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 29-year-old female complaining of the sudden onset of severe shortness of breath and chest pain. She indicates that she is recovering from surgery to her left femur after an automobile crash. You would expect to find which of the following in this patient?
questions.explanation: You would expect to see this patient in -tachycardia rather than bradycardia due to the increasing dyspnea and hypoxia resulting from the pulmonary embolism.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 29-year-old female complaining of the sudden onset of severe shortness of breath and chest pain. She indicates that she is recovering from surgery to her left femur after an automobile crash. This patient’s physiological problems are most likely due to what cardiac problem?
options: the right side of the heart pumping against increased resistance
the left side of the heart pumping against decreased resistance
the right side of the heart pumping against decreased resistance
the left side of the heart pumping against increased resistance
questions.explanation: The pulmonary embolism has caused the right side of the heart to have to pump harder against a resistance caused by the partial blockage. This results in the severe shortness of breath and hypoxia.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 29-year-old female complaining of the sudden onset of severe shortness of breath and chest pain. She indicates that she is recovering from surgery to her left femur after an automobile crash. Proper management of this patient includes which of the following?
questions.explanation: This patient should be transported in the position in which it is easiest for her to breathe (i.e., the shock position).
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 29-year-old female complaining of the sudden onset of severe shortness of breath and chest pain. She indicates that she is recovering from surgery to her left femur after an automobile crash. Which of the following conditions is a common cause of this patient’s problem?
questions.explanation: Myocardial infarction, ruptured aneurism, and anticoagulant use are not common causes of pulmonary embolism.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following combinations are the only two situations in which a prehospital provider should place a gloved hand into the vagina?
questions.explanation: In both of these cases, relieving pressure off the trapped umbilical cord (prolapsed) or the face and airway passages (trapped breech) may be the life-saving measure provided by the paramedic.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: A patient suspected of showing early signs of shock should usually be placed supine with his or her feet elevated. When is this position contraindicated?
options: when a head injury is suspected
if shock is due to hypovolemia
questions.explanation: The shock position is used only if head injury is not suspected.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which statement about deflation of the PASG/ MAST in the field setting is correct?
options: Deflation should be accomplished rapidly in the field.
Deflate the legs first and then the abdominal compartment.
Deflate the garment if the patient begins to experience dyspnea.
Deflation should not be attempted in the field without medical direction.
questions.explanation: Because the PASG corrects a symptom and not the underlying problem, deflation should be attempted only in the hospital after the underlying hypovolemia is corrected.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true regarding a single limb presentation during an emergency delivery?
options: Grasp the presenting part of the baby and gently rotate it so the baby will deliver.
This is a nondeliverable presentation and requires immediate transport to an appropriate receiving facility.
Assist the mother in delivering the baby by applying gentle traction to the limb.
Apply firm pressure to the presenting part to delay birth until the patient is transferred to the emergency department staff.
questions.explanation: A limb presentation during delivery requires a cesarean section to complete the delivery. Provide high-flow oxygen and care to the mother during transport.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: When responding to the scene of a hazardousmaterials incident, the EMS crew should approach from
questions.explanation: The rule of thumb when approaching a hazardous-materials situation can be remembered as being “up, up, and away.” This will help minimize risk exposure to the crew.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: How should you position a patient with a suspected CVA and no other pertinent history?
questions.explanation: The patient should be supine with the head elevated to enhance venous return. If congestive heart failure is present, the patient should be positioned at least semi-Fowler’s (sitting up at least 45°).
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The vital signs of a patient experiencing the later stages of increased intracranial pressure will be characterized by which of the following?
options: increased blood pressure, decreased pulse and respiratory rate
increased blood pressure, pulse, and respiratory rate
decreased blood pressure, increased pulse and respiratory rate
decreased blood pressure, pulse, and respiratory rate
questions.explanation: These changes (increased blood pressure, decreased pulse and respiratory rate) are characteristic of patients with increased intercranial pressure.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The T wave on an ECG tracing represents which of the following events?
questions.explanation: The T wave on the ECG reflects the repolarization of the ventricles.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You are analyzing a patient’s heart rate on an ECG strip. You note that there are 13 complexes within one 6-second interval. You would record the heart rate as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Calculate the rate by multiplying the number of complexes in a 6-second interval by 10. The heart rate is 130.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Normal atrial depolarization is seen on the Lead II ECG strip as which of the following waveforms?
questions.explanation: Normal atrial depolarization is represented in Lead II as a positive rounded P wave.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is an abnormal finding on an ECG strip?
questions.explanation: A normal P-R interval is 0.12–0.20 seconds; a normal QRS complex lasts 0.04–0.12 seconds.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: An EMS crew attempts to resuscitate a 50-yearold male in cardiac arrest and is not successful. The family sues the EMS organization for negligence. The crew will need to prove that its actions during the resuscitation
options: were not within their duty to act.
exceeded the standards set forth by the American Heart Association.
were similar to the actions a reasonably prudent person would do under similar circumstances.
questions.explanation: The “reasonable person” standard sets a minimum guideline for what similarly trained personnel would do under similar circumstances, in this case, a cardiac arrest. As long as the crew could demonstrate that its actions were reasonable and expected to be done by other prehospital-care providers in a similar situation, this would avoid a negligence allegation.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: How does the Valsalva maneuver improve a too-rapid heartbeat?
options: It forces the patient to slow down his or her respirations.
It stimulates the vagus nerve to slow the heart rate.
It inhibits the release of acetylcholine, slowing the heart.
It stimulates the carotid artery, slowing blood return.
questions.explanation: The Valsalva maneuver (bearing down against a closed glottis) stimulates the vagus nerve, which innervates the heart.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which dysrhythmia may be a sign of digitalis toxicity?
options: atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of less than 60
premature junctional contractions leading to tachycardia
atrial flutter with a ventricular rate greater than 120
questions.explanation: Atrial fibrillation with a low ventricular rate is suggestive of digitalis toxicity.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 67-year-old man who has a pulse and whose ECG strip shows ventricular tachycardia. What should you do after administering oxygen and placing an IV line?
options: Administer 6 mg of adenosine via rapid IV push.
Administer 2.5–5.0 mg of verapamil via IV.
questions.explanation: Lidocaine is the first-choice drug for perfusing ventricular tachycardia.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following is a risk factor for the formation of atherosclerosis?
questions.explanation: Risk factors for atherosclerosis include diabetes, advanced age, obesity, lack of exercise, hypertension, and smoking.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: The pain caused by myocardial infarction is usually relieved only by the use of which of the following medications?
questions.explanation: The pain of myocardial infarction is not relieved by nitroglycerin, acetaminophen, or oxygen; morphine is usually needed.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: During a multicasualty incident, the first two arriving paramedics should assume which roles?
options: medical group supervisor and communications officer
medical group supervisor and triage officer
questions.explanation: The medical group supervisor will need to establish overall responsibility for the medical section, interacting with other lead officers such as police, fire, and public works. The triage officer will need to establish the number of severity of victims as early as possible.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: All of the following are components of an incident command system EXCEPT
questions.explanation: C-FLOP is a mnemonic used to describe the components of an ICS system: commandfinance, logistics, operations, planning.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Why are patients who present with pulmonary edema usually assumed to have had a myocardial infarction?
options: An AMI is often the underlying cause of right heart failure.
An AMI is frequently a common cause of left ventricle failure.
An AMI decreases the oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood.
An AMI can result in the formation of a pulmonary embolism.
questions.explanation: Myocardial infarction is a common cause of left ventricular failure, which is closely associated with pulmonary edema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What is the primary goal of management for a patient with left ventricle failure and pulmonary edema?
questions.explanation: The primary goal for patients with left ventricular failure and pulmonary edema is to reduce venous return to the heart, or preload, and thus reduce pressure on the pulmonary circulation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 68-year-old male with a history of two prior AMIs. Your assessment findings include a pulse rate of 124, peripheral edema, and jugular vein distention. The patient denies any chest pain or breathing difficulty. What condition should you suspect?
questions.explanation: The patient’s history, signs, and symptoms suggest right ventricular failure.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is an 82-year-old female with a suspected MI. While en route to the hospital, you note that her systolic blood pressure, which had been stable, has started to drop, and that she is becoming confused. At the same time, her heart rhythm converts to sinus tachycardia. What should you should suspect is happening?
options: She is going into sudden cardiac arrest.
Pulmonary edema is causing heart failure.
She has thrown a pulmonary embolus.
questions.explanation: Signs of cardiogenic shock include a sudden drop in systolic blood pressure and increasing confusion.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 65-year-old male who is complaining of pain in his abdomen, back, and flanks. His blood pressure is 90/60. On examination, you note that the femoral pulses are markedly weaker than the radial pulses. What should you do next?
options: Palpate the abdomen gently for a pulsatile mass.
Administer dopamine to increase cardiac output.
Treat for hypovolemia and transport rapidly.
Administer lasix, morphine, nitroglycerine, and oxygen.
questions.explanation: The patient is showing signs and symptoms of abdominal aortic aneurysm. Do not palpate the abdomen unnecessarily; treat decreased tissue perfusion and transport.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which of the following are signs and symptoms of acute pulmonary embolism?
questions.explanation: The most common signs of a pulmonary embolism (rapid labored breathing and tachycardia) are given; onset is usually -sudden, and there may or may not be chest pain.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient is a 67-year-old female who complains of increasing leg pain and tenderness. The skin over the affected area is warm and red, and Homan’s sign is positive. Vital signs are unremarkable. How should you treat this patient?
options: Massage the affected area to relieve the pain and restore circulation.
Elevate the leg and transport the patient for further evaluation.
Give IV fluid boluses to treat the signs and symptoms of shock.
Have the patient walk to the ambulance to promote blood flow.
questions.explanation: The clinical picture suggests deep venous thrombosis. The correct action is to elevate the leg and transport the patient for further evaluation. Do not massage the area or allow the patient to walk, since pulmonary emboli may be provoked.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: In general, the court deems an emancipated minor to be one who
questions.explanation: Each of the answers defines an emancipated minor.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the initial energy setting for defibrillation in an adult cardiac arrest patient?
questions.explanation: Initial attempts in an adult patient typically use 200 joules; this is gradually increased to 360 joules.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Carotid sinus massage is used for patients with which dysrhythmia?
questions.explanation: Carotid sinus massage can convert PSVTs by increasing vagal tone and decreasing heart rate.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: External cardiac pacing is used for which of the following rhythms?
questions.explanation: External cardiac pacing is used to correct symptomatic bradycardias by directing the heart’s electrical system.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the usual dosage and route of administration of diazepam given before synchronized cardioversion?
questions.explanation: Five to 15 mg by slow IV push is the correct dosage and route when diazepam is used to relax the patient and cause amnesia before cardioversion.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which drug is used in management of congestive heart failure?
questions.explanation: Dobutamine is used in patients with congestive heart failure to increase stroke volume and in that way increase cardiac output.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which heart sounds are normal findings on auscultation?
questions.explanation: S1 and S2 are normal sounds; extra sounds are abnormal findings.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which set of vital signs is suggestive of left ventricular heart failure with pulmonary edema?
options: BP elevated, pulse slow and irregular, respirations slow and labored
BP diminished, pulse slow and irregular, respirations rapid but easy
BP diminished, pulse fast and regular, respirations rapid and labored
BP elevated, pulse fast and irregular, respirations rapid and labored
questions.explanation: These vital signs (BP elevated, pulse fast and irregular, respirations rapid and labored) are consistent with left ventricular failure with pulmonary edema.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond for a 44-year-old male diabetic who is complaining of a general feeling of weakness. During your questioning, you learn that he has been “constantly thirsty and hungry.” His breath has a fruity odor, and his level of consciousness appears to be diminishing. Which diabetic emergency is this patient most likely suffering from?
questions.explanation: These are symptoms of diabetic ketoacidosis.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond for a 44-year-old male diabetic who is complaining of a general feeling of weakness. During your questioning, you learn that he has been “constantly thirsty and hungry.” His breath has a fruity odor, and his level of consciousness appears to be diminishing. Which vital signs would you expect from this patient?
options: cool, clammy skin; bradycardia; increased respirations
warm, dry skin; tachycardia; increased respirations
warm, dry skin; bradycardia; decreased respirations
cool, clammy skin; tachycardia; decreased respirations
questions.explanation: Warm, dry skin; tachycardia; and increased respirations are common vitals seen in ketoacidotic patients.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond for a 44-year-old male diabetic who is complaining of a general feeling of weakness. During your questioning, you learn that he has been “constantly thirsty and hungry.” His breath has a fruity odor, and his level of consciousness appears to be diminishing. This patient’s symptoms are most likely due to which condition?
questions.explanation: Ketoacidotic patients have low levels of insulin.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You respond for a 44-year-old male diabetic who is complaining of a general feeling of weakness. During your questioning, you learn that he has been “constantly thirsty and hungry.” His breath has a fruity odor, and his level of consciousness appears to be diminishing. Which of the following statements is the most accurate with regard to this patient?
options: He has not eaten in a while, resulting in hypoglycemia.
He has taken too much insulin, resulting in hyperglycemia.
He has not taken his correct dose of insulin or is ill.
He has taken his insulin but did not regulate his fluid intake.
questions.explanation: Most likely, this patient has not taken his insulin or has an infection that upset the glucose-insulin balance.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You respond for a 44-year-old male diabetic who is complaining of a general feeling of weakness. During your questioning, you learn that he has been “constantly thirsty and hungry.” His breath has a fruity odor, and his level of consciousness appears to be diminishing. During transport, this patient slips into unconsciousness, and his breathing becomes very deep and rapid. What is this pattern called?
questions.explanation: Kussmaul’s respirations are very deep and rapid and represent the body’s attempt to compensate for the metabolic acidosis produced by the ketones and organic acids in the blood.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond for a 44-year-old male diabetic who is complaining of a general feeling of weakness. During your questioning, you learn that he has been “constantly thirsty and hungry.” His breath has a fruity odor, and his level of consciousness appears to be diminishing. What does appropriate treatment for this patient include?
options: an IV of D5W or Ringer’s lactate, oxygen, and IM glucagon
a blood glucose test, IV normal saline, and a fluid bolus
a blood glucose test, 25 gm of dextrose 50%, and limited fluids
hyperventilation, limited fluids, and sodium bicarbonate
questions.explanation: The appropriate treatment for this patient would be a blood glucose test; an IV of isotonic crystalloid solution like normal saline, Ringer’s lactate, or D5W; and an initial fluid bolus of 500 ml.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: After a critical incident, a “defusing” should be done
questions.explanation: A defusing is different from a debriefing in that they are held 8–10 hours after the event, rather than 24–48 hours. Defusings are more informal than a debriefing as well.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Asymmetrical movement during respiration typically suggests which condition?
questions.explanation: Asymmetrical movements during respiration suggest injury to the chest wall.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Your patient suffers a head trauma that results in a transient loss of consciousness followed by a complete return of function. What is the term for this condition?
questions.explanation: A concussion is a brief loss of consciousness in response to head trauma.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the proper procedure for aligning a fractured long bone?
options: Stabilize the entire limb in the position in which it is found, and then immobilize.
Immobilize the limb with all the joints in the position of function.
Immobilize the distal portion of the limb, and then move the proximal portion.
Stabilize the proximal portion, and then bring the distal portion into alignment.
questions.explanation: Use gentle traction to bring the distal part of the limb into alignment with the proximal part.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When would you bandage and splint limb injuries on scene?
options: only if the patient does not need rapid -transport
if the transport time will be longer than one hour
after treating any life-threatening injuries first
questions.explanation: If the patient’s condition is such that rapid transport is necessary, you would care for limb injuries while en route, rather than completing a detailed physical exam and bandaging and splinting on scene.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What should you always do if your examination of a limb suggests that it is fractured?
options: Apply traction to align the ends of long bones and position the joints.
Treat it as if a fracture exists and immobilize it to prevent further injury.
Check the proximal pulse, motor, and sensation, and adjust the position.
Transport the patient as quickly as possible to preserve function of the limb.
questions.explanation: You cannot harm a patient by immobilizing a limb properly, but you may possibly cause further injury by failing to immobilize a fracture. Always assess the distal pulse before and after splinting.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which type of wound would most likely require a tourniquet?
options: amputation of the hand at the forearm
bilateral open fractures of the femurs
tearing injury of the upper arm
questions.explanation: Because a tearing wound can tear multiple large blood vessels, bleeding may be particularly difficult to control, and a tourniquet may be necessary. Clean amputations often do not require a tourniquet.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A patient with hypoglycemia may present with which of the following signs or symptoms?
questions.explanation: Changes in behavior are the most common signs of hypoglycemia. Other signs and symptoms include diaphoresis, tachycardia, and -headache.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: What is the primary reason that diazepam is given to a seizure patient?
options: to help relieve any anxiety that may be caused from having a seizure
to suppress the spread of electrical activity in the brain and relax muscles
to prevent hypoglycemia by allowing the brain to effectively use insulin
to help increase the blood pressure by lowering the seizure threshold
questions.explanation: Although diazepam (Valium) does reduce anxiety, it is given to seizure patients to suppress the spread of electrical activity through the brain, as well as to relax the muscles.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is the correct dosage and route of administration of epinephrine for a patient in -anaphylaxis?
questions.explanation: The correct dosage and route of administration (0.3–0.5 mg epinephrine 1:10,000, -administered intravenously) is given.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: What is the role of beta agonists like albuterol in the treatment of anaphylaxis?
questions.explanation: Beta agonists such as albuterol are most frequently used in the treatment of reactive airway disease (asthma). They are also used to prevent or relieve bronchospasm and laryngeal edema in patients with anaphylaxis. Beta blockers are often used to control hypertension, but beta agonists are not given to raise blood pressure, making choice c incorrect.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: During a multicasualty incident, a conscious patient presents with a fractured femur, a palpable radial pulse, and a respiratory rate of 24/ min. According to START, this patient would be placed into what triage category?
questions.explanation: According to START triage principles, transport of this patient to definitive care can be delayed. He is breathing spontaneously, has a radial pulse, and is conscious. The fractured femur is not factored into the evaluation of transport status.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What do the symptoms of acetaminophen overdose include?
options: nausea, vomiting, malaise, diaphoresis, and right-upper-quadrant pain
nausea, vomiting, confusion, lethargy, seizures, and dysrhythmias
altered mental status, hypotension, slurred speech, and bradycardia
nausea, dilated pupils, rambling speech, lethargy, headache, and dizziness
questions.explanation: The most common symptoms of acetaminophen overdose (nausea and vomiting, malaise, diaphoresis, right-upper-quadrant pain) are listed.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Prehospital administration of sodium bicarbonate may be ordered for a patient who has overdosed on which of the following drugs?
questions.explanation: Sodium bicarbonate is sometimes ordered in the field for ingestions of tricyclic antidepressants with cardiac symptoms (widecomplex tachycardias).
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 19-year-old female who has been stung by a stingray while swimming. What should you do after ensuring airway breathing and circulation are intact?
options: Apply a tight constricting band between the wound and the heart.
Apply heat or warm water to reduce pain and detoxify the poison.
Use an icepack wrapped in a towel to relieve pain and swelling.
Administer morphine sulfate IM or IM titrated to relieve pain.
questions.explanation: Heat will cause the poison to break down and lessen the harm to the patient.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which drug can cause users to behave violently and aggressively?
questions.explanation: PCP can cause bizarre delusions as well as violent and uncontrollable behavior.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following patients shows signs and symptoms of heat exhaustion?
options: male, age 34: severe muscle cramps in legs and abdomen; fatigue; and dizziness
female, age 45: rapid; shallow respirations; weak pulse; cold, clammy skin; dizziness
male, age 42: deep respirations; rapid strong pulse; dry, hot skin; loss of consciousness
female, age 70: shallow respirations; weak, rapid pulse; dilated pupils; seizures
questions.explanation: Rapid, shallow respirations, weak pulse, cold, clammy skin; and dizziness are signs and symptoms of heat exhaustion. Patients c and d show signs and symptoms of heat stroke, and patient a shows signs of heat cramps.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following activities are performed by the EMT-Paramedic in the field?
questions.explanation: All of the activities are related to the practice of the paramedic.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which of the following statements about care of a near-drowning victim is correct?
options: The Heimlich maneuver should be used to clear the airway.
Ventilation should not be provided until the patient is clear of the water.
The patient should be admitted to the hospital for observation.
Near-drowning victims seldom experience head or neck injury.
questions.explanation: Due to the chance of postevent pulmonary edema, all near-drowning victims should be admitted to the hospital for observation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which of the following are the signs and symptoms of air embolism?
options: pruritus, skin pallor and cyanosis, pitting edema in the ankles
sharp chest pain with sudden onset, dyspnea with coughing
dizziness, auditory and vestibular disturbances, headache
fatigue, pain in chest and lower abdomen, nausea, and vomiting
questions.explanation: All other options list signs and symptoms of decompression sickness; only choice b includes signs of air embolism (sharp chest pain with sudden onset; dyspnea with coughing).
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You are interviewing a 43-year-old woman with a long history of schizophrenia. She appears to try to cooperate, but there are long periods of silence in your conversation while she listens to her “voices.” How should you respond during her silence?
questions.explanation: During silences, remain relaxed and attentive and wait to hear what the patient has to say. This will encourage the patient to talk.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A patient with bipolar disorder usually suffers from which of the following?
questions.explanation: A patient with bipolar disorder (manicdepressive disorder) suffers from wide mood swings from euporhia to debilitating depression.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: You respond to a 12-year-old male  who is wheezing and having difficulty breathing. The patient has a long history of asthma and states that he used his inhaler but that it didn’t help much. Upon examination, you discover that the patient is tachycardic and tachypneic with a nonproductive cough. What is the primary goal in treating this patient?
options: correct hypoxia, reverse bronchospasms, and decrease inflammation
decrease respiratory drive, decrease heart rate, and increase blood pressure
increase heart rate, decrease respiratory rate, and correct carbon dioxide levels
correct carbon dioxide levels, decrease inflammation, and increase heart rate
questions.explanation: The goal with this asthma patient would be to correct hypoxia, reverse bronchospasms, and decrease inflammation.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to a 12-year-old male  who is wheezing and having difficulty breathing. The patient has a long history of asthma and states that he used his inhaler but that it didn’t help much. Upon examination, you discover that the patient is tachycardic and tachypneic with a nonproductive cough. This patient’s condition might have been triggered by any of the following except
questions.explanation: Cold air, not warm air, is a common trigger for asthma. Choices b, c, and d are also triggers.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to a 12-year-old male  who is wheezing and having difficulty breathing. The patient has a long history of asthma and states that he used his inhaler but that it didn’t help much. Upon examination, you discover that the patient is tachycardic and tachypneic with a nonproductive cough. Treatment for this patient includes which of the following medications?
questions.explanation: Albuterol, terbutaline, and/or steroids are commonly used in the treatment of asthma.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Diazapam and lorazepam are examples of which of the following?
questions.explanation: Diazepam (Valium) and lorazepam (Ativan) are benzodiazepines, which have sedative effects and are prescribed for patients who suffer from acute anxiety attacks.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Starting an IV on an alert patient who refuses one could result in your being held liable for which of the following?
questions.explanation: Battery is the unlawful touching of an individual without consent. Assault is the threat of bodily harm. Libel is saying something that is not true.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: A 5-year-old male has multiple injuries in various stages of healing, including raccoon eyes and a new suspected broken leg. On questioning, his mother states that he fell out of his bunk bed, but his sister, age 9, says that “Daddy beat him.” The mother insists that her husband will take the child to the hospital when he gets home from work. How should you proceed?
options: Document your findings, convince the mother that transport to the hospital is necessary, and report your suspicions of child abuse.
Make sure his condition is stable, grant the mother’s request for delayed transport, and follow up that evening to confirm that treatment was sought.
Confront the mother with your suspicions, transport the child immediately and call the police.
Remove both children from the home immediately and transport both of them to the hospital for a complete evaluation.
questions.explanation: In cases of suspected child abuse, your responsibility is to ensure that the patient receives -necessary care immediately and report your -findings.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Until ruled out by a physician, documented fever in an infant younger than three months old is always considered to result from which of the following?
questions.explanation: Fever in a child younger than three months old is considered to be meningitis unless proven otherwise; transport all infants with fever promptly.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What does appropriate management of a child with epiglottitis consist of?
options: visualization of the airway and insertion of an endotracheal tube
administration of racemic epinephrine and nebulized albuterol
airway maintenance and administration of humidified oxygen
questions.explanation: Management of epiglottitis consists of airway maintenance, oxygen, and prompt transport.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to the residence of a 4-year-old male who was found in his backyard, head down in a 5-gallon bucket of water. The child, according to the mother, was in the water for approximately four minutes. The child is cyanotic, pulseless, and apneic. The ECG shows he is in asystole. The child weighs approximately 40 pounds. A physician at the scene of an incident instructs you to provide care for a patient that you know to be inappropriate given the patient’s present condition. Your best course of action will be to
options: contact the medical control physician and ask him to speak with the on-scene physician.
inform the on-scene physician that you disagree with his instructions and ask him to perform the procedures himself.
politely inform the physician that his instructions are inappropriate and ask him to leave the room.
ignore the on-scene physician’s order, load the patient in the ambulance as quickly as possible, and transport rapidly to the hospital.
questions.explanation: The medical control physician should be able to engage with the on-scene physician in discussion with the patient’s condition, the level of care that will be provided by the EMS personnel, and the transportation destination decision.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to the residence of a 4-year-old male who was found in his backyard, head down in a 5-gallon bucket of water. The child, according to the mother, was in the water for approximately four minutes. The child is cyanotic, pulseless, and apneic. The ECG shows he is in asystole. The child weighs approximately 40 pounds. Which of the following would be the most -reliable method for determining the proper medication dosing for this pediatric patient?
options: Give no more than 1−2 the adult dose for all medications.
Give 1−3 t he adult dose for all medications except atropine.
Use the Broselow Tape or another lengthweight measuring system.
Estimate the patient’s kilogram weight by dividing by 2.2.
questions.explanation: Using the Broselow Tape or another length-weight measuring system is the most appropriate way to determine proper medication for a pediatric patient.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to the residence of a 4-year-old male who was found in his backyard, head down in a 5-gallon bucket of water. The child, according to the mother, was in the water for approximately four minutes. The child is cyanotic, pulseless, and apneic. The ECG shows he is in asystole. The child weighs approximately 40 pounds. All of the following medications can be given through the endotracheal tube to pediatric patients except
questions.explanation: Valium is usually given orally or rectally.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to the residence of a 4-year-old male who was found in his backyard, head down in a 5-gallon bucket of water. The child, according to the mother, was in the water for approximately four minutes. The child is cyanotic, pulseless, and apneic. The ECG shows he is in asystole. The child weighs approximately 40 pounds. After endotracheal intubation of this patient, which of the following steps should be done first?
options: Inflate the cuff to prevent air leakage.
Dilute all ETT medication to 10 cc.
Tape the tube to the patient’s maxilla.
questions.explanation: Your first step following placement of the ET tube is to confirm its placement in the trachea. After confirmation of placement, secure the tube with tape or using a commercial device to help prevent dislodging of the tube. Pay especially close attention to these tubes as they are easily displaced, even when secured. Depending upon which size tube you use, it may or may not be a cuffed tube. Medication administration will -follow after confirmation of placement and securing the tube.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: You respond to the residence of a 4-year-old male who was found in his backyard, head down in a 5-gallon bucket of water. The child, according to the mother, was in the water for approximately four minutes. The child is cyanotic, pulseless, and apneic. The ECG shows he is in asystole. The child weighs approximately 40 pounds. After the first round of ACLS drugs, the patient converts to a sinus bradycardia at a rate of 40 beats per minute. What is the drug of choice for managing this patient’s bradycardia?
questions.explanation: Epinephrine is indicated for pediatric bradycardia in this situation.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Knee injuries and hip dislocations that occur during a motor vehicle crash are often the result of which pathway of energy transfer?
questions.explanation: During a frontal crash, the “down and under” pathway causes the knees to strike the lower dashboard violently, causing the described injury pattern.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A high-velocity bullet passes through the body. Besides the direct damage caused by the bullet, what other related condition could cause harm to the patient?
options: the type of metal used to make the bullet
the internal opening created by cavitation
questions.explanation: Cavitation caused by the pressure wave of the bullet’s forces creates both temporary and permanent openings within the tissues. Tremendous forces are transferred from the velocity of the bullet to the tissues, causing damage.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Your patient is a 5-year-old girl who presents with breathing difficulty of rapid onset. She is sitting upright and drooling. Her temperature is 104.6° F. What should you suspect?
questions.explanation: The signs and symptoms of epiglottitis are given.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: An injury to the brain opposite the site of a blunt force impact is called
questions.explanation: During significant force to the head, the brain can shift and strike the opposite side of the skull away from the original force. This mechanism can cause a contracoup injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: One way to determine the size of the endotracheal tube to use in a child is to use the -following equation: 16 + the child’s age divided by 4. Another consideration is the size of the child’s
questions.explanation: In addition to the child’s age, the size of the endotracheal tube should be based on the size of the cricoid ring, which is the narrowest part of a child’s airway.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: What should you do in order to preserve physical evidence in cases of suspected sexual assault?
options: Examine the patient’s perineal area carefully.
Bag all soiled articles of clothing together.
Have the patient change clothes and bathe before transport.
questions.explanation: To preserve physical evidence, avoid handling clothing and cleaning or bandaging wounds, unless there is hemorrhage. Do not allow the -victim to bathe, comb her hair, or change her clothing. Do not subject the patient to any unnecessary physical examinations.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 17-year-old male complains of a steadily worsening headache several days after being struck in the head during football practice. Which of the following injuries best describes this patient’s presentation?
questions.explanation: A subdural bleed occurs when a small vein bleeds into the area below the dura mater. It may take some time before pressure from the bleeding is high enough to cause clinical signs and symptoms.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When should you examine a woman who is about to give birth for crowning?
questions.explanation: Look for crowning, or the appearance of the baby’s head at the opening of the vagina, only during a contraction.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your patient is a 32-year-old woman who reports that she is 14 weeks pregnant. She complains of abdominal cramping and vaginal bleeding. How should you proceed?
options: Perform an internal vaginal exam and treat her for any signs of ectopic pregnancy.
Administer high-flow oxygen, monitor fetal heart tones, and transport immediately.
Treat for signs and symptoms of hypovolemia, provide emotional support, and transport.
Position the patient supine, open an OB kit, and prepare for emergency delivery in the field.
questions.explanation: The patient is most likely suffering a miscarriage. Treat her for signs and symptoms of shock due to blood loss, provide emotional support, retain any clumps of tissue she passes, and transport.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the cause of the supine-hypotensive syndrome in the pregnant patient?
options: pressure of the uterus on the inferior vena cava
questions.explanation: Supine-hypotensive syndrome, a normal occurrence during late pregnancy, is caused by the pressure of the pregnant uterus on the inferior vena cava when the patient is supine.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following is a differential sign or symptom of spinal shock associated with trauma?
options: warm, dry skin distal to the injury site
questions.explanation: While the other answers are signs and symptoms of shock, only choice d reflects a specific sign that points directly to the underlying cause of hypoperfusion.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: During a normal delivery, you would tell the mother to stop pushing when what occurs?
options: delivery of the infant is complete
questions.explanation: To avoid a precipitous delivery, tell the mother to stop pushing after the head is delivered.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the appropriate treatment for a prolapsed cord?
options: Attempt to push the cord back into the vagina and deliver the infant normally.
Place two fingers on either side of the baby’s nose and mouth and lift the head until it is delivered.
Place two fingers to raise the presenting part of the fetus off the cord, place the mother in knee-chest position, administer oxygen, and transport.
Assist and support the mother for a normal vaginal delivery.
questions.explanation: A prolapsed cord should be treated by placing two fingers of a gloved hand to raise the -presenting part of the fetus off the cord; then place the mother in Trendelenburg or kneechest position, administer high-flow oxygen, and transport immediately.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Immediately after delivery, how should you position the neonate?
options: higher than the mother’s vagina, with its head slightly elevated
at the level of the mother’s vagina, with its head slightly lower than the body
lower than the mother’s vagina, with the head at the same level as the body
at the level of the mother’s vagina, with the head slightly elevated
questions.explanation: Position the neonate at the level of the mother’s vagina, with his or her head slightly lower than the body to facilitate drainage of secretions.
questions.subject: Obstetrics and Pediatrics

questions.text: A patient who complains of a sudden and painless complete loss of vision in one eye is most likely suffering from
questions.explanation: The retina captures the image that is projected by the lens of the eye. It can spontaneously separate from the inner surface of the eye, causing sudden and painless loss of vision.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: The first step in the resuscitation of a distressed neonate is to
options: evaluate the respiratory rate and breath sounds.
questions.explanation: Ventilate with 100% oxygen, and then evaluate the heart rate and initiate chest compressions if necessary.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: All but which of the following statements about supplying supplemental oxygen to a neonate is correct?
options: Do not withhold oxygen from a neonate in the prehospital setting.
Oxygen toxicity in the prehospital setting is a serious concern.
Administer supplemental oxygen by blowing it across the neonate’s face.
Oxygen should be warmed if possible prior to administration.
questions.explanation: Oxygen toxicity occurs only if oxygen is administered for several days; do not withhold oxygen in the prehospital setting.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: How much intravenous fluid should you administer to a distressed neonate who weighs 2 kg?
questions.explanation: Fluid therapy for a neonate consists of 10 mL/kg (= 20 mL for a 2-kg neonate), administered via syringe over a 5to 10-minute period.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient was hit several times in the left chest with the large end of a pool cue. The patient is in severe respiratory distress with tachycardia and tachypnea. Crepitus can be felt over the left anterior fourth, fifth, sixth, and seventh rib area. Lung sounds are clear and equal, but diminished. Which of the following conditions best describes the patient’s presentation?
questions.explanation: Flail segment is very possible in this case, due to the mechanism of injury. The lack of other signs or symptoms such as jugular venous distension or unequal or absent breath sounds minimizes the possibilities of a pneumothorax or tamponade.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which statement describes a tiered EMS system?
options: Paramedics are the prehospital team leaders and they supervise lower-level BLS providers.
Physicians directly supervise all aspects of care provided by prehospital personnel.
Varying levels of resources are dispatched to calls, depending on the nature of the incident.
After they arrive at the hospital, patients are assigned a priority for care by a triage nurse.
questions.explanation: A tiered EMS system is one in which varying levels of resources are dispatched to calls depending upon the nature of the incident as determined by the 911 dispatcher.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: A patient is found lying supine on the floor with a stab wound to her right anterior chest, just below the breast. The patient is having difficulty breathing, with cool, clammy skin signs. No JVD is noted. Breath sounds are absent over the right side. This patient most likely is experiencing a
questions.explanation: The lack of jugular venous distension in the supine position is very telling; it suggests a large loss of volume from the circulatory system.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which activity is an example of indirect medical control?
options: A licensed physician who does not work in the EMS system assumes control at an accident scene.
A paramedic administers nitroglycerin to a patient with chest pain under standing medical orders.
A mobile intensive care nurse communicates orders to paramedics while en route to the hospital.
An EMS physician tells a paramedic on the radio to administer morphine sulfate to a patient.
questions.explanation: Indirect (or offline) medical control is any EMS activity that involves medical (physician) input either before or after (but not during) the care of the patient (e.g., standing orders, run critiques, quality improvement).
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You respond to reports of a bus collision. Upon arrival, it appears that you have approximately 35 patients. What is your first priority?
options: Set up the triage area close to the treament and transport areas.
Establish the morgue area away from the view of the patients.
Begin rapid treatment of the most seriously injured patients.
Separate the walking wounded from the more severely injured.
questions.explanation: Using START triage, separating out the walking wounded from the more severely injured patients is the first step in triaging large numbers of patients.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You respond to reports of a bus collision. Upon arrival, it appears that you have approximately 35 patients. The first parameter to assess when using the START triage algorithm should be
questions.explanation: Breathing/airway is the first parameter that should be assessed, followed by pulse and mental status.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to reports of a bus collision. Upon arrival, it appears that you have approximately 35 patients. A male patient is found to have a respiratory rate of 38. This would place him in which of the following categories?
questions.explanation: The START triage acronym dictates that patients with respiratory rates above 30 be classified as immediate.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You respond to reports of a bus collision. Upon arrival, it appears that you have approximately 35 patients. Another male patient is found to have a respiratory rate of 28 and a radial pulse of 84. He is confused about the incident. This patient would be placed in which of the following categories?
questions.explanation: The decreased level of consciousness places the patient in the immediate category. Once he is moved to the treatment area, he may be monitored for a while or transported quickly.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond to reports of a bus collision. Upon arrival, it appears that you have approximately 35 patients. A female patient is found to have no spontaneous respirations. What should you do next?
options: Use a bag-valve mask and ventilate with 100% oxygen.
Reposition the airway and check her again for respirations.
Start CPR and call for immediate removal to the treatment area.
questions.explanation: Reposition the head and examine for spontaneous respirations. If they are immediately present, categorize the patient as immediate. If they are absent, categorize the patient as dead/unsalvageable and move on to the next patient.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: A person with a serious illness can delegate the right to make medical decisions to someone else by enacting which of the following legal documents?
options: durable power of attorney for healthcare
questions.explanation: A durable power of attorney for healthcare delegates the right to make medical decisions to someone else in the event that the patient becomes disabled or incompetent. Choices a, b, and c are all examples of advanceddirectives, stating one’s will regarding medical interventions.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: What is the legal term for an intentional deviation from the accepted standard of care that results in harm to a patient?
questions.explanation: Negligence refers to an intentional deviation from the accepted standard of care that results in harm to a patient.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You are acting under the doctrine of implied consent when you treat which of the following patients?
options: a person who is drunk and refuses treatment
a confused elderly man whose adult child is with him
a small child whose parent is not present
a person who is upset and mentally distraught
questions.explanation: Implied consent covers situations in which the patient is not capable of consenting to treatment but a reasonable person would do so.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Your patient is a 28-year-old man who is suffering a seizure. You monitor his condition during the seizure and throughout the postictal period, but when the patient recovers, he refuses transport or further treatment. What should you do next?
options: Explain the risks of refusing care and then restrain the patient before you begin treatment.
Call for police assistance to help you convince the patient to go to the hospital.
Sedate the patient and transport him to the hospital while he is unconscious.
Consult with medical control, explain the risks of refusing care, and document the patient’s refusal.
questions.explanation: If a competent adult patient refuses treatment and transport, you should consult with medical control, explain to the patient the risks of refusal in detail, document the informed refusal, and, if possible, have the refusal witnessed by someone who is not an EMS employee.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: A wrestler feels his shoulder “pop” when his opponent twists his arm during a hold. There is immediate pain and loss of range of motion in the shoulder. What is the patient’s likely injury?
questions.explanation: A subluxation of the shoulder is a partial dislocation of the joint, due to injuries sustained by the rotator cuff during a severe twisting force to the joint.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: All of the following are correct statements about the care of patients who are contaminated by hazardous material EXCEPT
options: trained personnel should immediately remove nonambulatory patients from the “hot zone.”
decontamination activities should be carried out while the patient is in the “warm zone.”
intravenous therapy and invasive procedures should begin only under specific physician direction.
An initial assessment should be done while the patient is located within the “hot zone.”
questions.explanation: Only absolutely essential care, such as ABCs and spinal immobilization, should be done in the “hot zone.”
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Your unit is the first to arrive at the scene of a bombing at a large office building. The purpose of your initial size-up of the incident is to
options: determine what additional resources will be needed.
decide how seriously injured each patient is.
assess whether the building can be salvaged.
help the police look for clues about who placed the bomb.
questions.explanation: The purpose of the initial scene size-up at a mass-casualty incident is to determine what addi-tional resources will be needed.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: At a major incident response, which of the -following is the responsibility of the staging officer?
options: Establish the triage, treatment, and transportation sectors in the most appropriate sites.
Evaluate what resources are needed for treatment and report this to incident command.
Collect and assess patients with minor injuries and remove them from the treatment areas.
Maintain a log of units available and an inventory of special equipment.
questions.explanation: The staging officer is primarily responsible for assembling all available vehicles to make sure they are ready for deployment as needed.
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: Which statement about the triage operation at a mass-casualty incident is correct?
options: Each patient’s triage and initial assessment should take less than 60 seconds.
A detailed physical exam of the walking wounded is part of the basic triage -operation.
Triage assessment of each individual patient takes approximately one to two minutes.
Triage personnel at a mass-casualty incident should have the highest level of training.
questions.explanation: START and METTAG, systems used at mass-casualty incidents, are designed to be carried out very quickly by minimally trained personnel.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: Using the START triage method at a multiplecasualty accident scene, you encounter a patient who is making no spontaneous respiratory effort despite attempts at repositioning. What should you do next?
options: Tag the patient as unsalvageable and move on.
Request medical backup from the treatment area.
Begin mouth to mask or bag-valve mask ventilation.
Move the patient to the care area and -intubate.
questions.explanation: If a patient continues to remain apneic following a second attempt to open the airway, he or she is considered unsalvageable.
questions.subject: Operations

questions.text: You have responded to a home for an unknown emergency. Your patient, a 74-year-old male named George Evans, has been found at home in rigor mortis with dependent lividity. The best thing to tell his family initially is
options: “Mr. Evans is no longer with us.”
“I’m sorry to tell you that Mr. Evans has died.”
“It’s obvious that Mr. Evans is long gone.”
“I think it’s possible that Mr. Evans passed in his sleep.”
questions.explanation: Inform the family that the patient has died in plain, simple language. This avoids any misunderstanding and prevents any false hopes that may arise from a misunderstanding. Always deliver this news with compassion and respect.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: All but which of the following statements about use of disposable gloves and infection control are true?
options: Gloves should be worn for every patient -contact.
Gloves should be changed for each new patient contact.
Gloves cannot protect healthcare workers from needle sticks.
Gloves should be worn while eating or drinking in the ambulance.
questions.explanation: Gloves cannot protect you from foodor -airborne-infectious agents (and personnel should not eat or drink inside the ambulance).
questions.subject: Medical

questions.text: A 72-year-old man trips and falls while walking to his bathroom. Physical findings include a lateral rotation of the left foot and knee. The patient has tenderness and a protrusion in the left groin area. You suspect
questions.explanation: The outward rotation of the left extremity along with the deformity in the groin area points to an anterior dislocation injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When is use of “reasonable” force or use of restraints permissible for a patient?
options: The patient is alert, cooperative but anxious.
The patient has altered level of conscious caused by injury, substance abuse, or illness.
It is never appropriate to use any force on a patient.
This is at the discretion of the paramedic or the crew’s officer.
questions.explanation: If the patient is altered and is placing himself or others in harm’s way, the EMS provider should use any method within reason to control the patient’s actions.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Tertiary injuries from a blast include
questions.explanation: Tertiary blast injuries are caused by the victim being propelled away from the blast and into objects on the ground. Injuries from this are similar to those sustained from ejection from an automobile. Primary blast injuries result from compression of hollow organs such as the lungs. Secondary blast injuries are caused by flying debris propelled by the force of the blast.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond code 3 to a “shortness of breath” call. Upon arrival, you find the first responders placing a 70-year-old male patient on a nonrebreather mask, running the oxygen flowmeter at 15 lpm. Family members state that the patient came back from the store approximately 45 minutes ago, complaining of moderate respiratory distress. He has a history of “some sort of lung disease,” for which he uses an inhaler. The family does not know what type of medication the patient is taking. There is no history of fever or recent illness. You observe that the patient is cyanotic. The oxygen reservoir on the mask does not appear to collapse, even though the liter flow is set correctly. Although he is sitting on the edge of the bed, his eyes are closed and he does not respond to verbal commands. There is accessory muscle use evident, with intercostal muscle retractions. His breath sounds are diminished in all fields and absent in both bases. Faint expiratory wheezing is auscultated at only the apices. His heart rate is 140, respiratory rate 42, and BP 106/72. The pulse oximeter registers an SpO2 of 75%. The rest of his physical exam is unremarkable. Based on the information given, what might be a suspected assessment?
questions.explanation: The timing of the onset, medical history, and physical findings point to emphysema as the primary suspect.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond code 3 to a “shortness of breath” call. Upon arrival, you find the first responders placing a 70-year-old male patient on a nonrebreather mask, running the oxygen flowmeter at 15 lpm. Family members state that the patient came back from the store approximately 45 minutes ago, complaining of moderate respiratory distress. He has a history of “some sort of lung disease,” for which he uses an inhaler. The family does not know what type of medication the patient is taking. There is no history of fever or recent illness. You observe that the patient is cyanotic. The oxygen reservoir on the mask does not appear to collapse, even though the liter flow is set correctly. Although he is sitting on the edge of the bed, his eyes are closed and he does not respond to verbal commands. There is accessory muscle use evident, with intercostal muscle retractions. His breath sounds are diminished in all fields and absent in both bases. Faint expiratory wheezing is auscultated at only the apices. His heart rate is 140, respiratory rate 42, and BP 106/72. The pulse oximeter registers an SpO2 of 75%. The rest of his physical exam is unremarkable. Of the following options, what would be your initial priority in managing this patient’s condition?
options: Get additional medical history from the family.
Instruct first responders to reduce the liter flow from 15 to 10 lpm to allow the reservoir to collapse.
Instruct first responders to assist the patient’s ventilations with a BVM and 100% oxygen.
Begin an IV of normal saline.
questions.explanation: This patient has begun to decompensate in terms of his ability to ventilate adequately, as evidenced by the decreased mental status and very poor skin signs. This patient will require aggressive airway management in order to be ventilated.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond code 3 to a “shortness of breath” call. Upon arrival, you find the first responders placing a 70-year-old male patient on a nonrebreather mask, running the oxygen flowmeter at 15 lpm. Family members state that the patient came back from the store approximately 45 minutes ago, complaining of moderate respiratory distress. He has a history of “some sort of lung disease,” for which he uses an inhaler. The family does not know what type of medication the patient is taking. There is no history of fever or recent illness. You observe that the patient is cyanotic. The oxygen reservoir on the mask does not appear to collapse, even though the liter flow is set correctly. Although he is sitting on the edge of the bed, his eyes are closed and he does not respond to verbal commands. There is accessory muscle use evident, with intercostal muscle retractions. His breath sounds are diminished in all fields and absent in both bases. Faint expiratory wheezing is auscultated at only the apices. His heart rate is 140, respiratory rate 42, and BP 106/72. The pulse oximeter registers an SpO2 of 75%. The rest of his physical exam is unremarkable. What airway adjunct(s) might you consider first for this patient?
questions.explanation: The most immediate piece of ventilation equipment to use would be a CPAP. CPAP eliminates the need for intubation. If CPAP is not available, a BVM would be the next choice. The patient will need preoxygenation before any attempt of intubation. In addition, as implied by the patient’s presenting position, he will likely have an intact gag reflex, making insertion of an OPA difficult, if not impossible.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: You respond code 3 to a “shortness of breath” call. Upon arrival, you find the first responders placing a 70-year-old male patient on a nonrebreather mask, running the oxygen flowmeter at 15 lpm. Family members state that the patient came back from the store approximately 45 minutes ago, complaining of moderate respiratory distress. He has a history of “some sort of lung disease,” for which he uses an inhaler. The family does not know what type of medication the patient is taking. There is no history of fever or recent illness. You observe that the patient is cyanotic. The oxygen reservoir on the mask does not appear to collapse, even though the liter flow is set correctly. Although he is sitting on the edge of the bed, his eyes are closed and he does not respond to verbal commands. There is accessory muscle use evident, with intercostal muscle retractions. His breath sounds are diminished in all fields and absent in both bases. Faint expiratory wheezing is auscultated at only the apices. His heart rate is 140, respiratory rate 42, and BP 106/72. The pulse oximeter registers an SpO2 of 75%. The rest of his physical exam is unremarkable. Which of the following medications may be used in the management of this patient’s condition?
questions.explanation: A beta agonist like albuterol is indicated in this situation. Morphine sulfate may worsen the patient’s condition by potentially depressing respiratory drive. Adenosine is a cardiac medication used to control supraventricular tachycardia. Epinephrine has significant cardiac side effects, especially in older patients.
questions.subject: Airway and Breathing

questions.text: Which patient’s vital signs are considered normal for the age and sex described?
questions.explanation: Only this patient’s vital signs are within -normal limits for her age. Patient a has increased respirations and the systolic blood pressure is within the hypertensive range. Patient c has bradycardia (he is 14) and is at the lower end of normal for -respirations and blood pressure. Patient d is dangerously hypertensive and the heart rate and -respiratory rate are above normal limits for that age range.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: When using the OPQRST mnemonic to assess a patient’s pain, you would assess the R portion of the mnemonic by asking
options: “Does the pain feel sharp or dull?”
questions.explanation: In the OPQRST mnemonic, R stands for -radiation. You should determine if the pain is radiating, referred, or causing any associated problems.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.text: Which set of signs and symptoms is characteristic of a patient in compensated shock?
options: lethargy; confusion; pulse and blood pressure normal to slightly elevated; skin cool; and capillary refill delayed
coma; pulse and blood pressure very low; skin pale, cold, and clammy with delayed capillary refill time
